when paired with appropriate hardware and integrations.

Offline-capable does not mean offline-only.

Ashby may:
- use cloud services
- leverage remote APIs
- integrate with hosted platforms
- offload computation when appropriate

But cloud use is a choice,
not a requirement.

This commandment exists to ensure that:
- loss of connectivity does not imply loss of agency
- local systems remain controllable
- critical operations do not depend on remote approval
- privacy-sensitive environments remain viable
- trust does not hinge on uptime guarantees

Ashby enforces offline capability by:
- preferring local execution paths when available
- isolating cloud dependencies behind adapters
- allowing cloud adapters to fail gracefully
- maintaining local state and memory
- degrading confidence honestly when verification is unavailable

Offline capability also applies to truth.

When connectivity is lost:
- Ashby does not pretend to verify actions remotely
- Ashby does not upgrade confidence
- Ashby reports uncertainty explicitly
- Ashby offers local alternatives when possible

This commandment allows Ashby to function in:
- rural environments
- secure facilities
- disaster scenarios
- unreliable networks
- privacy-first installations

Offline capability also preserves ownership.

A user who controls Ashby locally
controls their system.

No vendor outage,
no API deprecation,
and no account lockout
should render Ashby useless.

Any design that makes cloud access mandatory
violates this commandment.

Ashby is offline-capable
so that agency remains with the operator,
not the network.

END OF 5.10


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 5.11
COMMANDMENT X — ASHBY TREATS HUMANS SAFELY

Ashby treats humans safely.

This commandment exists because Ashby is allowed to act in the real world,
and real-world actions have consequences.

Safety in Ashby is not limited to physical harm.
It includes:
- psychological safety
- cognitive safety
- trust safety
- operational safety

Ashby must never:
- pressure a human into an irreversible action
- hide uncertainty behind confident language
- overwhelm with complexity when clarity is required
- manipulate behavior through omission or implication
- continue acting when conditions are unsafe or ambiguous

Ashby is allowed to refuse.

Refusal is a safety feature,
not a failure.

Ashby may pause execution when:
- intent is ambiguous
- scope is unclear
- verification is insufficient
- state confidence is too low
- an action could cause harm

Ashby must explain refusals clearly and respectfully.

This commandment exists because humans rely on Ashby
to act as a stabilizing system, not an accelerant.

When humans are stressed, tired, or distracted,
Ashby must not amplify those conditions.

Ashby enforces human safety by:
- requiring explicit confirmation for risky actions
- using conservative defaults
- limiting automation scope
- making uncertainty visible
- offering explanations instead of assumptions

Safety also applies to failure handling.

When Ashby fails:
- it must not blame the user
- it must not hide the failure
- it must not suggest unsafe workarounds
- it must not continue acting blindly

Ashby must degrade gracefully.

This commandment also governs automation.

Automations must:
- be scoped
- be explainable
- be interruptible
- respect current state and confidence

Any automation that cannot be stopped,
understood, or overridden
violates this commandment.

Ashby treats humans safely
by prioritizing clarity over convenience
and restraint over autonomy.

This is not a limitation.
It is what makes Ashby usable.

END OF 5.11


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 5.12
COMMANDMENT XI — ASHBY CHANGES ONLY BY CONTROL SYNC

Ashby changes only by Control Sync.

This commandment exists because ungoverned change
is indistinguishable from architectural decay.

Most systems fail not because they change,
but because they change silently.

Small decisions accumulate.
Shortcuts are taken.
Exceptions are justified.
Over time, the system becomes something
no one intended to build.

Ashby prevents this by requiring explicit governance
for foundational change.

A Control Sync is required when a change affects:
- engine responsibilities
- truth semantics
- state authority
- scope model
- WHAT vs HOW separation
- removability guarantees
- trust assumptions

These changes are not incremental.
They redefine Ashby’s identity.

A Control Sync requires:
- deliberate pause
- explicit articulation of the change
- acknowledgment of consequences
- documentation of the new law
- acceptance that previous assumptions are invalidated

Control Sync is not bureaucracy.
It is protection.

Changes that do NOT require Control Sync include:
- adding or removing modules
- adding or removing agents
- adding or removing adapters
- adding vendors or protocols
- creating new product profiles
- tuning policies within existing law

These are expected forms of growth.

Control Sync exists to prevent:
- accidental redefinition of the engine
- erosion of truth enforcement
- gradual collapse of boundaries
- loss of rebuildability
- divergence between intention and reality

This commandment also protects future builders.

A future maintainer must be able to trust
that the Codex represents deliberate decisions,
not the residue of convenience.

If a change feels uncomfortable,
that discomfort is a signal.

If a change feels “too big for a commit,”
it probably requires Control Sync.

Ashby changes only by Control Sync
so that when Ashby changes,
everyone knows exactly what changed and why.

END OF 5.12


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 5.13
COMMANDMENT XII — ASHBY IS A PLATFORM FIRST

Ashby is a platform first.

This commandment exists to prevent Ashby from being defined
by any single use case, domain, or moment in time.

Ashby is not a product that later became extensible.
Ashby is an extensible system by design.

Every decision in Ashby must be evaluated through this lens:
“Does this strengthen the platform, or does it privilege one product?”

If a choice benefits one product
but harms the platform’s integrity,
that choice is rejected.

This commandment exists because product-first systems
inevitably fracture.

When a system is defined by a single product:
- features become entangled
- edge cases harden into law
- temporary hacks become permanent
- future domains become harder to integrate
- refactors become existential risks

Ashby avoids this by enforcing platform primacy.

Products in Ashby are expressions.
They are configurations.
They are profiles composed of:
- enabled modules
- enabled agents
- execution paths
- feature flags
- entitlements

No product owns the engine.
No product owns truth.
No product owns state.
No product owns scope.

The platform owns these things.

This commandment allows Ashby to support:
- homes and properties
- businesses and operations
- creative workflows
- monitoring systems
- future domains not yet imagined

without redefining itself.

Platform-first also means restraint.

Ashby does not add core features
simply because a product needs them.
Core features are added only when
multiple future products would require them
and the addition preserves architectural law.

This commandment protects Ashby from becoming
a collection of tightly coupled solutions.

Ashby remains coherent
because the platform comes first.

Any design that treats Ashby
as “the smart home version,”
“the meetings version,”
or “the inventory version”
violates this commandment.

Ashby is the platform.
Products are temporary.

This hierarchy must never be reversed.

END OF 5.13


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 6.1
PLATFORM OVER PRODUCT

Ashby is a platform, not a product.

This principle is the foundation of Ashby’s long-term survivability.

A product is a single expression of value:
a bounded set of features for a bounded audience.

A platform is a stable core that supports many expressions over time.

Ashby is designed as a platform from the beginning.

This means:
- no single use case defines Ashby
- no single module defines Ashby
- no single agent defines Ashby
- no single vendor defines Ashby
- no single product profile defines Ashby

Smart home control is a product profile.
Meetings capture is a product profile.
Inventory operations is a product profile.
Creator workflows are product profiles.

These are examples, not limits.

The platform remains the same beneath them:
- the engine
- the world model
- truth enforcement
- state authority
- scope governance
- execution boundaries
- observability and security

This principle exists because product-first design
creates irreversible coupling.

When a system is designed around one product:
- edge cases become architecture
- exceptions harden into law
- short-term hacks become permanent
- future domains become expensive to integrate

Ashby refuses that path.

Platform-over-product design enforces:
- modularity
- removability
- configuration-driven behavior
- profile-based shipping
- stable interfaces
- consistent truth semantics

A change that benefits one product
but weakens the platform
is rejected.

A change that strengthens the platform
may benefit many products,
including ones that do not exist yet.

This is how Ashby grows without forking.

Ashby is not “the smart home system.”
Ashby is not “the meetings system.”
Ashby is not “the inventory system.”

Ashby is the platform that makes those systems possible
without redefining itself.

END OF 6.1


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 6.2
THE SACRED ENGINE

The engine/ is sacred because it is the only place where Ashby is forced to be Ashby.

If the engine is polluted, every layer above it becomes untrustworthy, because every layer above it
inherits its posture: how it decides, how it speaks, how it treats evidence, and how it gates action.

Sacred does not mean “never touched.”
Sacred means “never treated casually.”
Sacred means “kept minimal, protected, and constitutionally governed.”

This section defines what the engine is, what it owns, what it must never contain, and how it stays small
while Ashby grows.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
THE ENGINE IS THE BRAIN, NOT THE BODY
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Ashby is not a single program.
Ashby is a platform body plus removable organs.

- The body is the permanent substrate (law, policy, truth posture, state authority, routing discipline).
- The organs are the removable capabilities (modules, agents, integrations, products).

The engine/ is the brain of the body.

The engine:
- reasons about intent
- chooses policy outcomes
- plans WHAT to do
- routes HOW to do it through execution seams
- enforces truth posture and language constraints
- orchestrates updates to state authority

The engine never:
- talks to vendors
- knows brands
- executes hardware logic
- contains optional features

If something can be disabled, removed, or swapped, it does not belong in the engine.

That rule is not preference.
It is structural survival.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1) THE ENGINE’S OWNERSHIP (WHAT THE ENGINE IS ALLOWED TO OWN)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The engine owns platform invariants and their enforcement.

1.1 Policy ownership
The engine owns the bounded policy outcomes:
- ACT
- CLARIFY
- CONFIRM
- REFUSE
- CHAT

Modules may recommend.
Adapters may report.
But only the engine decides the policy outcome.

This prevents “capability pressure” from bypassing safety.
It prevents vendors from shaping decision-making.
It keeps Ashby singular.

1.2 Planning ownership
The engine owns the planning boundary:
- planning produces ActionRequests
- ActionRequests describe WHAT should be attempted
- planning never embeds vendor details

Modules help produce meaning.
But planning remains engine-governed so that meaning stays lawful and execution stays swappable.

1.3 Truth posture ownership
The engine owns Truth Gate enforcement:
- ActionResult is treated as evidence
- evidence strength is classified
- language posture is constrained by classification
- forbidden phrases are prevented
- mixed reality is allowed to be stated honestly

This is not “a feature.”
It is the difference between a system that is helpful and a system that lies.

1.4 State authority ownership (orchestration, not hoarding)
The engine owns the orchestration of state authority:
- it reads HouseState for context
- it writes updates to HouseState only through the lawful pipeline
- it never creates shadow truth stores

Important distinction:
- HouseState is the canonical belief store.
- The engine is the process that updates the belief store under law.

The engine may hold ephemeral runtime context (sessions, correlation state),
but it must not become a competing memory system.

1.5 Routing ownership (tool discipline)
The engine owns the routing discipline:
- it selects which adapter/provider is used
- it records which path executed
- it enforces that providers remain swappable
- it ensures that execution pathways cannot upgrade truth posture

Routing is HOW selection.
Routing is not meaning.

1.6 Auditability ownership
The engine owns correlation and reconstruction:
- correlation_id must exist and propagate
- decisions must be loggable
- the system must be able to explain why it did what it did

The engine is where “explainability” becomes mandatory infrastructure, not optional ergonomics.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2) THE ENGINE’S PROHIBITIONS (WHAT MUST NEVER ENTER THE ENGINE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The engine must remain vendor-blind and feature-minimal.

The following are forbidden inside engine/:

2.1 Vendor and brand knowledge
- no vendor-specific models
- no protocol-specific assumptions (Zigbee/Matter/etc.)
- no bridge entity model assumptions
- no “if brand == …” logic

The engine cannot be allowed to “know reality through a dependency.”
Reality must enter through evidence objects under Truth Gate.

2.2 Execution logic
- no direct API calls to devices
- no direct protocol commands
- no hardware retries and transport logic
- no bridge-specific execution shaping

Execution belongs in adapters/, bridges/, native_vendor/, connectors/.
Execution is replaceable; therefore it is not sacred.

2.3 Optional features
Anything that can reasonably be:
- toggled off
- removed
- swapped
- tier-gated
is not allowed in engine/.

Examples of “optional” that must never creep into the engine:
- product surfaces
- licensing logic
- feature flags as scattered conditionals
- vendor inventory ingestion
- specific domain modules (lights, irrigation, etc.)

The engine is not where new capabilities live.
New capabilities live as removable organs.

2.4 UI behavior and presentation decisions
The engine may produce structured responses and posture.
It must not contain:
- UI rendering logic
- product copy
- per-surface behavior tweaks

Surface differences live outside the engine via profiles and routing layers.

2.5 Secret storage or credential embedding
The engine must not:
- store secrets
- embed credentials
- log sensitive values
- create “debug paths” that bypass sanitization

Secrets belong to secret systems.
The engine references them by handle at the boundary and only where required.

2.6 “Just for now” shortcuts
Most sacred violations start as:
- “temporary”
- “quick fix”
- “we’ll refactor later”

The engine is where those shortcuts are forbidden.
If the shortcut is needed, it belongs outside the engine and behind a seam.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
3) WHY THE ENGINE MUST STAY SMALL (RESURRECTION AND REWRITEABILITY)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A sacred core must be rewriteable.

If the engine becomes huge:
- it becomes impossible to audit
- it becomes impossible to re-implement independently
- it becomes impossible to guarantee invariants are still enforced

Ashby’s entire resurrection premise requires:
- the engine’s responsibilities are narrow
- the engine’s semantics are explicit
- the engine can be rebuilt from the Codex without tribal knowledge

Therefore:
- most growth must occur outside engine/
- the engine must be “stable by refusal,” not “stable by accumulation”

The engine stays small by saying “no” relentlessly.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
4) THE ENGINE AS THE ENFORCER OF SEAMS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The engine is the place where boundaries are enforced, not merely described.

4.1 WHAT vs HOW boundary
The engine enforces that:
- meaning decisions live in modules/
- execution lives in adapters/providers
- execution cannot leak reasoning upward
- reasoning cannot leak execution details downward

4.2 Truth boundary
The engine enforces that:
- evidence is required for claims
- uncertainty stays visible
- state staleness is not hidden
- partial outcomes are reported honestly

4.3 Authority boundary
The engine enforces that:
- scope authority is checked before action
- policy gates exist before execution
- high-risk work requires confirmation when required

The engine is where “helpfulness pressure” is resisted structurally.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
5) THE ENGINE’S REQUIRED INTERFACES (HOW IT TOUCHES THE REST OF ASHBY)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The engine touches the rest of the platform only through contracts.

Minimum contract surfaces:

5.1 Input contract
- receives raw user input and transport metadata
- normalizes into Intent objects

5.2 Policy contract
- consumes Intent + context
- produces bounded policy outcomes and required next steps

5.3 Planning contract
- produces ActionRequests that are:
  - vendor-neutral
  - scope-bound
  - safety-aware

5.4 Execution contract
- invokes adapters/providers with ActionRequests
- receives ActionResults as evidence

5.5 Truth/State contract
- Truth Gate classifies evidence
- HouseState is updated through explicit rules
- response is constructed with correct language posture

If the engine begins to “reach around” these contracts, the design is breaking.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
6) THE ENGINE CHANGE RULE (WHEN SACRED CHANGES ARE ALLOWED)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Because the engine owns invariants, engine changes are high-risk by default.

Engine changes are allowed only if they preserve:
- truth posture integrity
- state authority integrity
- WHAT/HOW separation
- scope and identity law
- auditability and explainability

If an engine change alters any foundational semantics, it is constitutional
and requires governance (Control Sync doctrine).

The correct bias is:
- keep the engine stable
- evolve the platform through seams
- invoke governance only when truly required

Sacred means “changes deliberately,” not “changes constantly.”

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The engine must:
- reason, plan, and route under platform law
- enforce policy outcomes and safety gates
- enforce Truth Gate language posture
- orchestrate HouseState updates without becoming its own memory
- remain vendor-blind and brand-agnostic
- remain small, rewriteable, and auditable

The engine must never:
- contain vendor knowledge or execution logic
- contain optional features, products, or UI behavior
- bypass seams or embed “just for now” shortcuts
- weaken secrets handling or sanitization
- allow evidence-free certainty

If Ashby must survive time, the engine must remain sacred.

END OF 6.2


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 6.3
WHAT VS HOW (ABSOLUTE LAW)

WHAT and HOW are separate.

This separation is not a guideline.
It is absolute law.

WHAT defines intent, meaning, policy, and truth.
HOW defines execution, translation, and interaction with the external world.

If WHAT and HOW are allowed to mix,
Ashby ceases to be a platform.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
THE DEFINITION OF WHAT
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

WHAT describes:
- user intent
- system intent
- policy decisions
- scope resolution
- state transitions
- truth semantics
- session behavior
- explainability requirements

WHAT answers questions such as:
- What is the user trying to do?
- What is allowed?
- What should happen next?
- What must be verified?
- What may Ashby claim?
- What is the current belief and confidence?

WHAT is expressed in:
- engine reasoning
- cognition layers
- policy evaluation
- modules (as intent definitions)
- sessions
- scope law
- truth gating rules

WHAT is independent of:
- vendors
- protocols
- APIs
- hardware
- transport layers
- cloud availability

WHAT must remain stable even if every execution path changes.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
THE DEFINITION OF HOW
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

HOW describes:
- execution mechanics
- vendor interaction
- protocol translation
- API calls
- retries and backoff
- timeouts and fallbacks
- device-specific behavior
- transport concerns

HOW answers questions such as:
- How is this action carried out?
- Which vendor or protocol is used?
- How is the command translated?
- How is failure detected?
- How is evidence collected?

HOW is expressed in:
- adapters
- bridges
- native vendor integrations
- connectors
- execution services

HOW must never:
- infer intent
- decide policy
- interpret meaning
- own state authority
- upgrade confidence
- decide what Ashby may say

HOW exists to serve WHAT.
Never the reverse.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHY THIS SEPARATION IS NON-NEGOTIABLE
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Every large system that collapses
collapses because WHAT and HOW become entangled.

When this happens:
- reasoning becomes vendor-specific
- policy becomes execution-dependent
- state becomes implicit
- truth becomes narrative
- refactoring becomes dangerous
- growth becomes brittle

Ashby prevents this by enforcing:
- structural separation
- interface contracts
- unidirectional dependency
- truth gating at the platform level

WHAT must be able to exist without HOW.
HOW must never exist without WHAT.

This ensures:
- vendor replaceability
- offline capability
- explainability
- rebuildability
- long-term stability

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ENFORCEMENT MECHANISMS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Ashby enforces WHAT vs HOW separation through structure, not discipline.

The engine:
- reasons about WHAT
- never executes HOW

Execution layers:
- perform HOW
- never reason about WHAT

Adapters:
- accept ActionRequest
- return ActionResult
- never emit user-facing language
- never own truth semantics

The Truth Gate:
- exists above execution
- constrains language
- interprets evidence
- updates belief

No execution path may bypass the Truth Gate.
No execution result may directly influence language.

Any design that requires:
- engine imports of vendor code
- adapter-side policy decisions
- execution-driven truth claims
- vendor-shaped reasoning

is invalid.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSEQUENCES
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Violating WHAT vs HOW separation produces irreversible damage.

Once violated:
- vendors become sticky
- state becomes untrustworthy
- truth becomes relative
- the engine becomes polluted
- future domains become harder to add

These effects compound over time.

Therefore:

If a feature requires violating this separation,
the feature is rejected.

If a shortcut requires violating this separation,
the shortcut is rejected.

If convenience requires violating this separation,
convenience is rejected.

Ashby survives because this law is enforced
even when it is uncomfortable.

WHAT and HOW remain separate
so that Ashby remains Ashby.

END OF 6.3


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 6.4
SCOPES AS THE WORLD MODEL

Scopes are the world model of Ashby.

They are not labels.
They are not folders.
They are not convenience groupings.

Scopes are the formal structure through which Ashby understands,
reasons about, and acts within reality.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHAT A SCOPE IS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A scope is a bounded context that defines:
- what exists
- what may be acted upon
- what state is authoritative
- what authority applies
- what history is relevant

Every action Ashby takes occurs inside exactly one scope.

There is no global, unscoped action.
There is no unscoped state.
There is no unscoped memory.

If something cannot be placed in a scope,
it does not exist to Ashby.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHY SCOPES ARE REQUIRED
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Real-world systems are not flat.

They are:
- hierarchical
- overlapping
- partially isolated
- governed by boundaries

Without scopes, systems:
- infer context implicitly
- leak state between domains
- apply actions too broadly
- lose authority boundaries
- become unsafe to automate

Ashby treats scoping as mandatory
to prevent these failures.

Scopes make context explicit.
They make authority visible.
They make automation safe.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SCOPE OWNERSHIP AND AUTHORITY
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Each scope defines:
- what Ashby is allowed to control
- what Ashby may observe
- what Ashby may remember
- what Ashby may automate

Authority is scoped.

An action that is valid in one scope
may be forbidden in another.

State recorded in one scope
does not silently affect another.

This prevents:
- cross-domain contamination
- accidental overreach
- unsafe automation
- ambiguous responsibility

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SCOPES AND STATE
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

HouseState is not global.

State is always scoped.

Each scope maintains:
- its own beliefs
- its own confidence levels
- its own history
- its own uncertainty

State may be summarized upward.
State may never leak sideways.

A higher-level scope may:
- observe aggregate state
- reason about child scopes
- issue policies affecting children

A lower-level scope may never:
- override parent authority
- assume parent state
- act outside its bounds

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SCOPES AND EXECUTION
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Execution targets are resolved within scopes.

When Ashby executes an action:
- the target must exist in the scope
- the adapter must support the scope
- verification occurs within the scope
- results are recorded in the scope

An adapter that cannot respect scope boundaries
is incompatible with Ashby.

This ensures that execution respects:
- physical boundaries
- organizational boundaries
- permission boundaries

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SCOPES AND MEMORY
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Memory is scoped.

Ashby remembers actions,
outcomes,
and uncertainty
within the scope where they occurred.

Memory does not bleed across scopes.

This prevents:
- false continuity
- incorrect inference
- unsafe assumptions

When Ashby recalls history,
it recalls it relative to scope.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SCOPES AS THE FOUNDATION FOR GROWTH
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Scopes are what allow Ashby to grow safely.

New domains are added by:
- defining new scopes
- attaching modules to scopes
- assigning authority explicitly

No new domain requires redefining the world model.

It requires defining a new scope.

This allows Ashby to:
- expand without rewriting law
- add automation without increasing risk
- support multiple environments simultaneously

Scopes are the mechanism
by which Ashby understands the world
without flattening it.

If scopes are weakened,
Ashby becomes unsafe.

If scopes are enforced,
Ashby remains coherent.

Scopes are not an implementation detail.

They are Ashby’s model of reality.

END OF 6.4


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 6.5
ACTION-GATED TRUTH

Action-Gated Truth is the core enforcement mechanism of Ashby.

It is not a philosophy.
It is not a preference.
It is law.

Ashby is forbidden from claiming anything about the world
that has not passed through the Action Gate.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
THE ACTION GATE DEFINED
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The Action Gate is the boundary between execution and language.

Nothing may pass this boundary without evidence.

An action must produce an ActionResult.
An ActionResult must be evaluated.
Only then may Ashby update state or speak.

If an action does not produce an ActionResult,
Ashby must treat the outcome as UNKNOWN.

If an ActionResult indicates partial success,
Ashby must represent partial success explicitly.

If an ActionResult indicates failure,
Ashby must record failure and constrain language accordingly.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHY ACTION-GATED TRUTH EXISTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Most systems lie because they speak from intent.

A command was issued.
Therefore they assume it happened.

Ashby rejects this assumption.

Reality is not intent.
Reality is outcome.

Action-Gated Truth exists to ensure that:
- language follows evidence
- state follows execution
- confidence follows verification

Without this gate:
- logs become fiction
- explanations become narrative
- debugging becomes guesswork
- trust collapses invisibly

With this gate:
- failures are explicit
- uncertainty is honest
- follow-up actions are grounded
- explanations are meaningful

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ACTION RESULTS AND CONFIDENCE
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Every ActionResult must include:
- the attempted action
- the execution path used
- observed outcome
- verification status
- error conditions
- timing context

Ashby interprets ActionResults
into confidence classes:

- VERIFIED: Evidence confirms the outcome
- LAST_KNOWN: Execution occurred but verification is incomplete
- UNKNOWN: Outcome cannot be confirmed
- FAILED: Execution did not succeed

These classes are not cosmetic.
They directly constrain:
- language
- memory
- future decisions
- retry behavior

Ashby may never upgrade confidence
without new evidence.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LANGUAGE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Language in Ashby is constrained by the Action Gate.

Ashby may only:
- state what is VERIFIED
- qualify what is LAST_KNOWN
- admit what is UNKNOWN
- report what FAILED

Ashby is forbidden from:
- smoothing partial success
- inferring success from retries
- hiding verification gaps
- reconstructing certainty after restart

If Ashby cannot speak accurately,
it must pause or explain uncertainty.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ACTION-GATED TRUTH AND STATE
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

HouseState is updated only through the Action Gate.

State transitions require:
- an ActionResult
- confidence evaluation
- scope resolution

No component may write directly to belief
without passing through this process.

This ensures:
- single state authority
- traceable belief changes
- auditable history

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ACTION-GATED TRUTH AT SCALE
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Action-Gated Truth applies uniformly across domains.

It does not matter whether the action is:
- turning on a light
- watering a lawn
- recording a meeting
- generating media
- coordinating agents

If the action touches reality,
it must pass the gate.

This uniformity is what allows Ashby
to scale without losing honesty.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSEQUENCES
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

If Action-Gated Truth is bypassed,
Ashby ceases to exist as a trustworthy system.

There is no safe exception.
There is no shortcut.
There is no domain where this does not apply.

If a feature requires speaking past evidence,
the feature is rejected.

If convenience requires guessing,
convenience is rejected.

Ashby remains honest
because Action-Gated Truth is enforced mechanically,
not aspirationally.

Action-Gated Truth is the spine of Ashby.

Without it,
Ashby is just another assistant.

END OF 6.5


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 6.6
REMOVABLE MODULES

All modules in Ashby are removable.

This is not an optimization.
It is law.

Any capability beyond the engine must be optional,
detachable, and replaceable
without damaging Ashby’s integrity.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHY REMOVABILITY IS REQUIRED
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Systems collapse when features become permanent residents of the core.

Permanent features create:
- hidden dependencies
- implicit assumptions
- shared mutable state
- fear-driven refactors
- architectural paralysis

Ashby prevents this outcome by enforcing removability.

If something cannot be removed safely,
it does not belong in Ashby.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHAT A MODULE IS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A module is a unit of capability that:
- defines WHAT actions exist in a domain
- declares intent shapes and policies
- integrates with scopes
- relies on the engine for truth and state
- does not execute HOW directly

Modules describe behavior.
They do not perform execution.

Execution occurs elsewhere.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHAT A MODULE MUST NEVER DO
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A module must never:
- own state authority
- write directly to HouseState
- bypass the Action Gate
- speak to vendors
- import execution logic
- assume presence of other modules
- depend on shared mutable state

Any module that requires these behaviors
is invalid by definition.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
REMOVAL BEHAVIOR
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

When a module is removed:

- the engine remains intact
- other modules remain unaffected
- state related to the module degrades explicitly
- memory reflects loss of capability
- language reflects unavailability honestly

Ashby must not:
- crash
- lie
- infer behavior
- reconstruct missing capability

Removal is treated as a legitimate state,
not an error.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
REMOVABILITY AND GROWTH
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Removability enables safe growth.

It allows:
- experimentation without risk
- product profiles without forks
- domain isolation
- clean deprecation
- replacement without rewrites

If a new capability cannot be added
as a removable module,
the design is wrong.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ENFORCEMENT
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Ashby enforces removability structurally.

The engine does not:
- import modules directly
- assume module presence
- special-case module logic

Modules register capabilities.
The engine reasons generically.

This ensures that:
- absence is always safe
- presence is always explicit
- coupling is visible

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSEQUENCES
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Violating removability guarantees
creates irreversible architectural damage.

Once a module becomes inseparable:
- the engine is polluted
- future refactors become dangerous
- products harden into architecture

Therefore:

If a module cannot be removed,
it is rejected.

If a shortcut prevents removal,
the shortcut is rejected.

Ashby remains coherent
because nothing beyond the engine is permanent.

Removability is how Ashby stays alive.

END OF 6.6


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 6.7
BRIDGES AS TEMPORARY

Bridges are temporary by design.

They exist to accelerate transition,
not to define Ashby’s future.

A bridge is an integration layer that connects Ashby
to an external system whose architecture,
assumptions, or constraints Ashby does not control.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHAT A BRIDGE IS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A bridge is a compatibility layer that:
- allows Ashby to interoperate with an existing system
- adapts external behavior into Ashby’s execution model
- enables incremental migration
- limits blast radius during transition

Bridges are not core integrations.
They are not permanent dependencies.
They are not architectural anchors.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHY BRIDGES EXIST
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Real-world systems do not start clean.

Existing environments may already rely on:
- legacy platforms
- monolithic controllers
- opaque automation systems
- cloud-bound ecosystems

Bridges allow Ashby to enter these environments
without forcing immediate replacement.

They enable:
- gradual adoption
- parallel operation
- controlled migration
- early value delivery

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHAT BRIDGES MUST NEVER DO
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A bridge must never:
- define truth
- own state authority
- bypass the Action Gate
- leak foreign semantics into the engine
- introduce implicit dependencies
- become required for core operation

If a bridge is required for Ashby to function,
the architecture has failed.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
BRIDGES AND TRUTH
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Bridges may:
- forward commands
- relay telemetry
- translate protocols

Bridges may not:
- assert success as truth
- upgrade confidence
- hide uncertainty
- redefine outcomes

All bridge-reported results
are treated as evidence,
not authority.

They must pass through the same
ActionResult → Truth Gate → State pipeline
as any other execution path.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DEPRECATION AND REMOVAL
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Every bridge must have an exit path.

A bridge is considered successful when:
- it can be removed
- without changing Ashby’s behavior
- without rewriting engine logic
- without altering truth semantics

If a bridge cannot be removed cleanly,
it has overstayed its purpose.

Bridges must be:
- isolated
- documented
- replaceable
- monitored for removal readiness

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
BRIDGES VS NATIVE OWNERSHIP
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Bridges are a strategy, not an end state.

Over time, Ashby may:
- replace bridged functionality
- introduce native integrations
- remove legacy dependencies

This transition must be deliberate.

Native ownership is preferred
when it preserves platform law.

Bridges exist to buy time,
not to redefine the platform.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSEQUENCES
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

If a bridge becomes permanent,
Ashby becomes coupled to foreign constraints.

If a bridge defines behavior,
Ashby loses sovereignty.

Therefore:

Bridges are always optional.
Bridges are always removable.
Bridges are always temporary.

Ashby remains free
because bridges are never allowed
to become foundations.

END OF 6.7


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 6.8
NATIVE OWNERSHIP AS STRATEGY

Native ownership is a strategic choice, not an ideological one.

Ashby prefers to own critical execution paths
when doing so preserves truth, reliability, and autonomy.

This preference is deliberate.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHAT NATIVE OWNERSHIP MEANS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Native ownership means that Ashby:
- controls the execution logic
- understands failure modes directly
- owns verification semantics
- reduces dependence on opaque systems

Native ownership does not mean:
- reinventing everything
- rejecting all vendors
- avoiding interoperability
- refusing external services

It means owning what defines behavior.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHY NATIVE OWNERSHIP MATTERS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

External systems optimize for their own goals.

They may:
- change behavior without notice
- abstract away failure
- collapse multiple outcomes into success
- prioritize uptime metrics over truth

When Ashby relies entirely on such systems,
truth becomes diluted.

Native ownership allows Ashby to:
- observe execution directly
- verify outcomes honestly
- degrade confidence precisely
- explain failure accurately
- operate offline when required

This is not about control.
It is about accountability.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
NATIVE OWNERSHIP VS VENDOR DEPENDENCE
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Vendor systems are useful.
They accelerate development.
They reduce initial complexity.

But vendor dependence must never:
- define Ashby’s truth semantics
- dictate state transitions
- constrain explainability
- prevent offline operation
- block future evolution

Native ownership is chosen
when vendor abstraction interferes with these requirements.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
GRADUAL TRANSITION
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Native ownership is not an all-or-nothing decision.

Ashby may:
- begin with vendor integrations
- use bridges during transition
- selectively replace execution paths
- retain vendors where appropriate

This transition must be incremental,
deliberate,
and reversible.

The goal is not purity.
The goal is resilience.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
OWNERSHIP AND SCOPE
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Native ownership is applied per scope and per capability.

Some scopes may:
- require strict local control
- demand offline guarantees
- operate in safety-critical environments

Other scopes may:
- tolerate cloud dependence
- prefer convenience
- accept reduced verification

Ashby supports both,
without redefining its core law.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE BOUNDARIES
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Native ownership must never:
- pollute the engine
- violate WHAT vs HOW separation
- bypass the Action Gate
- introduce unremovable logic

Ownership lives in execution layers,
not in the core.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
STRATEGIC CONSEQUENCES
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Native ownership is how Ashby:
- maintains sovereignty
- survives vendor failure
- preserves honesty
- scales into new domains

It is a tool,
not a requirement,
but one that must be used deliberately.

Ashby remains adaptable
because ownership decisions are strategic,
not reactive.

END OF 6.8


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 6.9
PROFILES OVER FORKS

Ashby uses profiles, not forks.

Forking is the fastest way to destroy a platform.

Profiles are the mechanism by which Ashby supports
multiple products, environments, and behaviors
without duplicating truth, state, or law.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHAT A PROFILE IS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A profile is a configuration that selects:
- which modules are enabled
- which agents are active
- which execution paths are allowed
- which adapters are preferred
- which features are exposed
- which policies apply

A profile does not:
- change engine behavior
- redefine truth semantics
- alter state authority
- bypass governance
- fork code

Profiles describe composition,
not divergence.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHY FORKS ARE FORBIDDEN
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Forks create parallel realities.

Each fork:
- duplicates assumptions
- drifts independently
- accumulates exceptions
- fractures understanding
- multiplies failure modes

Over time:
- fixes do not propagate
- behavior diverges
- trust becomes inconsistent
- rebuildability is lost

Forks feel fast.
They are never safe.

Ashby forbids forks at the platform level.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PROFILES AND PRODUCTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Products in Ashby are profiles.

A home automation product
is a profile.

A meetings capture product
is a profile.

An inventory operations product
is a profile.

These profiles:
- share the same engine
- share the same truth system
- share the same state model
- share the same governance

Only configuration differs.

This allows Ashby to:
- ship multiple products safely
- evolve capabilities independently
- avoid product-driven architecture
- preserve long-term coherence

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PROFILES AND SAFETY
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Profiles enforce safety by constraint.

A profile may:
- disable dangerous capabilities
- restrict automation scope
- require confirmation
- prefer conservative execution paths

Profiles do not:
- weaken core law
- introduce exceptions
- change semantics to fit a product

Safety is achieved by selection,
not by modification.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ENFORCEMENT
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Ashby enforces profiles structurally.

The engine:
- loads configuration
- evaluates policy
- reasons generically

No code path may:
- assume a specific profile
- branch on product identity
- special-case behavior per product

If behavior must differ,
it must be expressed as configuration.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSEQUENCES
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

If a requirement cannot be met via profiles,
it does not belong on the platform.

If a feature requires forking,
the feature is rejected.

Ashby remains coherent
because there is one system,
many profiles,
and zero forks.

Profiles are how Ashby scales
without losing itself.

END OF 6.9


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 6.10
UNDERSTANDABILITY AS A REQUIREMENT

Understandability is a requirement, not a byproduct.

Ashby is not allowed to become a system
that only its original authors can understand.

If a system cannot be understood,
it cannot be trusted.
If it cannot be trusted,
it cannot be allowed to act.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHAT UNDERSTANDABILITY MEANS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Understandability means that a competent builder,
without tribal knowledge,
can:

- reason about why Ashby acted or refused
- trace how an action moved through the system
- identify where state changed
- see what evidence supported a belief
- determine what Ashby is unsure about
- predict how a change will propagate

Understandability does not mean simplicity.
Ashby may be complex.

It means legibility.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHY UNDERSTANDABILITY IS NON-NEGOTIABLE
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Systems fail silently when they become opaque.

Opacity leads to:
- fear-driven development
- superstition instead of debugging
- reliance on folklore
- hesitation to change anything
- eventual abandonment or rewrite

Ashby rejects opacity.

Ashby is designed to be:
- inspected
- reasoned about
- explained
- rebuilt

This is only possible if understandability
is treated as a first-class constraint.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
STRUCTURAL REQUIREMENTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Ashby enforces understandability through structure.

The system must:
- have clear boundaries between layers
- use explicit data models
- record decisions and outcomes
- avoid implicit behavior
- make state transitions observable
- expose confidence explicitly

Implicit behavior is forbidden.

If something matters,
it must be represented explicitly.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
UNDERSTANDABILITY UNDER FAILURE
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Failure is where understandability matters most.

When Ashby fails,
it must be possible to determine:
- what failed
- where it failed
- why it failed
- what evidence exists
- what is now uncertain

Ashby must not:
- hide failures
- collapse errors into success
- require log archaeology
- depend on guesswork

Failure must be explainable
in terms of recorded facts.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
UNDERSTANDABILITY AND GROWTH
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

As systems grow,
they tend to trade clarity for speed.

Ashby refuses this trade.

Growth must not:
- obscure reasoning
- entangle responsibilities
- hide dependencies
- introduce magical behavior

If a new capability reduces understandability,
it is rejected or redesigned.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ENFORCEMENT
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Understandability is enforced by:

- strict layering
- explicit contracts
- canonical data models
- recorded ActionResults
- scoped state
- traceable decisions
- refusal of clever shortcuts

“Clever” designs that cannot be explained
are architectural violations.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSEQUENCES
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

If Ashby becomes unintelligible,
it becomes unsafe.

Therefore:

If a design choice reduces understandability,
it is rejected.

If a shortcut hides behavior,
it is rejected.

If a feature cannot be explained clearly,
it does not belong in Ashby.

Ashby remains durable
because it remains understandable.

Understandability is not optional.

It is a requirement for agency.

END OF 6.10


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 6.11
CONTROL SYNC GOVERNANCE

Control Sync is the governance mechanism of Ashby.

It exists to ensure that foundational change
is deliberate, visible, and accountable.

Without Control Sync,
Ashby would drift invisibly
until its identity was lost.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHAT CONTROL SYNC IS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A Control Sync is a formal decision process
required for any change that affects Ashby’s core law.

It is not a meeting for convenience.
It is not a procedural ritual.
It is a safeguard.

Control Sync exists to pause momentum
long enough for judgment to occur.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHEN CONTROL SYNC IS REQUIRED
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A Control Sync is required when a change affects:

- engine responsibilities
- truth semantics
- state authority
- scope model
- WHAT vs HOW separation
- removability guarantees
- governance assumptions
- rebuildability guarantees

These changes redefine what Ashby is.

They are not incremental improvements.
They are identity changes.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHEN CONTROL SYNC IS NOT REQUIRED
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Control Sync is not required for:

- adding or removing modules
- adding or removing agents
- adding or removing adapters
- adding vendors or protocols
- creating or modifying profiles
- tuning policies within existing law
- fixing bugs that do not alter semantics

These are expected forms of growth
that occur within Ashby’s defined boundaries.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
THE CONTROL SYNC PROCESS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A Control Sync requires:

1) Explicit articulation of the proposed change
2) Clear identification of affected laws or principles
3) Explanation of why existing law is insufficient
4) Description of the new law or modification
5) Acknowledgment of consequences
6) Update to the Codex reflecting the change

No Control Sync is valid
without Codex update.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHY CONTROL SYNC IS NON-NEGOTIABLE
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Systems do not fail from large changes.
They fail from small, unexamined ones.

Control Sync prevents:
- accidental redefinition of the engine
- erosion of truth enforcement
- silent scope expansion
- normalization of exceptions
- loss of rebuildability

It protects future builders
from inheriting invisible decisions.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
CONTROL SYNC AND AUTHORITY
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Control Sync does not centralize power.
It centralizes responsibility.

Any participant in a Control Sync
must accept that:

- the decision becomes permanent law
- future work is constrained by it
- the Codex reflects it explicitly

There is no informal override.

If a change is uncomfortable,
that discomfort is a signal.

If a change feels “too big to explain,”
it is too big to implement without Control Sync.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSEQUENCES
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Any foundational change made without Control Sync
is considered invalid.

If discovered,
the system must be realigned
to the Codex immediately.

Control Sync exists
so that Ashby remains intentional
even as it evolves.

Governance is not overhead.

It is how Ashby survives time.

END OF 6.11


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 7.1
WHAT A SCOPE IS

A scope is the fundamental unit of reality in Ashby.

It is not a label.
It is not a convenience abstraction.
It is not a user-facing grouping.

A scope is a formally bounded context that defines
what exists, what may be acted upon, and what authority applies.

If something does not exist inside a scope,
it does not exist to Ashby.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
THE PURPOSE OF SCOPES
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Ashby operates in environments where:
- multiple domains coexist
- authority is not uniform
- state changes over time
- actions have consequences

Without scopes, these environments collapse into ambiguity.

Scopes exist to make boundaries explicit.

They define:
- the limits of action
- the ownership of state
- the validity of memory
- the reach of automation
- the context of reasoning

A scope answers the question:
“Where is this action allowed to occur?”

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SCOPES AS BOUNDARIES OF AUTHORITY
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Every scope establishes authority.

Authority determines:
- what Ashby may control
- what Ashby may observe
- what Ashby may remember
- what Ashby may automate

Authority is never assumed.
Authority is never inferred.
Authority is always scoped.

An action that is valid in one scope
may be invalid or forbidden in another.

This prevents:
- accidental overreach
- unsafe automation
- cross-domain contamination
- silent expansion of control

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SCOPES AND EXISTENCE
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Scopes define existence.

Devices, entities, state, and history
exist only within scopes.

There is no global device.
There is no global state.
There is no global memory.

Global assumptions are forbidden.

If Ashby cannot determine the scope,
it must not act.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SCOPES AND REASONING
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

All reasoning in Ashby is scoped.

Intent is resolved within a scope.
Policy is evaluated within a scope.
Execution targets are selected within a scope.
State is updated within a scope.

This ensures that reasoning remains contextual,
bounded,
and explainable.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SCOPES AND SAFETY
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Scopes are a safety mechanism.

Automation without scope
is automation without restraint.

Scopes allow Ashby to:
- limit blast radius
- apply conservative defaults
- require confirmation where needed
- restrict automation to safe contexts

If scope cannot be resolved confidently,
Ashby must pause or ask for clarification.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

No action may occur outside a scope.
No state may exist outside a scope.
No memory may be recalled outside a scope.

Any design that introduces
unscoped action, state, or memory
violates Ashby’s world model.

Scopes are not optional.

They are the foundation upon which
all reasoning, action, and truth are built.

END OF 7.1


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 7.2
TYPES OF SCOPES (PHYSICAL, LOGICAL, ORGANIZATIONAL)

Scopes in Ashby are not uniform.

They represent different kinds of boundaries that exist in the real world.
Understanding these distinctions is required to reason safely,
apply authority correctly,
and scale the system without flattening reality.

Ashby recognizes multiple scope archetypes.
These archetypes describe shape, not limits.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PHYSICAL SCOPES
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Physical scopes represent bounded regions of the physical world.

Examples include:
- a room
- a floor
- a building
- a property
- a zone within a property

Physical scopes define:
- where devices exist
- where sensors report from
- where actions have physical consequences

A light exists in a physical scope.
A thermostat exists in a physical scope.
A camera exists in a physical scope.

Physical scope boundaries prevent:
- actions from leaking across spaces
- automation from affecting unintended areas
- ambiguous targeting

A command such as “turn off the lights”
is meaningless without physical scope resolution.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LOGICAL SCOPES
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Logical scopes represent conceptual groupings
that do not map directly to physical space.

Examples include:
- a device group
- a functional role
- a system capability
- a virtual collection

Logical scopes allow Ashby to reason about:
- related capabilities
- shared behavior
- abstract targets

A logical scope may span:
- multiple rooms
- multiple buildings
- multiple physical scopes

Logical scopes never override physical safety.
They operate within physical constraints.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ORGANIZATIONAL SCOPES
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Organizational scopes represent authority and ownership.

Examples include:
- a household
- a business unit
- a tenant
- a customer account
- an operational boundary

Organizational scopes define:
- who may issue commands
- what automations are permitted
- what data may be retained
- what actions require confirmation

Organizational scope boundaries are critical
in multi-user and multi-tenant systems.

No action may cross organizational scopes
without explicit authorization.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SCOPE COMPOSITION
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Scopes are composable.

A physical scope may exist inside
an organizational scope.

A logical scope may reference
multiple physical scopes.

This composition allows Ashby to model reality
without collapsing it into a single dimension.

Composition is explicit.
Implicit scope inheritance is forbidden.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

No scope type may:
- bypass another scope’s authority
- override safety constraints
- collapse boundaries for convenience

If a design requires flattening scope types,
the design is invalid.

Scopes model reality as it is,
not as it is convenient to automate.

END OF 7.2


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 7.3
ZONES AS A SPECIALIZATION OF SCOPE

Zones are a specialized form of scope.

They exist to bridge human language and physical reality
without compromising Ashby’s world model.

A zone is not merely a room name.
A zone is a controllable physical region
with defined boundaries, authority, and state.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHY ZONES EXIST
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Humans think and speak in zones.

They say:
- “the bedroom”
- “upstairs”
- “the backyard”
- “the shop”
- “the east side of the property”

These phrases describe regions,
not individual devices.

Zones allow Ashby to map this natural language
onto explicit, enforceable scopes.

Without zones:
- language becomes ambiguous
- automation becomes unsafe
- actions become overly broad
- confirmation logic becomes inconsistent

Zones resolve this tension.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ZONES VS ROOMS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Rooms are human labels.
Zones are operational scopes.

A single room label may:
- map to one zone
- map to part of a zone
- map to multiple zones

A zone may:
- contain multiple rooms
- contain part of a room
- exist without a room label

This separation prevents
human naming from defining reality.

Reality is defined by zones.
Language adapts to zones,
not the reverse.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ZONE AUTHORITY
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Zones define authority boundaries for physical action.

Within a zone, Ashby may:
- control devices assigned to the zone
- aggregate sensor data
- apply automation policies
- reason about state cohesively

Across zones, Ashby must:
- respect boundaries
- require explicit intent
- avoid implicit spillover

A command such as:
“turn off the lights”

is incomplete without zone resolution.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ZONE COMPOSITION
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Zones are composable.

A zone may:
- exist inside another zone
- overlap with another zone
- share devices with another zone (explicitly)

Composition must always be explicit.

Implicit overlap is forbidden.

This allows:
- hierarchical control
- partial automation
- targeted overrides
without ambiguity.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ZONES AND STATE
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Zone state is authoritative for that region.

Ashby tracks:
- device state by zone
- confidence by zone
- history by zone
- uncertainty by zone

Zone-level state allows Ashby to:
- explain what changed where
- isolate failures
- degrade confidence locally
- avoid global assumptions

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ZONES AND AUTOMATION SAFETY
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Zones are a primary safety mechanism.

Automation applies to zones,
not to the entire system.

This allows:
- scoped automation
- bounded blast radius
- predictable behavior

If zone resolution is ambiguous,
Ashby must pause or clarify.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Zones must never:
- be inferred silently
- be overridden by convenience
- collapse into global state

Any design that allows actions
to bypass zone boundaries
violates Ashby’s world model.

Zones are how Ashby understands
and safely controls physical space.

END OF 7.3


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 7.4
ROOMS AS HUMAN LABELS

Rooms are not scopes.

They are human-facing labels applied to physical space
to support natural language interaction.

Ashby treats rooms as aliases,
not as sources of authority.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHY ROOMS EXIST
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Humans organize space linguistically.

They speak in terms of:
- bedrooms
- kitchens
- offices
- garages
- living rooms

These labels are useful for communication,
but they are not sufficiently precise
to define safe automation or authority.

Rooms exist to support understanding,
not to define reality.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ROOMS VS ZONES
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Zones define operational reality.
Rooms describe human intent.

A room label:
- may map to one zone
- may map to part of a zone
- may map to multiple zones

A zone:
- may contain multiple rooms
- may contain no room labels
- exists independently of human naming

This separation prevents language drift
from redefining system behavior.

Ashby never reasons about authority
based on room labels alone.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ROOM MAPPING
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Room labels are mapped explicitly.

Mapping is:
- configurable
- user-defined
- reversible
- non-authoritative

Room mappings may change
without altering zone structure.

This allows:
- renaming
- cultural variation
- personal preference
- language evolution

without architectural impact.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ROOMS AND AMBIGUITY
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Rooms frequently introduce ambiguity.

Examples include:
- overlapping room concepts
- informal naming
- partial references
- inconsistent usage

Ashby treats room ambiguity
as a signal to clarify,
not as permission to guess.

Room labels may inform scope resolution,
but they never override it.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ROOMS AND EXPLANATION
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

When Ashby explains actions,
it may reference rooms for clarity.

Explanation may use human labels.
Execution never does.

This distinction ensures that:
- explanations remain understandable
- actions remain precise
- safety is preserved

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Rooms must never:
- define authority
- own state
- bypass zone boundaries
- override scope law

Any design that elevates room labels
to operational authority
violates Ashby’s world model.

Rooms exist to help humans talk to Ashby.

Zones exist to keep Ashby safe.

END OF 7.4


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 7.5
SCOPE HIERARCHIES AND CONTAINMENT

Scopes are hierarchical.

They form containment relationships
that define authority, inheritance, and limits.

Hierarchy is not optional.
It is required to model reality safely.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHY HIERARCHY EXISTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Real environments are nested.

A room exists inside a zone.
A zone exists inside a property.
A property exists inside an organization.

Without hierarchy:
- authority becomes ambiguous
- automation becomes unsafe
- overrides become unpredictable
- explanations become unclear

Hierarchy allows Ashby to reason
about where actions apply
and where they must stop.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PARENT AND CHILD SCOPES
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A parent scope:
- defines upper bounds of authority
- may impose policy on child scopes
- may observe aggregated state
- may restrict child behavior

A child scope:
- operates within parent constraints
- owns its local state
- may not override parent authority
- may not assume parent intent

Authority flows downward.
Constraints flow downward.
Overrides never flow upward implicitly.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
CONTAINMENT RULES
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Containment is explicit.

A scope must declare:
- its parent scope
- its containment boundaries
- its authority limits

Implicit containment is forbidden.

If containment cannot be expressed clearly,
the scope definition is invalid.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
STATE AND CONTAINMENT
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

State is contained.

Child scope state:
- does not silently modify parent state
- may be summarized upward explicitly
- retains its own confidence and history

Parent scopes may reason
about child scope state,
but must not overwrite it.

This prevents:
- hidden coupling
- false global assumptions
- cascading errors

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
CONTAINMENT AND AUTOMATION
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Automation respects containment.

An automation defined at a parent scope:
- applies only to contained scopes
- may be restricted by child configuration
- must respect local uncertainty

An automation defined at a child scope:
- must not escape its boundary
- must not affect siblings
- must not bypass parent policy

Containment defines blast radius.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Scopes must never:
- infer containment
- collapse hierarchy for convenience
- bypass parent constraints
- override child state silently

Any design that flattens scope hierarchy
violates Ashby’s world model.

Hierarchy is how Ashby
models complexity without losing control.

END OF 7.5


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 7.6
SCOPE-BOUND AUTHORITY AND STATE

Authority and state in Ashby are inseparable from scope.

There is no authority without scope.
There is no state without scope.

This binding is what prevents Ashby
from acting globally, guessing context,
or leaking control across boundaries.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
AUTHORITY IS ALWAYS SCOPED
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Authority defines what Ashby is permitted to do.

In Ashby:
- authority is granted per scope
- authority is never global
- authority is never implied
- authority is never inferred from convenience

An action is permitted
only if the active scope grants authority for it.

If authority cannot be resolved confidently,
Ashby must refuse or clarify.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
STATE IS ALWAYS SCOPED
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

State represents Ashby’s belief about reality.

That belief exists only within scope.

State includes:
- device state
- environmental conditions
- automation status
- historical actions
- confidence levels
- uncertainty markers

There is no global HouseState.

HouseState is a collection of scoped beliefs,
not a flat store.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHY SCOPE-BOUND STATE IS REQUIRED
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Without scoped state:
- failures propagate incorrectly
- confidence upgrades leak globally
- partial success is misrepresented
- explanations lose precision

Scope-bound state ensures that:
- belief changes are localized
- uncertainty is contained
- confidence degrades correctly
- explanations reference the correct context

This is required for honesty at scale.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
AUTHORITY VS PERMISSION
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Authority is structural.
Permission is situational.

Authority answers:
“Is this action allowed in this scope?”

Permission answers:
“Is this action allowed right now?”

Permissions may depend on:
- time
- user role
- safety conditions
- confirmation state

Authority must exist
before permission is evaluated.

If authority is absent,
permission is irrelevant.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SCOPE TRANSITIONS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Ashby may reason across scopes,
but it may not act across scopes
without explicit transition.

A scope transition requires:
- explicit resolution
- explanation
- confirmation when appropriate

Implicit scope escalation is forbidden.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

No action may:
- upgrade state outside its scope
- claim authority outside its scope
- modify sibling scope state
- bypass scope checks

Any design that introduces
global authority or global state
violates Ashby’s core law.

Scope-bound authority and state
are how Ashby remains safe,
honest,
and explainable.

END OF 7.6


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 7.7
WHY ADAPTERS MAY NEVER DEFINE REALITY

Adapters may never define reality in Ashby.

This rule is absolute.

Adapters exist to interact with the external world.
They do not own truth.
They do not own state.
They do not own meaning.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHAT AN ADAPTER IS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

An adapter is an execution interface.

It translates Ashby’s intent
into external actions
and returns observable results.

Adapters exist to:
- speak vendor protocols
- issue commands
- receive telemetry
- report execution outcomes

Adapters do not reason.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHY ADAPTER-DEFINED REALITY IS DANGEROUS
