──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

External systems:
- collapse outcomes into success
- hide partial failure
- report intent as completion
- mask uncertainty
- change semantics over time

If Ashby accepts adapter output as truth,
then reality becomes vendor-defined.

This leads to:
- false confidence
- silent failure
- inconsistent explanations
- loss of rebuildability
- erosion of trust

Ashby rejects this.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EVIDENCE VS TRUTH
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Adapters produce evidence.

Evidence must be:
- interpreted
- evaluated
- contextualized
- gated

Only the engine may perform this interpretation.

An adapter may say:
“Command accepted.”

Ashby may say:
“The action was attempted, but verification is incomplete.”

This distinction preserves honesty.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ADAPTER BOUNDARIES
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Adapters must never:
- write directly to HouseState
- upgrade confidence
- decide language
- infer success
- assume continuity
- reconcile conflicting data

Adapters must:
- return raw execution results
- report errors explicitly
- expose uncertainty
- avoid semantic interpretation

Any adapter that violates these rules
is incompatible with Ashby.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ADAPTERS AND SCOPES
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Adapters operate within scope,
but do not define it.

They may:
- receive scoped targets
- execute scoped actions
- return scoped evidence

They may not:
- infer scope
- escalate scope
- collapse scope boundaries

Scope law is owned by the engine.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHY THIS MATTERS AT SCALE
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

As Ashby grows:
- more vendors are added
- more protocols are bridged
- more domains are integrated

If adapters are allowed to define reality,
Ashby fractures into vendor-shaped truths.

By forbidding this,
Ashby maintains:
- consistent truth semantics
- explainable behavior
- vendor replaceability
- offline survivability
- long-term integrity

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSEQUENCES
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

If adapters define reality,
Ashby becomes a proxy, not a platform.

Therefore:

Adapters produce evidence.
The engine defines reality.

This boundary must never be crossed.

END OF 7.7


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 8.1
PURPOSE OF THE ARCHITECTURE TREE

The Architecture Tree defines the physical shape of Ashby.

It is not an example layout.
It is not a suggestion.
It is not an implementation snapshot.

It is a zoning diagram that enforces
where responsibilities may live
and where they must never live.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHY THE ARCHITECTURE TREE EXISTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

As systems grow, files accumulate.

Without a strict structural model:
- logic drifts toward convenience
- responsibilities blur
- core files swell
- refactors become dangerous
- understanding decays

The Architecture Tree exists to prevent this.

It defines:
- ownership boundaries
- dependency direction
- allowed interactions
- forbidden couplings

If something cannot be placed cleanly
within the Architecture Tree,
the design is incomplete.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
THE TREE AS LAW, NOT ORGANIZATION
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The Architecture Tree is law.

It enforces WHAT vs HOW separation,
engine sanctity,
module removability,
and scope integrity
through physical placement.

This is intentional.

By forcing structure at the filesystem level,
Ashby makes violations visible early.

A misplaced file is not cosmetic.
It is a signal.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
THE TREE AND REBUILDABILITY
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The Architecture Tree allows Ashby
to be rebuilt from text alone.

A builder who understands the tree
can:
- recreate directory structure
- infer responsibility boundaries
- place new logic safely
- avoid polluting the engine

The tree encodes architectural intent
in a form that survives:
- time
- personnel change
- refactors
- platform migration

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
THE TREE AND GROWTH
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Growth without a tree
creates entropy.

Growth with a tree
creates structure.

As Ashby grows:
- new domains are added
- new modules appear
- new execution paths emerge

The tree absorbs this growth
without changing its shape.

If growth requires changing the tree,
a Control Sync is required.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHAT THE ARCHITECTURE TREE IS NOT
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The Architecture Tree is not:
- a complete inventory of files
- a guarantee of current implementation
- a prescription for naming conventions
- a restriction on internal complexity

It defines zones of responsibility,
not file counts.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

No component may:
- live outside the tree
- violate dependency direction
- bypass its assigned layer
- redefine ownership by placement

The Architecture Tree is how Ashby
remains legible,
auditable,
and rebuildable
as it scales.

END OF 8.1


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 8.2
ENGINE (SACRED CORE)

The engine is the sacred core of Ashby.

It is the only component that defines identity.
Everything else is replaceable.
The engine is not.

The Architecture Tree exists primarily
to protect the engine from pollution.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHAT THE ENGINE IS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The engine is the reasoning and governance core.

It owns:
- intent interpretation
- policy evaluation
- scope resolution
- truth enforcement
- state authority
- memory semantics
- session law
- explainability guarantees
- Control Sync governance

The engine decides WHAT Ashby is allowed to do
and WHAT Ashby is allowed to claim.

The engine never decides HOW anything is executed.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHAT THE ENGINE IS NOT
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The engine is not:
- a vendor integration layer
- a protocol handler
- a device controller
- a hardware abstraction
- a product feature hub
- a convenience layer

If a component can be removed
without changing Ashby’s identity,
it does not belong in the engine.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ENGINE DEPENDENCY DIRECTION
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

All dependencies point inward.

The engine may:
- define interfaces
- declare contracts
- consume abstract results

The engine must never:
- import adapters
- import vendors
- import bridges
- import connectors
- depend on execution details

Execution layers depend on the engine.
The engine never depends on them.

This ensures:
- replaceability
- testability
- rebuildability
- clarity of authority

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ENGINE AND TRUTH
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Truth enforcement lives exclusively in the engine.

Only the engine may:
- interpret ActionResults
- assign confidence levels
- update HouseState
- constrain language
- degrade certainty
- mark unknowns

No other layer may claim truth.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ENGINE AND MEMORY
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Memory semantics are engine-owned.

The engine defines:
- what is remembered
- how memory is scoped
- how confidence decays
- how history is recalled
- how loss is represented

Memory storage may live elsewhere.
Memory meaning lives here.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ENGINE AND GOVERNANCE
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The engine enforces governance.

Changes to:
- engine responsibilities
- truth semantics
- scope law
- state authority

require Control Sync.

This prevents accidental redefinition.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The engine must never:
- contain optional behavior
- special-case domains
- encode vendor logic
- accept execution shortcuts
- allow truth leakage
- become a god-object

Any violation of engine sanctity
is an architectural failure.

The engine remains small,
disciplined,
and sacred
so that Ashby remains Ashby.

END OF 8.2


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 8.3
COGNITION SUBSYSTEMS

Cognition subsystems define how Ashby thinks.

They do not define what Ashby is.
They define how reasoning is structured,
sequenced,
and constrained.

Cognition exists to transform intent into action
without violating truth, state, or scope law.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
THE ROLE OF COGNITION
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Cognition is the internal decision machinery of the engine.

It is responsible for:
- interpreting normalized intent
- resolving scope and context
- selecting policy paths
- deciding whether to act, clarify, or refuse
- sequencing reasoning steps
- preparing ActionRequests

Cognition never executes actions.
It prepares decisions for execution layers.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SUBSYSTEM, NOT MONOLITH
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Cognition is not a single algorithm.

It is composed of subsystems,
each with a bounded responsibility.

This prevents:
- god-reasoners
- hidden coupling
- untraceable decisions
- brittle logic growth

Each subsystem must be:
- explicit
- testable
- replaceable
- auditable

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
CORE COGNITION SUBSYSTEMS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Cognition subsystems include, but are not limited to:

- Intent normalization
- Scope resolution
- Ambiguity detection
- Policy evaluation
- Session management
- Safety checks
- Action planning
- Clarification generation

These are representative categories,
not a fixed inventory.

The shape is invariant.
The instances may evolve.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SEQUENCING AND FLOW
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Cognition is sequential and gated.

Each stage:
- consumes explicit inputs
- produces explicit outputs
- may halt the pipeline
- may require clarification
- may refuse to proceed

No stage may:
- assume success of a later stage
- skip required checks
- bypass scope or truth law

This sequencing makes decisions traceable.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
COGNITION AND EXPLAINABILITY
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Cognition must leave a trail.

Every decision must be explainable
in terms of:
- which subsystem acted
- what inputs were considered
- why a path was chosen
- why alternatives were rejected

Opaque reasoning is forbidden.

If a decision cannot be explained,
the cognition design is invalid.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
COGNITION AND GROWTH
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

As Ashby grows,
cognition complexity increases.

Subsystem boundaries prevent collapse.

New reasoning capabilities must:
- fit within existing subsystems
- or introduce a new explicit subsystem
- without modifying unrelated logic

Ad-hoc reasoning is forbidden.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Cognition subsystems must never:
- execute actions
- define truth
- own state
- bypass scope checks
- invent certainty

Cognition prepares decisions.
The engine governs truth.
Execution proves reality.

This separation preserves honesty
even as reasoning becomes more powerful.

END OF 8.3


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 8.4
TRUTH, STATE, AND MEMORY

Truth, state, and memory form a single system in Ashby.

They are not independent concerns.
They are mechanically linked
to prevent drift, illusion, and silent failure.

This section defines how these concepts coexist
inside the architecture.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TRUTH AS A CONSTRAINED OUTPUT
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Truth in Ashby is not opinion.
It is not confidence.
It is not conversational tone.

Truth is a constrained output
derived from evidence.

Ashby may only claim what passes
the Action-Gated Truth system.

Truth constrains:
- language
- state updates
- explanations
- future reasoning

No subsystem may speak past truth.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
STATE AS BELIEF WITH CONFIDENCE
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

State represents Ashby’s belief about the world.

Every belief carries confidence.

State is:
- scoped
- explicit
- persistent
- degradable

State is never assumed.
State is never reconstructed silently.
State is never global.

Belief may be:
- VERIFIED
- LAST_KNOWN
- UNKNOWN
- FAILED

These classes are not descriptive.
They are enforcement mechanisms.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
MEMORY AS RECORDED HISTORY
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Memory records:
- attempted actions
- execution results
- verification outcomes
- confidence changes
- uncertainty events

Memory is not conversation history.
Conversation is transient.
Memory is durable.

Memory exists to:
- preserve accountability
- enable explanation
- inform future decisions
- prevent repetition of failure

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
THE LINKAGE BETWEEN THE THREE
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Truth gates state.
State informs memory.
Memory constrains future truth.

This loop is intentional.

If any link is broken:
- truth becomes narrative
- state becomes assumption
- memory becomes fiction

Therefore, the linkage is enforced structurally.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DECAY AND LOSS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Knowledge degrades over time.

Ashby treats decay as first-class behavior.

Confidence may:
- expire
- degrade
- be downgraded explicitly

Memory may:
- record loss
- mark uncertainty
- trigger re-verification

Ashby never pretends certainty persists.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ARCHITECTURAL PLACEMENT
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Truth enforcement lives in the engine.
State authority lives in the engine.
Memory semantics live in the engine.

Storage may live elsewhere.
Meaning does not.

This prevents:
- external truth definition
- fragmented belief
- hidden state mutation

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

No component may:
- define truth outside the engine
- write state without evidence
- alter memory semantics
- upgrade confidence without verification

Truth, state, and memory
are inseparable by design.

This triad is how Ashby
remains honest over time.

END OF 8.4


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 8.5
SCOPES AND DOMAIN MODELING

Domains in Ashby are modeled through scopes.

Ashby does not model domains directly.
It models reality through scoped contexts,
and domains emerge from those contexts.

This distinction prevents domain-specific assumptions
from contaminating the core.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHAT A DOMAIN IS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A domain is a category of behavior
that operates within defined scopes.

Examples include:
- lighting
- irrigation
- climate
- monitoring
- inventory
- meetings

These examples are illustrative,
not exhaustive.

Domains describe WHAT can happen.
Scopes describe WHERE it may happen.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHY DOMAIN MODELING IS SCOPE-FIRST
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

If domains are modeled directly,
they tend to hardcode assumptions:
- where they operate
- what they control
- how authority is applied

Ashby avoids this by anchoring domains to scopes.

A domain declares:
- which scope types it supports
- what actions it may perform in those scopes
- what state it maintains per scope
- what safety constraints apply

The scope remains authoritative.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DOMAIN ISOLATION
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Domains must be isolated from each other.

No domain may:
- assume another domain exists
- modify another domain’s state
- infer cross-domain intent
- bypass scope boundaries

Cross-domain coordination occurs
through the engine,
not through direct coupling.

This preserves removability.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DOMAIN STATE AND SCOPES
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Domain state is always scoped.

A lighting domain does not have “global state.”
It has lighting state per scope.

This allows:
- partial failure isolation
- local confidence degradation
- precise explanation

Domain state never escapes its scope.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ADDING NEW DOMAINS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

New domains are added by:
- defining supported scope types
- defining actions within those scopes
- defining state models per scope
- integrating with cognition and execution layers

No new domain requires
changing the scope system.

If it does,
the design is invalid or incomplete.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Domains must never:
- define new scope types implicitly
- override scope authority
- flatten scoped state
- introduce global assumptions

Scopes are the foundation.
Domains adapt to scopes,
not the reverse.

This ensures that Ashby can expand
into new domains
without redefining reality.

END OF 8.5


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 8.6
MODULES (WHAT)

Modules define WHAT Ashby can do.

They do not define HOW actions are executed.
They do not define truth.
They do not define state authority.

Modules exist to describe capability
within the bounds of Ashby’s core law.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHAT A MODULE IS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A module is a declarative unit of behavior.

It defines:
- available actions
- intent shapes
- policy constraints
- supported scope types
- domain-specific rules

A module describes WHAT is possible
without describing HOW it is achieved.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHAT MODULES MUST NEVER DO
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A module must never:
- execute actions
- speak to vendors
- define truth semantics
- own state authority
- bypass scope checks
- assume execution success

Any module that performs these functions
violates Ashby’s architecture.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
MODULES AND THE ENGINE
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Modules integrate with the engine
through explicit contracts.

The engine:
- loads module definitions
- reasons over module capabilities
- enforces policy and scope law

Modules:
- register capabilities
- expose intent patterns
- declare constraints

The engine remains generic.
Modules remain removable.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
MODULES AND SCOPES
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Modules are scope-aware by declaration.

A module must explicitly state:
- which scope types it supports
- what actions are allowed per scope
- what safety constraints apply

If a module cannot declare its scope compatibility,
it is incomplete.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
MODULES AND STATE
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Modules may define domain state models,
but do not own state authority.

State is:
- scoped
- written through the Action Gate
- governed by engine semantics

Modules may request state transitions.
They may not enforce them.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
MODULE LIFECYCLE
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Modules must support:
- installation
- activation
- deactivation
- removal

Removal must:
- not break the engine
- not affect other modules
- degrade state explicitly
- reflect loss of capability honestly

This is non-negotiable.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

If a capability requires:
- embedding logic in the engine
- bypassing Action-Gated Truth
- coupling to execution layers
- assuming global state

it does not belong in a module.

Modules define WHAT Ashby can do
so that HOW may evolve freely.

END OF 8.6


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 8.7
ADAPTERS, BRIDGES, NATIVE VENDORS (HOW)

Execution in Ashby is expressed through HOW layers.

These layers exist to touch the external world
without contaminating the engine,
truth semantics,
or scope law.

Adapters, bridges, and native vendors
are distinct execution roles,
not interchangeable concepts.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
THE ROLE OF HOW LAYERS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

HOW layers are responsible for:
- translating ActionRequests into external actions
- interacting with protocols and APIs
- handling retries, timeouts, and transport failures
- returning observable evidence

HOW layers never:
- reason about intent
- define policy
- assign confidence
- update state
- decide language

They execute.
They report.
They stop.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ADAPTERS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Adapters are the primary execution interface.

An adapter:
- accepts an ActionRequest
- executes against a specific capability
- returns an ActionResult
- exposes uncertainty explicitly

Adapters are:
- replaceable
- testable
- scope-aware
- vendor-specific

Adapters never:
- define truth
- collapse outcomes
- upgrade confidence
- speak to users

If an adapter must explain itself,
the architecture has failed.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
BRIDGES
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Bridges connect Ashby to external systems
that Ashby does not control.

Bridges exist to:
- enable incremental migration
- integrate legacy platforms
- reduce adoption friction

Bridges are always:
- optional
- removable
- temporary

A bridge must never:
- define behavior
- define state
- become required for operation

If a bridge becomes permanent,
native ownership must be evaluated.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
NATIVE VENDORS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Native vendor integrations represent
direct ownership of execution paths.

Native ownership is chosen when:
- verification requires deeper access
- vendors abstract away failure
- offline guarantees are required
- long-term stability demands control

Native integrations:
- still live outside the engine
- still obey WHAT vs HOW separation
- still return ActionResults
- never define truth

Native does not mean privileged.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SEPARATION AND DEPENDENCY
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Dependency direction is strict.

- Engine → abstract contracts
- Modules → capability declarations
- HOW layers → execution only

The engine must never import HOW layers.
HOW layers may depend on engine contracts.

This preserves replaceability.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
FAILURE AND EVIDENCE
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

All HOW layers must report:
- explicit success
- partial success
- explicit failure
- uncertainty

Silence is forbidden.

Evidence must be raw.
Interpretation belongs to the engine.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

No HOW layer may:
- bypass the Action Gate
- write to state
- upgrade confidence
- infer intent
- decide user-facing language

HOW touches reality.
The engine defines meaning.

This boundary is what allows Ashby
to scale execution
without fragmenting truth.

END OF 8.7


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 8.8
CONNECTORS (NON-PHYSICAL DOMAINS)

Connectors integrate Ashby with non-physical domains.

They exist to interface with systems
where execution does not directly touch the physical world,
but still requires truth, state, and accountability.

Connectors are execution layers.
They obey the same laws as all HOW components.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHAT A CONNECTOR IS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A connector is an integration interface
for abstract or digital domains.

Examples include:
- calendars
- messaging systems
- document stores
- knowledge bases
- task systems
- digital workflows

These examples are representative,
not exhaustive.

Connectors execute actions
whose effects are informational rather than physical.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
CONNECTORS VS ADAPTERS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Connectors and adapters share structure,
but differ in domain.

Adapters:
- interact with physical or hardware-backed systems
- deal with latency, partial actuation, and device failure

Connectors:
- interact with logical or informational systems
- deal with consistency, permissions, and abstraction

Despite this difference,
the same constraints apply.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TRUTH AND EVIDENCE IN CONNECTORS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Connectors must produce evidence.

Even in non-physical domains:
- actions may fail
- permissions may be denied
- systems may be unavailable
- outcomes may be partial

A connector must return ActionResults
that reflect observable outcomes,
not intent.

Success must be explicit.
Failure must be explicit.
Uncertainty must be explicit.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
CONNECTORS AND STATE
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Connector-driven state is still scoped.

A calendar entry exists within:
- an organizational scope
- a user scope
- a project scope

Connectors must never:
- write global state
- infer cross-scope effects
- bypass scope resolution

State authority remains with the engine.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
CONNECTORS AND SECURITY
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Connectors often interact with sensitive data.

They must:
- respect scope boundaries
- enforce permission constraints
- avoid data leakage
- report access failures explicitly

Security is not optional.

A connector that hides authorization failure
is unsafe.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
CONNECTORS AND REMOVABILITY
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Connectors are removable.

If a connector is removed:
- the engine remains intact
- related state degrades explicitly
- explanations reflect reduced capability

No connector may be required
for Ashby’s core operation.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Connectors must never:
- define truth
- own state authority
- bypass Action-Gated Truth
- collapse scope boundaries
- invent success

Non-physical domains
are still part of reality.

They are governed by the same law.

END OF 8.8


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 8.9
PACKAGING, PROFILES, AND PRODUCTS

Packaging defines how Ashby is delivered
without redefining what Ashby is.

Profiles define behavior.
Packaging defines distribution.
Products are composed expressions of both.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PACKAGING AS DISTRIBUTION
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Packaging determines:
- how Ashby is deployed
- how components are bundled
- how updates are delivered
- how environments are targeted

Packaging may vary by:
- hardware platform
- customer type
- deployment model
- regulatory environment

Packaging must never:
- alter engine law
- redefine truth semantics
- change scope behavior
- introduce hidden coupling

Packaging is operational,
not architectural.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PROFILES AS BEHAVIOR SELECTION
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Profiles select behavior.

A profile determines:
- which modules are enabled
- which agents are active
- which execution paths are allowed
- which automations are permitted
- which safety constraints apply

Profiles do not:
- fork code
- redefine the engine
- modify truth enforcement
- bypass governance

Profiles are configuration,
not divergence.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PRODUCTS AS COMPOSITION
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Products are composed from:
- the engine
- selected modules
- selected agents
- profiles
- packaging

A product is not a fork.
It is a constrained expression
of the same platform.

Examples include:
- a residential automation product
- an enterprise operations product
- a creator workflow product

These are representative,
not exhaustive.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHY THIS SEPARATION MATTERS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Without separation:
- product needs pollute the engine
- packaging hacks become law
- profiles become exceptions
- rebuildability is lost

Ashby enforces this separation
to remain a platform.

A product may be discontinued.
The platform must not be altered.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
UPDATES AND EVOLUTION
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Packaging may change frequently.
Profiles may evolve.
Products may come and go.

The engine must remain stable.

If a change requires modifying the engine
to support a product,
that change must be evaluated via Control Sync.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Packaging must never:
- embed domain logic
- bypass removability
- hide execution assumptions

Profiles must never:
- weaken safety constraints
- redefine scope law
- introduce special cases

Products must never:
- redefine the platform
- hardcode behavior into the engine

This separation allows Ashby
to ship broadly
without losing itself.

END OF 8.9


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 8.10
PLUGINS AND EXTENSION ECOSYSTEM

Plugins extend Ashby without altering Ashby.

They exist to allow experimentation,
third-party contribution,
and future growth
without compromising core law.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHAT A PLUGIN IS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A plugin is an extension unit
that augments Ashby’s capabilities
without modifying the engine or core modules.

Plugins may:
- introduce new modules
- add new agents
- add new connectors or adapters
- provide tooling or visualization

Plugins must conform
to existing contracts and laws.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PLUGINS VS CORE COMPONENTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Plugins are never core.

They are:
- optional
- removable
- sandboxed
- versioned independently

If removing a plugin
changes Ashby’s identity,
the plugin has violated architectural boundaries.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PLUGIN ISOLATION
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Plugins must be isolated.

They must not:
- write directly to HouseState
- bypass Action-Gated Truth
- modify engine internals
- override scope law
- introduce global assumptions

Isolation is enforced structurally,
not by convention.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PLUGIN CONTRACTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Plugins interact with Ashby
through explicit contracts.

These contracts define:
- allowed inputs
- allowed outputs
- scope compatibility
- execution boundaries
- failure semantics

A plugin that cannot express itself
through existing contracts
is incompatible.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
GOVERNANCE OF PLUGINS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Plugins do not bypass governance.

If a plugin requires:
- new engine behavior
- new truth semantics
- new scope rules

a Control Sync is required.

Plugins may expose new capabilities,
but they may not redefine law.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PLUGIN FAILURE HANDLING
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Plugin failure must be explicit.

Failure must:
- degrade capability honestly
- not affect unrelated subsystems
- not corrupt state
- not alter truth semantics

A failing plugin must never
cause Ashby to lie.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Plugins must never:
- redefine Ashby
- weaken safety guarantees
- obscure accountability
- hide uncertainty

Plugins exist so Ashby can grow
without losing its spine.

END OF 8.10


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 8.11
SETUP, PROVISIONING, AND ONBOARDING

Setup and provisioning define how Ashby enters an environment
without redefining Ashby’s architecture.

Onboarding is not a shortcut around law.
It is a controlled process that introduces Ashby
to a specific reality.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SETUP AS STRUCTURAL INITIALIZATION
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Setup establishes:
- initial scope hierarchy
- authority boundaries
- core configuration
- enabled profiles
- security context

Setup does not:
- embed assumptions
- hardcode environment-specific logic
- bypass governance
- weaken safety constraints

If setup requires modifying the engine,
the design is invalid.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PROVISIONING AS CONTROLLED EXPANSION
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Provisioning adds capabilities
to an already-defined structure.

Provisioning may:
- register new scopes
- associate devices or resources
- enable modules or connectors
- configure adapters
- assign permissions

Provisioning must:
- respect existing scope law
- record changes explicitly
- validate authority
- refuse unsafe configurations

Provisioning is additive,
not transformative.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ONBOARDING AND HUMAN INTERACTION
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Onboarding introduces humans
to Ashby’s model of reality.

It must:
- make scope boundaries visible
- explain authority and limits
- surface uncertainty
- avoid false confidence
- prevent accidental overreach

Onboarding is a safety interface,
not a marketing flow.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
FAILURE AND RECOVERY DURING SETUP
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Failures during setup must be explicit.

Ashby must:
- report incomplete configuration
- mark affected scopes as uncertain
- refuse unsafe automation
- allow recovery without reset

Silent partial setup is forbidden.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
REPEATABILITY AND REBUILD
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Setup and provisioning must be repeatable.

A system must be reconstructable
from:
- configuration
- recorded state
- explicit provisioning steps

This supports:
- rebuild after loss
- migration
- audit
- controlled replication

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Setup, provisioning, and onboarding must never:
- define truth
- bypass Action-Gated Truth
- introduce hidden state
- create global assumptions
- weaken scope enforcement

They introduce Ashby to the world.
They do not redefine it.

END OF 8.11


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 8.12
SUPPORT, OBSERVABILITY, AND LONGEVITY

Support and observability exist to ensure Ashby remains
understandable, debuggable, and trustworthy over time.

Longevity is not accidental.
It is engineered.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SUPPORT AS STRUCTURAL CLARITY
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Support in Ashby is not reactive firefighting.

It is enabled by:
- explicit state
- recorded history
- traceable decisions
- scoped authority
- explainable failures

A system that cannot explain itself
cannot be supported safely.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
OBSERVABILITY AS FIRST-CLASS REQUIREMENT
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Observability is mandatory.

Ashby must expose:
- what actions were attempted
- what execution paths were used
- what succeeded or failed
- what evidence exists
- what is currently uncertain
- what confidence has decayed

Logs are not enough.
Signals must be meaningful.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
OBSERVABILITY WITHOUT LYING
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Observability must reflect truth.

It must never:
- smooth failure
- collapse uncertainty
- infer success
- reconstruct narrative

Metrics that hide reality
are worse than no metrics.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LONGEVITY THROUGH DISCIPLINE
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Longevity emerges from discipline.

Ashby survives time by:
- resisting convenience
- enforcing structure
- preventing silent drift
- governing change explicitly
- preserving rebuildability

Support tooling may change.
Observability tooling may evolve.
The underlying guarantees must not.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INCIDENTS AND POST-MORTEMS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

When Ashby fails,
failure must be analyzable.

Post-mortems must be able to answer:
- what happened
- where it happened
- why it happened
- what evidence exists
- what assumptions failed

If these questions cannot be answered,
the architecture is insufficient.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EVOLUTION WITHOUT EROSION
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

As Ashby evolves:
- new tooling may be added
- new diagnostics may appear
- new interfaces may exist

None may:
- bypass truth enforcement
- obscure state authority
- weaken scope law
- hide uncertainty

Longevity is preserved
by refusing erosion.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Support and observability must never:
- define behavior
- invent certainty
- bypass Action-Gated Truth
- replace explanation with metrics

They exist to reveal Ashby,
not to reshape it.

This is how Ashby remains operable
years after its creation.

END OF 8.12


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 8A.1
engine/

This section is not a file listing.
It is a zoning law for the most sacred directory in Ashby.

engine/ is the body.

Everything that makes Ashby Ashby lives here:
- routing discipline
- policy outcomes
- Truth Gate enforcement
- HouseState authority
- session and confirmation law
- execution orchestration (as orchestration, not vendor logic)
- observability and accountability hooks

engine/ must remain stable.
engine/ must remain readable.
engine/ must remain vendor-neutral.

If engine/ becomes “where we put logic,” Ashby dies by monolith.
If engine/ becomes “where we integrate vendors,” Ashby dies by dependency capture.
If engine/ becomes “where we make exceptions,” Ashby dies by drift.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1) PURPOSE OF engine/
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

engine/ exists to enforce platform law mechanically.

Its purpose is to ensure that:
- intent does not become action without permission
- language does not become truth without evidence
- state does not become fantasy without decay and attribution
- modules cannot bypass invariants
- vendors cannot rewrite meaning
- products cannot fork the constitution

engine/ is the place where the platform protects itself from its own growth.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2) WHAT MUST LIVE IN engine/ (OWNED RESPONSIBILITIES)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

engine/ owns the following responsibilities:

A) INPUT NORMALIZATION (PRE-MODULE)
- normalize user input into a structured intent representation
- manage conversation/session context as a control artifact, not as “memory truth”
- anchor contextual phrases through scoped context rules

B) ROUTING (THIN ORCHESTRATION)
- select eligible modules based on:
  - intent match
  - profile/entitlement availability
  - scope capability maps
- route to modules for planning only
- never route “because it feels right”
- log routing decisions

C) POLICY AND OUTCOME SELECTION
- decide ACT / CLARIFY / CONFIRM / REFUSE / CHAT
- enforce confirmation for high-impact actions
- enforce refusal when capability is unavailable or unsafe
- enforce “no guessing” posture where ambiguity exists

D) PLANNING ORCHESTRATION (NOT DOMAIN PLANNING)
- accept module PlanCandidates
- assemble multi-step execution flows when required by platform mechanics
  (e.g., verification readback step, retry policies, timeouts)
- ensure the plan remains within the WHAT/HOW boundary

E) EXECUTION ORCHESTRATION (HOW SEAM, NOT VENDOR LOGIC)
- transform ActionRequests into execution calls through:
  - adapters/
  - providers/
- manage retries, timeouts, and correlation IDs
- capture ActionResults as evidence artifacts
- ensure execution is attributable (which provider did what)

F) TRUTH GATE ENFORCEMENT
- classify evidence into truth classes
- constrain language posture in responses
- prevent “verification inflation”
- preserve UNKNOWN where evidence is absent

G) HOUSESTATE AUTHORITY
- persist beliefs with scope and staleness
- apply decay rules
- represent mixed reality (partial success, contradictions)
- ensure state updates are derived from evidence, not narrative

H) OBSERVABILITY AND AUDITABILITY
- structured logs at each gate:
  input → intent → policy → plan → execute → evidence → truth → state → response
- correlation_id propagation everywhere
- sanitization rules for secrets and sensitive payloads

I) GOVERNANCE ENFORCEMENT HOOKS
- mechanisms that make Control Sync meaningful:
  - tests that fail when invariants are violated
  - explicit classification points where constitutional changes are detectable

engine/ is the system of laws and enforcement.
It is not the system of features.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
3) WHAT MUST NEVER LIVE IN engine/ (FORBIDDEN CONTENT)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

engine/ must not contain:

A) DOMAIN-SPECIFIC MEANING
- “lighting logic,” “inventory logic,” “meeting logic,” etc.
That belongs in modules/.

B) VENDOR SDKS OR PROTOCOL CLIENTS
- Home Assistant clients
- Zigbee coordinators
- Matter controllers
- vendor-specific APIs
These belong in providers/ and/or bridges/ and/or native_vendor/.

C) PRODUCT-SPECIFIC BRANCHING
- “enterprise behavior”
- “home version behavior”
- “premium tier behavior”
Productization is profiles/flags/entitlements, not core branching.

D) SECRETS OR CREDENTIAL MATERIAL
engine/ must never store raw secrets.
Secret handling is a separate system and accessed through handles.

E) UI / FRONTEND / PRESENTATION
engine/ does not do “pretty.”
It enforces correctness.

F) “ONE-OFF EXCEPTIONS”
If a decision cannot be expressed as a policy rule, schema rule, or test,
it does not belong in engine/.
Exceptions are how constitutions rot.

If any of these appear in engine/, it is a structural violation.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
4) SUBSTRUCTURE INSIDE engine/ (CANONICAL ZONES)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

engine/ may contain subpackages that map to the platform control loop.

These are canonical zones (names may vary, responsibilities may not):

A) router/
- discovery of eligible modules
- dispatch of intents to modules
- orchestration logic only (thin)

B) cognition/intent/
- NLU management (local + optional cloud)
- intent schema definitions
- normalization and parsing
- never execution, never vendor assumptions

C) cognition/policy/
- action policy (ACT/CLARIFY/CONFIRM/REFUSE/CHAT)
- confirmation policy
- refusal policy
- response style policy (but constrained by truth law)

D) planning/
- assembly of execution steps
- verification readback planning
- timeouts/retry policies as platform mechanics
- never domain logic

E) truth/
- Truth Gate implementation
- evidence evaluation rules
- language constraint library (forbidden phrases, certainty gating)

F) state/
- HouseState interfaces and persistence wrappers
- state update application rules
- staleness/decay evaluation

G) session/
- session lifecycle and expiry
- pending clarification artifacts and merge-complete control

H) observability/
- logging primitives
- correlation tools
- sanitization tooling

The names are less important than the boundaries.
The boundaries are sacred.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
5) DEPENDENCY DIRECTION (THE ENGINE’S IMPORT LAW)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

engine/ may import:
- schemas and shared contracts
- domain models (abstract, vendor-neutral)
- safe utilities (pure logic)
- storage interfaces (not vendor-specific implementations)

engine/ must not import:
- modules/ (engine must not depend on optional organs)
- providers/ (engine orchestrates via interfaces, not concrete vendor code)
- bridges/ (engine does not speak vendor/protocol dialects)
- native_vendor/ (same reason)
- connectors/ (connectors are domain extensions, not core law)

If engine/ depends on optional components, removability is broken.
If engine/ depends on vendor code, vendor neutrality is broken.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
6) ENGINE CHANGES AND GOVERNANCE (WHAT NEEDS CONTROL SYNC)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Not every code change in engine/ needs Control Sync.
But changes that alter platform meaning do.

Control Sync is required when a change modifies:
- Truth Gate semantics or language posture rules
- HouseState semantics, decay, or authority boundaries
- policy outcome selection rules (ACT/CLARIFY/CONFIRM/REFUSE/CHAT)
- scope and identity law enforcement
- WHAT/HOW boundary enforcement mechanisms

Implementation improvements (refactor, optimization) do not require Control Sync
as long as invariants remain identical and tests enforce equivalence.

engine/ must be the hardest place to “accidentally change meaning.”

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
7) REQUIRED TEST SURFACES FOR engine/
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

engine/ must be protected by tests that fail loudly when law is violated:

- boundary tests (forbidden imports)
- Truth Gate posture tests (no certainty inflation)
- HouseState persistence/decay tests
- policy outcome tests (risk → confirmation)
- routing tests (availability gates, scope capability maps)
- offline behavior tests (honest degradation)
- observability tests (correlation_id everywhere, sanitization)

If tests do not enforce the law, engine/ will drift under pressure.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

engine/ must:
- embody platform law and enforcement
- remain vendor-neutral
- remain product-neutral
- remain readable and stable
- enforce WHAT/HOW separation mechanically
- enforce Truth Gate and HouseState authority
- remain independent of optional modules (removability)

engine/ must never:
- contain domain feature logic
- contain vendor/protocol SDKs
- store or expose secrets
- become a dumping ground for exceptions
- silently change constitutional semantics without governance

engine/ is the body.
Keep it sacred.

END OF 8A.1


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 8A.2
scopes/

scopes/ is the universal zone system of Ashby.

Scopes are not “labels.”
Scopes are not “tags.”
Scopes are not “folders.”

Scopes are the primary mechanism by which Ashby:
- binds meaning to place / context
- binds authority to boundary
- binds state to a coherent world model
- resolves contextual phrases safely (“in here,” “upstairs,” “the office”)

If scopes/ is sloppy, Ashby becomes:
- guessy under natural language
- unsafe under ambiguity
- and fragmented under multi-environment growth

scopes/ must be Ashby-owned and vendor-neutral.
No vendor is allowed to define Ashby’s scope reality.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1) PURPOSE OF scopes/
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

scopes/ exists to provide:

A) CONTEXT ANCHORING
When the user says:
- “in here”
- “this room”
- “upstairs”
- “the warehouse”
Ashby must resolve those phrases into a scope anchor using explicit rules.

B) AUTHORITY BOUNDARIES
Scopes are where permissions live.
A user may have authority in one scope and not another.

C) STATE PARTITIONING
HouseState beliefs must be scoped.
A belief without a scope is a leak across realities.

D) CAPABILITY MAPS
What Ashby can do is scope-dependent.
A scope defines which modules/capabilities are enabled.

Scopes turn “natural language” into “bounded execution.”
That is why they are core.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2) WHAT MUST LIVE IN scopes/ (OWNED RESPONSIBILITIES)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

scopes/ owns:

A) SCOPE GRAPH (THE WORLD SHAPE)
- parent/child relationships
- containment rules
- identity of each scope (scope_id / scope_key)
- invariants that prevent cycles and contradictions

B) ALIAS AND NAME RESOLUTION
- stable naming
- alias mapping (“living room” vs “lounge”)
- user-specific aliases (optional)
- normalization rules (case, punctuation, common synonyms)

C) ACTIVE CONTEXT RESOLUTION
- rules for “current scope”
- rules for “most recent relevant scope”
- session-tethered context (time-bounded)
- safe fallback rules (CLARIFY if ambiguous)

D) CAPABILITY MAP (SCOPE → ENABLED SURFACES)
- which modules/capabilities are enabled in which scope
- how profiles and entitlements apply to scopes
- explicit prohibition of “implicit enablement”

E) SCOPE-BOUND IDENTITY BINDING (INTERFACE, NOT CANONICAL IDENTITY)
Scopes may maintain mappings like:
- which entities belong to which scope
- what “the kitchen light” means inside a scope
But canonical identity remains platform-owned (device_key), not vendor-owned.

scopes/ is the layer that makes “place” computable without guessing.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
3) WHAT MUST NEVER LIVE IN scopes/ (FORBIDDEN CONTENT)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

scopes/ must not contain:

A) VENDOR ENTITY MODELS AS AUTHORITY
Vendor entity objects may be referenced as evidence inputs,
but they must not become the scope graph.

B) DEVICE STATE AS TRUTH
Scopes are not a state cache.
HouseState stores beliefs; providers store their own raw states; scopes binds context.
Do not mix these roles.

C) SECRETS OR CREDENTIALS
Scopes do not own credentials.
A scope may reference a connector handle, but not store secrets.

D) PRODUCT FORK LOGIC
Scopes define capabilities, not behavior rewrites.
If scope changes meaning of truth/safety rules, that is a violation.

E) “MAGIC” HEURISTICS WITHOUT TESTS
If scope resolution depends on vibes or hidden heuristics,
it must be made explicit and tested, or it is not allowed.

scopes/ is foundational.
It must be conservative.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
4) CANONICAL ARTIFACTS INSIDE scopes/
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The canonical internal artifacts include (representative):

A) scope_graph.py (or equivalent)
- scope definition objects
- parent/child relationships
- validation (no cycles)
- serialization/deserialization
- migration hooks for graph changes

B) alias_resolver.py (or equivalent)
- alias tables
- normalization rules
- conflict handling (“two scopes share alias” → ambiguity set)

C) capability_map.py (or equivalent)
- map of enabled capability surfaces per scope
- integration with profiles/entitlements
- explicit “disabled” state

These files are examples.
The responsibilities are law.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
5) HOW SCOPES INTERACT WITH THE REST OF ASHBY
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A) WITH HOUSESTATE
Every belief must be scoped.
HouseState must not “float.”
Scopes provide the partition key and containment semantics.

B) WITH MODULES
Modules ask questions like:
- “which room?”
- “do you mean the one in here?”
Scopes provide:
- active scope anchors
- ambiguity sets when aliases collide
- capability availability context

Modules must not invent scope resolution.
They must request it.

C) WITH POLICY
Policy decisions depend on scope because:
- risk differs by environment
- permissions differ
- capability availability differs

D) WITH TARGET RESOLUTION
Target resolution uses scopes to narrow search spaces.
If scope cannot narrow to a unique target, Ashby must CLARIFY.

E) WITH PROFILES AND PRODUCTIZATION
Profiles may enable different module sets.
Scopes apply those sets in a bounded way.
Profiles do not change scope law.

scopes/ is a core dependency for safe natural language action.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
6) CONTEXTUAL PHRASES AND SCOPES (LINK TO 15.2)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Contextual phrases (“in here,” “this room,” “upstairs”) are only safe when:

- there is an active scope anchor, AND
- the anchor is within a valid session window, AND
- the phrase resolves to a unique scope or yields an explicit ambiguity set.

If any condition fails:
- Ashby must ask a clarifying question
- Ashby must not guess

This is why scopes/ must provide:
- explicit context anchoring rules
- explicit expiry behavior
- explicit ambiguity exposure (not hidden tie-breaks)

The system must prefer “ask” over “assume.”

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
7) SCOPE CHANGES (RENAME, MOVE, MERGE, SPLIT) ARE MIGRATIONS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Scopes will change over time:
- rooms are renamed
- businesses restructure
- warehouses split
- environments are cloned

These changes affect:
- identity mapping
- capability maps
- HouseState partitioning
- historical interpretability

Therefore scope-graph edits must be treated as migrations.

Rules:
- scope_id (or scope_key) must remain stable unless the scope is truly replaced
- renames must preserve identity continuity
- merges/splits must be explicit, auditable operations
- structural realignment playbooks apply when scope changes would re-partition state

Scopes are not “just strings.”
They are the backbone of meaning.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
8) REQUIRED TEST SURFACES FOR scopes/
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Scopes must be protected by tests that enforce conservatism:

- graph validity tests (no cycles, no orphan contradictions)
- alias collision tests (ambiguity surfaced, not hidden)
- context phrase resolution tests (15.2 compliance)
- session expiry behavior tests (no stale “in here” anchors)
- capability map gating tests (disabled means unroutable)
- permission boundary tests (scope authority enforced)

If scope resolution is not test-protected, drift is guaranteed.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

scopes/ must:
- be Ashby-owned, vendor-neutral
- provide explicit scope graph, alias resolution, and capability mapping
- enable safe contextual phrase grounding without guessing
- enforce authority boundaries by scope
- enable HouseState partitioning and interpretability
- surface ambiguity sets rather than hiding tie-breaks
- treat scope-graph changes as migrations

scopes/ must never:
- let vendor models become the scope truth
- become a device state cache or truth source
- store secrets or credentials
- encode product forks
- rely on untested “magic” heuristics for resolution

scopes/ is how Ashby turns language into safe reality control.

END OF 8A.2


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 8A.3
domain/

domain/ is the contract of reality.

It defines what exists in Ashby’s world in a form that is:
- stable across time
- stable across integrations
- stable across products
- portable across deployments
- legible to builders who have never seen the codebase

If engine/ is the body, domain/ is the skeleton.

This directory is not where “features” live.
This directory is where Ashby’s vocabulary lives.

If the vocabulary drifts, everything drifts.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1) PURPOSE OF domain/
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

domain/ exists to answer one question:

“What are the things Ashby can talk about and act upon?”

It is the place where Ashby defines:
- what an Entity is (internal identity + metadata)
- what a Device is (physical or logical host of entities)
- what a Capability is (what can be done)
- what a State is (observable condition or output)
- what a Job is (background/async work, durable or scheduled)

domain/ is required so that:
- modules can reason without depending on vendor shapes
- adapters can execute without inventing internal meaning
- scopes can organize reality without inventing object definitions
- Truth Gate enforcement has unambiguous types to verify against

domain/ makes Ashby rebuildable because it is the minimal “ontology” a rebuild requires.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2) ABSOLUTE BOUNDARY LAW
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

domain/ is the “Abstract Reality” layer.

It describes what exists without caring how it is controlled.

That means:

domain/ may describe:
- the existence of a light entity
- the notion of brightness state
- the idea that a capability called “lights” exists
- the mapping from a vendor entity id to an internal id (as data)

domain/ must never describe:
- how to call a vendor API
- how to authenticate
- how to speak HTTP
- how to use an SDK
- how to perform an action

domain/ is not allowed to do IO.

If a file in domain/ performs network calls, reads secrets, or “reaches out” to the world,
the architecture is violated.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
3) WHAT BELONGS IN domain/
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

domain/ contains three kinds of objects:

A) Models
The canonical datatypes the rest of Ashby shares.

B) Registries
The authoritative mapping layer between:
- internal identity
- external identity
- metadata needed for resolution and verification

C) Resolution
Deterministic resolution algorithms that convert ambiguous references into canonical targets.

Resolution is allowed here only if it remains:
- deterministic
- vendor-agnostic
- IO-free
- truth-neutral (it proposes candidates; it does not “declare reality”)

If resolution needs policy, it returns options.
It does not enforce policy.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
4) WHAT IS FORBIDDEN IN domain/
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Forbidden in domain/ includes (non-exhaustive):
- HTTP clients
- OAuth clients
- SDK imports for vendors
- any “bridge” or “native vendor” code
- secrets handling
- filesystem persistence as the source of truth
- user-facing language construction
- any attempt to “interpret intent”
- any attempt to “plan execution”

domain/ is allowed to be strict.
domain/ is not allowed to be clever.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
5) CANONICAL SUBTREE (MINIMUM EXPECTED SHAPE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The canonical minimum layout for domain/ is:

domain/
├── models/
│   ├── scope.py
│   ├── entity.py
│   ├── device.py
│   ├── capability.py
│   ├── state.py
│   └── job.py
│
├── registry/
│   ├── entity_registry.py
│   ├── external_id_map.py
│   └── asset_registry.py
│
└── resolution/
    ├── target_resolver.py
    ├── adapter_resolver.py
    └── capability_matcher.py

This subtree is not “everything domain will ever contain.”
It is the minimum skeleton that prevents drift.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
6) SUBFOLDER LAW
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

6.1 models/

models/ defines the canonical types.

Rules:
- types must be serializable
- fields must be explicit (no hidden magic)
- default values must be safe
- validation must exist where ambiguity would corrupt truth enforcement

models/ must be stable.
Changing a model is a platform event.

If a model field changes, every downstream layer changes:
- HouseState persistence
- adapter verification output
- module planning assumptions
- migrations

Model changes are Control Sync territory.

6.2 registry/

registry/ is where Ashby remembers what reality contains.

It is the authoritative mapping for:
- internal IDs (“Ashby IDs”)
- external IDs (HA entity_id, vendor ids, connector ids)
- metadata needed for safe resolution and safe execution

registry/ is allowed to store identifiers and metadata.
registry/ is not allowed to store credentials.
registry/ is not allowed to store secrets.

The external id map is allowed to mention external systems by name as data,
but it must not import their code.

Example of allowed registry truth:
- internal_entity_id: “entity_83…”
- external_system: “home_assistant”
- external_id: “light.kitchen_counter”

Example of forbidden registry behavior:
- making an API call to fetch current state
- deciding that an external state is “true”
- deciding that an action succeeded

6.3 resolution/

resolution/ exists because humans speak in ambiguity.

“Upstairs lights” is not an ID.
“Kitchen” is not an entity.
“Turn it down” is not a target.

Resolution converts:
- vague references
- scope-relative references
- grouped references
into canonical internal targets and candidate capability pathways.

Rules:
- resolution must be deterministic for the same inputs
- resolution must attribute uncertainty (multiple candidates allowed)
- resolution must never fabricate entities
- resolution must never claim that a target exists without registry evidence
- resolution must never call adapters or vendors

resolution is allowed to output:
- a list of candidate targets (internal IDs)
- an ambiguity object describing what is missing
- a suggested adapter pathway (bridge vs native) as a recommendation, not an action

resolution is not allowed to output:
- a completed ActionResult
- a VERIFIED claim
- a response sentence

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
7) DEPENDENCY DIRECTION (THE SAFE GRAPH)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

domain/ should be near the bottom of the dependency graph.

Preferred direction:
- engine/ depends on domain/
- scopes/ depends on domain/
- modules/ depend on domain/
- adapters/ depend on domain/

Forbidden direction:
- domain/ depends on modules/
- domain/ depends on adapters/
- domain/ depends on bridges/ or native_vendor/
- domain/ depends on connectors/
- domain/ depends on packaging/

If a dependency cycle forms around domain/,
the platform becomes untestable and unrebuildable.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
8) FAILURE MODES (WHAT RUINS domain/)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A) Vendor leakage
When domain models start matching a vendor’s shape instead of Ashby’s reality.

Symptom:
- a model field exists “because Home Assistant uses it”
- a field name matches vendor naming instead of internal semantics

B) IO creep
When domain code performs network calls “just for convenience.”

This destroys determinism and destroys testability.

C) Truth inflation
When domain starts “declaring reality” instead of describing what is known.

Truth belongs to:
- ActionResult verification
- HouseState confidence and decay
- Truth Gate enforcement

D) Model churn
When core types change weekly.

If types churn, rebuilding Ashby becomes archaeology.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
9) MINIMUM ACCEPTANCE CHECKLIST
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A builder should be able to validate domain/ with these checks:

- No network imports in domain/
- No secrets imports in domain/
- Deterministic resolution tests (same input → same output)
- Registry contains only identifiers + metadata (no credentials)
- Models are explicit, serializable, and validated
- No references to specific adapters or vendor implementations (only abstract names/data)
- No user-facing language construction

If these pass, domain/ is doing its job:
it defines reality without executing it.

END OF 8A.3


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 8A.4
modules/

modules/ is the “WHAT” layer.

It is where Ashby’s features live.

A module is not infrastructure.
A module is a removable feature bundle that:
- reasons about a domain
- plans actions
- registers tools
- composes workflows
- expresses intent in canonical shapes

If the platform is rebuilt, modules/ is where the builder rebuilds behavior.

If modules/ becomes non-removable, Ashby stops being a platform and becomes a monolith.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1) PURPOSE OF modules/
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

modules/ exists to ensure that “what Ashby can do” is:
- explicit
- auditable
- removable
- growable without refactors
- isolated from engine/ sanctity

engine/ must remain generic.
modules/ is where features are allowed to expand.

This separation prevents:
- engine bloat
- dependency capture
- feature lock-in
- “one more exception” collapse

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2) THE MODULE CONTRACT (WHAT MAKES SOMETHING A MODULE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A valid module:

A) Declares its domain
The module states what capability or behavior it owns (lights, comfort, cameras, inventory, meetings).

B) Exposes tools
A module registers callable tools in a stable schema so the router can invoke it safely.

C) Produces ActionRequests, not miracles
A module may plan and decide, but execution occurs through adapters/connectors.

D) Accepts removability
If the module folder is removed or disabled:
- Ashby still boots
- other modules still work
- the engine still enforces truth
- packaging profiles remain coherent

If removing a module breaks Ashby, the architecture is violated.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
3) WHAT BELONGS IN modules/
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

modules/ can contain:
- planning logic
- feature workflows
- domain-specific heuristics (inside the module, not in engine/)
- tool registration scaffolding
- optional heavy compute pipelines (ASR, diarization, vision, rendering)
- module-specific configuration schemas
- module-scoped helper logic for invoking adapters or connectors

modules/ is where intelligence is allowed to be “feature-shaped.”

This means heavy pipelines belong here.
If heavy pipelines live in engine/, the engine becomes non-removable by gravity.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
4) WHAT IS FORBIDDEN IN modules/
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

modules/ must never become execution infrastructure.

Forbidden includes:
- vendor drivers
- vendor SDK usage directly inside modules/
- direct OAuth/HTTP calls to external systems “because it’s easy”
- secrets handling
- truth authority (“I did it” without ActionResult evidence)
- direct mutation of HouseState as if the module is the authority

Modules may compute.
Modules may plan.
Modules may not declare truth.

Truth is action-gated.

The execution boundary is strict:
- Modules call adapters/ and connectors/
- Adapters/connectors talk to vendors and APIs

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
5) CANONICAL MODULE LAYOUT (REPRESENTATIVE SHAPE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A module should look boring on purpose.

The architecture vision includes representative examples like:

modules/
├── lights/
│   ├── handler.py
│   ├── service.py
│   ├── tools.py
│   └── schemas.py
│
├── comfort/
│   ├── handler.py
│   └── service.py
│
├── cameras/
│   ├── handler.py
│   ├── service.py
│   └── tools.py
│
├── image_gen/
│   ├── handler.py
│   ├── service.py
│   ├── tools.py
│   ├── schemas.py
│   └── workflows/
│       ├── flux_default.json
│       └── workflow_2.json
│
├── stuart_meetings/
│   ├── handler.py
│   ├── service.py
│   ├── tools.py
│   ├── schemas.py
│   ├── pipelines/
│   │   ├── recorder.py
│   │   ├── diarization.py
│   │   ├── transcription.py
│   │   ├── summarization.py
│   │   └── search_index.py
│   └── models/
│       ├── (placeholder)_whisper/
│       ├── (placeholder)_pyannote/
│       └── (placeholder)_model_3/
│
├── tori_inventory/
│   ├── handler.py
│   ├── service.py
│   ├── tools.py
│   ├── schemas.py
│   └── connectors/
│       ├── shopify_helpers.py
│       └── quickbooks_helpers.py
│
└── (placeholder)_module_4/
    ├── handler.py
    └── service.py

This is not a mandatory file list.
It is a proven shape that prevents drift:
- handler.py is the boundary
- tools.py is the surface
- service.py is the body
- schemas.py is the safety rail
- pipelines/ and models/ keep heavy compute out of engine/

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
6) MODULE INTERNAL ROLES (WHY THESE FILES EXIST)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

handler.py
The boundary adapter between the router and the module.
It wires tool calls to internal service logic.

service.py
The core implementation.
This file is allowed to be large, because it is quarantined inside the module.

tools.py
The tool surface.
This is where the module declares:
- tool names
- arguments
- which actions are supported
- what ActionRequests it produces

schemas.py
The safety layer.
Schemas prevent ambiguity from becoming accidental behavior.
Schemas are how the module stays stable across time.

workflows/
Static or semi-static workflow definitions (rendering, pipelines, etc).
This keeps “workflow tuning” out of code and keeps execution reproducible.

pipelines/
Heavy compute orchestration.
If it can take seconds or minutes, it belongs here, not in engine/.

models/
Local model artifacts and registries.
This is not “memory.”
This is “compute assets.”

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
7) DEPENDENCY LAW FOR MODULES
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Modules may depend on:
- domain/ (canonical types)
- scopes/ (world model boundaries)
- engine/ interfaces (only through stable, narrow contracts)
- adapters/ (capability-level execution)
- connectors/ (non-physical execution systems)

Modules must not depend on:
- bridge internals
- native vendor internals
- another module’s private internals

Cross-module coupling is poison.

If two modules must coordinate, they do so through:
- shared domain types
- engine eventing
- explicit tool invocation
- a shared, declared interface

They do not import each other casually.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
8) REMOVABILITY GUARANTEES (WHAT MUST REMAIN TRUE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A removable module system implies:

- Module enablement is controlled by packaging/profiles (not by code edits)
- Module registration is discoverable
- The router can determine “module absent” and degrade gracefully
- No module is assumed by engine as “always present”
- Module data, if persisted, is migrated safely when the module is removed

If removing a module requires editing engine/, the architecture has failed.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
9) FAILURE MODES (WHAT RUINS modules/)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A) Vendor leakage
When modules start containing vendor calls.

This destroys WHAT vs HOW.
It makes removability fake.

B) Engine reach-in
When modules import engine internals and start relying on private state.
