- safe deletes (and safe handling of referenced artifacts)

D) EXPORT SAFETY
Exports must:
- never include secrets
- include only permitted scopes and users
- include manifests so the export is interpretable later

If retention is undefined, storage becomes a liability.
If redaction is sloppy, storage becomes a leak.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
7) ACTIONRESULT STORAGE (EVIDENCE BUNDLES)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

ActionResults are the most important artifacts because they are evidence.

storage/ must support a durable way to store ActionResult bundles that include:
- the ActionRequest (WHAT) reference
- provider/adapter attribution (HOW)
- timestamps
- raw provider response references (sanitized)
- verification readback results (if any)
- error classifications (if failures)
- correlation_id
- scope_id
- any captured “device observed state” snapshots used for Truth Gate evaluation

Rules:
- evidence bundles must remain readable even if the module is removed
- evidence bundles must not contain secrets
- evidence bundles must be addressable by artifact_id so truth/state can cite them

If evidence cannot be recovered, truth becomes impossible to audit.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
8) INDEXES AND SEARCH (HELPERS, NOT TRUTH)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Indexes are allowed to speed retrieval:
- file indexes
- metadata search indexes
- vector indexes (optional)

But indexes must be treated as:
- caches
- helpers
- non-authoritative

The authoritative source is:
- the artifact itself + manifest
not the index.

Indexes must be rebuildable from storage content.
If an index is required to interpret artifacts, resurrection is broken.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
9) DEPENDENCY LAW
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

storage/ must be generic infrastructure.

It may depend on:
- common schemas
- encryption libraries
- filesystem/db primitives
- sanitization utilities

It must not depend on:
- modules/
- connectors/
- adapters/
- bridges/
- vendor SDKs

Those components may use storage/.
storage/ must not need them.

This preserves removability and prevents circular dependencies.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
10) REQUIRED TEST SURFACES FOR storage/
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

storage/ must be protected by tests because it protects resurrection.

Minimum test categories:

- artifact manifest integrity tests (required fields)
- reference durability tests (HouseState references remain valid)
- retention policy tests (purges happen correctly)
- redaction tests (no secrets in artifacts/logs/exports)
- export tests (manifests included; scope/user limits respected)
- ActionResult bundle tests (evidence completeness and readability)
- offline survivability tests (local-first works without cloud)
- migration tests (storage layout changes are migratable)

If storage is not test-protected, it will become a junk pile.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

storage/ must:
- store artifacts and blobs separately from HouseState beliefs
- use stable artifact IDs and manifests for interpretability
- remain local-first with optional policy-governed remote backup
- enforce retention and redaction as law
- store ActionResult evidence bundles durably and safely
- keep indexes rebuildable and non-authoritative
- remain independent infrastructure (no module/vendor dependencies)
- support exports that preserve meaning without leaking secrets

storage/ must never:
- become HouseState (no belief truth stored as blobs)
- become a surveillance hoard by default
- leak secrets or PII through artifacts or exports
- require cloud to function
- hide blobs without manifests
- tie artifact readability to the presence of any specific module

storage/ is where Ashby keeps its history without losing its mind.

END OF 8A.12


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 8A.13
packaging/

packaging/ is how Ashby becomes a deliverable platform.

A system that only exists as “source code in a repo” is not resurrectable by strangers.
A platform must be packageable, installable, updatable, and verifiable.

packaging/ defines:
- build shapes
- distribution artifacts
- environment separation (dev/staging/prod)
- versioning discipline
- dependencies pinning
- reproducibility guarantees

If packaging is sloppy:
- environments drift
- upgrades break silently
- security posture decays
- and resurrection becomes impossible because nobody can recreate the same system.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1) PURPOSE OF packaging/
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

packaging/ exists to guarantee:

A) REPRODUCIBLE BUILDS
The same inputs produce the same outputs.

B) CONSISTENT RUNTIME SHAPES
Ashby can run as:
- local daemon
- appliance image
- container
- hybrid (local core + optional cloud services)

C) VERSIONED DELIVERY
Every build has:
- version
- commit hash
- dependency manifest
- migration compatibility metadata

D) SAFE UPDATES
Updates can be rolled out, rolled back, and audited.

packaging/ is the bridge between the platform and reality.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2) CANONICAL packaging/ DIRECTORY LAYOUT (THE TEMPLATE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

packaging/
├── build.py                                 # build orchestration
├── versioning.py                            # version rules
├── environments/                            # dev/stage/prod manifests
│   ├── dev.env
│   ├── stage.env
│   └── prod.env
│
├── dependencies/
│   ├── requirements.lock                    # pinned dependencies
│   ├── hashes.txt                           # integrity (optional)
│   └── licenses/                            # compliance
│
├── images/                                  # deployable artifacts
│   ├── docker/
│   │   ├── Dockerfile
│   │   └── compose.yml
│   └── appliance/
│       ├── image_builder.py
│       └── filesystem_layout.json
│
└── release/
    ├── manifest.json                        # release metadata (version, migrations, notes)
    ├── sign.py                              # artifact signing (optional but preferred)
    └── publish.py                           # release publishing (local or remote)

This is the canonical template.
Specific tooling can vary, but these responsibilities must exist.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
3) VERSIONING LAW (WHAT A VERSION MEANS)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Ashby must use meaningful versioning.

At minimum:

- PATCH: bug fixes, no schema/semantic changes
- MINOR: new capability surfaces, backward compatible
- MAJOR: breaking changes requiring migrations or behavioral changes

But beyond semantic versioning, Ashby must record:

A) PLATFORM VERSION (CORE LAW VERSION)
This corresponds to:
- engine truth semantics
- HouseState semantics
- policy outcome rules
- interface contracts baseline

B) MODULE SET VERSION
Modules may be added/removed independently.
The build must record which module set is included/enabled.

C) MIGRATION COMPATIBILITY
A release must declare:
- which persisted schema versions it can read
- which migrations it can perform
- whether rollback is safe and under what conditions

“Version” is not a number.
It is a compatibility contract.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
4) REPRODUCIBLE BUILDS (THE NON-NEGOTIABLE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Reproducibility requires:

- pinned dependencies (lock files)
- pinned toolchains (compiler/interpreter versions)
- deterministic build steps
- artifact hashing

A build should produce a manifest that includes:
- build timestamp
- git commit hash
- dependency lock hash
- artifact digests
- platform version info
- included module list
- enabled profiles list (optional)
- migration requirements

If a build cannot be reproduced, it cannot be audited and it cannot be resurrected.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
5) ENVIRONMENT SEPARATION (DEV/STAGE/PROD)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Environments are not “folders.”

They are safety boundaries.

Rules:

A) CONFIG SEPARATION
- dev configuration must never silently leak into prod
- prod secrets must never exist in dev configs

B) FEATURE FLAGS AND EXPERIMENTS
Flags may exist, but:
- must be explicit
- must be observable
- must not create hidden forks

C) LOGGING DIFFERENCES
Dev may log more.
Prod must sanitize and respect privacy.
But truth posture and law must be identical.

D) DATA AND STATE BOUNDARIES
Prod HouseState and artifacts must not be used casually in dev.

Environment separation is how Ashby stays safe under iteration.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
6) SIGNING AND INTEGRITY (OPTIONAL BUT RECOMMENDED)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Packaging should support signing:
- release artifacts can be signed
- updates can verify signatures

This matters because:
- a resurrected system might be built by someone who needs provenance
- a compromised build chain must be detectable

If signing is not used, hashing must still exist to detect corruption.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
7) UPDATES AND ROLLBACK (LINK TO engine/updates/)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

packaging/ must coordinate with engine/updates/ so that:

- release manifests declare migrations
- update clients know whether rollback is safe
- schema changes are explicit
- identity continuity is preserved

Rules:
- never ship a release that changes persisted shapes without declaring it
- never ship a release that requires a migration without a migration plan
- never ship a release that cannot be rolled back without stating it plainly

An update system without packaging discipline is a corruption engine.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
8) DEPENDENCY LAW
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

packaging/ is infrastructure.
It should depend on:
- build tools
- manifests
- lock files
- signing libs

It must not depend on:
- modules/
- connectors/
- specific vendors

Packaging must not require the platform to be “running” to build.
That is circular and fragile.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
9) REQUIRED TEST SURFACES FOR packaging/
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Minimum test categories:

- build reproducibility tests (same input → same artifact hash)
- dependency lock tests (no unpinned deps)
- environment separation tests (no secret leakage)
- release manifest validation tests
- update/rollback simulation tests (dry-run migrations)
- container/appliance boot tests
- provenance tests (sign/verify if enabled)

If packaging is not test-protected, it will rot quietly.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

packaging/ must:
- produce reproducible builds with pinned dependencies
- generate release manifests that declare compatibility and migrations
- support environment separation (dev/stage/prod)
- support update and rollback discipline
- preserve auditability (hashes, provenance, included modules)
- avoid coupling to optional capability components

packaging/ must never:
- allow environment drift to become normal
- ship untracked dependency changes
- hide migrations
- embed secrets in build artifacts
- make resurrection depend on “the original build machine”

packaging/ is how Ashby becomes a real, repeatable platform.

END OF 8A.13


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 8A.14
support/

support/ is Ashby’s operational empathy layer.

Not emotional empathy.
Operational empathy:
the ability for the system to be debugged, repaired, and trusted by people
who are not the original builders.

support/ exists because:
- production always breaks
- users report symptoms, not root causes
- and resurrection requires the ability to diagnose without guessing

support/ is where Ashby becomes serviceable.

If support/ is missing:
- every issue becomes a mystery
- every outage becomes a rewrite
- and trust dies because nobody can explain what happened.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1) PURPOSE OF support/
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

support/ exists to provide:

A) DIAGNOSTIC TOOLS
- “what happened”
- “what is failing”
- “what does the system believe”
- “what evidence do we have”

B) SAFE RECOVERY FLOWS
- retry safely
- reset safely
- re-sync safely
- repair mappings safely

C) SUPPORT BUNDLES (SANITIZED)
- exportable diagnostics that contain:
  - correlation_id traces
  - evidence references (not secrets)
  - configuration summaries (redacted)
  - health checks
so others can help without leaking private data.

D) OPERATOR GUIDANCE
- playbooks
- common failure modes
- known-safe procedures

support/ is not the place where the system lies to look competent.
It is the place where the system tells the truth about its own condition.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2) CANONICAL support/ DIRECTORY LAYOUT (THE TEMPLATE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

support/
├── diagnostics/
│   ├── health_checks.py                    # service health, provider health, storage health
│   ├── dependency_report.py                # versions, enabled modules, profiles
│   └── truth_posture_report.py             # evidence vs claims sanity checks
│
├── bundles/
│   ├── build_bundle.py                     # generate sanitized diagnostic bundle
│   ├── redaction.py                        # ensure no secrets leak
│   └── manifest_schema.json                # bundle structure
│
├── repair/
│   ├── resync_housestate.py                # controlled re-syncs (policy-governed)
│   ├── remap_identity.py                   # device_key mapping repairs
│   └── clear_stale_sessions.py             # session cleanup
│
└── playbooks/
    ├── offline_mode.md
    ├── bridge_outage.md
    ├── connector_oauth_fail.md
    ├── identity_drift.md
    └── truth_gate_investigation.md

The exact file extensions can vary.
The responsibilities are law.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
3) SUPPORT IS A TRUST SYSTEM (LANGUAGE AND POSTURE RULES)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

support/ must obey the same truth posture rules as the assistant itself.

Rules:
- no fabricated causes
- no “it worked” claims without evidence
- no certainty inflation
- explicit “UNKNOWN” where evidence is missing

Support output must be evidence-linked:
- correlation_id
- ActionResult references
- staleness indicators
- provider attribution

Support is not allowed to be vague.

If support tools are vague, humans will guess.
Guessing is how invariants die.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
4) DIAGNOSTIC BUNDLES (THE SAFE EXPORT CONTRACT)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A diagnostic bundle must be:

A) SANITIZED
- no secrets
- no tokens
- no raw OAuth payloads
- no Wi-Fi credentials
- no unredacted PII beyond what the operator explicitly permits

B) INTERPRETABLE
- include a manifest describing what is included
- include versions and module list
- include recent correlation traces (bounded time window)
- include references to evidence artifacts (not raw sensitive blobs unless permitted)

C) BOUNDED
- bundles must be size-limited
- time-window-limited
- scope-limited
to avoid accidental data exfiltration.

D) AUDITABLE
- creation event logged (who requested it, which scope, what included)
- redaction report included

Bundles are how Ashby can be helped by outsiders without becoming a leak.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
5) REPAIR TOOLS (WHY THEY ARE DANGEROUS)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Repair tools can change reality (or belief about reality).
Therefore they must obey policy law.

Repair actions include:
- resyncing HouseState
- clearing mappings
- resetting connectors
- replaying events
- removing stale automation triggers
- clearing sessions

Rules:
- repair tools must require confirmation when impact is high
- repairs must be observable (correlation_id)
- repairs must produce ActionResults as evidence
- repairs must not silently rewrite HouseState as “truth”
- repairs must preserve history (don’t erase; mark and migrate)

Support must never become an unlogged admin backdoor.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
6) COMMON FAILURE MODE PLAYBOOKS (THE REQUIRED SET)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

At minimum, support/ must have playbooks for:

- offline mode / degraded mode (what Ashby can still do)
- bridge outage (how to verify and route around)
- connector OAuth failures (how to re-auth without leakage)
- identity drift (duplicate devices, remap rules)
- staleness and HouseState decay confusion (how to interpret beliefs)
- Truth Gate investigations (why Ashby said VERIFIED vs LAST_KNOWN vs UNKNOWN)
- automation misfires (eventing triggers, confirmation gates)

Playbooks must not be vague.
They must map symptoms → evidence sources → safe actions.

The point is to reduce human guessing.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
7) DEPENDENCY LAW
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

support/ may depend on:
- observability outputs (logs/traces)
- storage artifacts (evidence bundles)
- engine reports (health checks)
- schemas

support/ must not depend on:
- optional modules to function
Support must work even when modules are broken.
Otherwise support becomes useless.

support/ must never require cloud to diagnose local failures.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
8) REQUIRED TEST SURFACES FOR support/
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Minimum test categories:

- bundle redaction tests (secrets never included)
- bundle interpretability tests (manifest complete)
- health check tests (correct classification of failures)
- repair tool confirmation tests (high-impact requires confirmation)
- repair ActionResult evidence tests (no silent changes)
- offline support tests (diagnose without cloud)
- playbook completeness tests (required playbooks exist)

Support that is not test-protected becomes a liability.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

support/ must:
- provide evidence-linked diagnostics
- preserve truth posture honesty
- generate sanitized, bounded diagnostic bundles
- offer repair tools that obey policy/confirmation law
- preserve auditability and ActionResult evidence for repairs
- include explicit playbooks mapping symptoms to safe procedures
- work under degraded and offline conditions

support/ must never:
- leak secrets through bundles or logs
- invent explanations
- bypass confirmation gates for high-impact repair actions
- erase history to “make it look clean”
- depend on optional modules to be functional

support/ is how Ashby stays serviceable after the original builders are gone.

END OF 8A.14


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 8A.15
tests/

tests/ is Ashby’s immune system.

Everything that makes Ashby resurrectable is enforced here.
Because people forget.
Teams change.
Vendors change.
Pressure happens.
And without mechanical enforcement, the system drifts.

tests/ exists to make the Ashby constitution real:
- WHAT/HOW separation stays true
- Truth Gate posture cannot be weakened quietly
- HouseState staleness and decay cannot be hand-waved
- scope and identity law cannot be bypassed
- modules remain removable
- bridges and vendors remain optional
- secrets do not leak
- offline/degraded behavior stays honest
- migrations remain safe

If tests/ is weak, the platform’s written laws become optional.
Optional law is how platforms die.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1) PURPOSE OF tests/
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

tests/ exists to guarantee:

A) CONSTITUTIONAL INVARIANTS ARE ENFORCED
The most important properties are not “features.”
They are laws.

B) CHANGE CAN BE TRUSTED
A platform that cannot verify changes cannot evolve safely.

C) REGRESSION IS DETECTABLE
If a change breaks truth posture, safety, or removability,
the system must fail loudly before it ships.

D) RESURRECTION IS POSSIBLE
A future builder can run tests and know:
- whether Ashby still matches its doctrine
- where it deviated
- and what needs repair

tests/ is not “quality assurance.”
It is Ashby’s continuity mechanism.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2) CANONICAL tests/ DIRECTORY LAYOUT (THE TEMPLATE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

tests/
├── unit/                                      # pure logic tests (fast)
│   ├── engine/
│   ├── scopes/
│   ├── domain/
│   ├── modules/
│   └── utils/
│
├── contract/                                  # interface contract tests
│   ├── adapters/
│   ├── bridges/
│   ├── native_vendor/
│   ├── connectors/
│   └── plugins/
│
├── integration/                               # stitched system tests (real seams)
│   ├── engine_loop/                           # intent→policy→plan→execute→truth→state→response
│   ├── storage/
│   ├── identity/
│   └── eventing/
│
├── acceptance/                                # “platform behaves as Ashby” tests
│   ├── harness/                               # test harness runtime + fixtures
│   ├── scenarios/                             # high-level scenarios by domain
│   └── golden/                                # golden outputs (truth posture + language)
│
├── migration/                                 # schema + structural realignment tests
│   ├── fixtures/
│   ├── forward/
│   └── rollback/
│
├── security/                                  # secrets, sanitization, access control
│   ├── leak_tests/
│   ├── permission_tests/
│   └── plugin_sandbox_tests/
│
├── performance/                               # latency + throughput + resource bounds
│   ├── load/
│   └── soak/
│
└── chaos/                                     # degraded/offline/outage tests
    ├── offline/
    ├── vendor_outage/
    ├── bridge_outage/
    └── partial_failure/

The names are canonical at the category level.
Tools can vary.
Responsibilities do not.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
3) THE CONSTITUTIONAL TEST SET (WHAT MUST BE TESTED, ALWAYS)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Ashby must have explicit tests that enforce the following invariants.
These are non-negotiable.

3.1 WHAT / HOW SEPARATION (THE SACRED SPLIT)
Tests must fail if:
- modules perform execution
- modules import vendor SDKs, bridge clients, or connector clients
- modules perform network IO
- engine becomes dependent on optional organs (modules/)

Examples of enforcement:
- forbidden import tests
- network syscall blocking tests in module execution context
- “adapter-only execution” contract tests

3.2 TRUTH GATE POSTURE (NO LYING)
Tests must fail if:
- the system claims an action occurred without ActionResult evidence
- the system claims VERIFIED when evidence is weak or missing
- forbidden phrases appear under UNKNOWN/LAST_KNOWN
- uncertainty qualifiers disappear during formatting

These tests must cover:
- success cases (VERIFIED)
- partial success
- contradictory readbacks
- outages (UNKNOWN)
- stale beliefs (LAST_KNOWN with timestamps)

3.3 HOUSESTATE BELIEF LAW (STALENESS/DECAY)
Tests must fail if:
- HouseState stores blobs as belief content
- stale beliefs are presented as current reality
- decay rules are bypassed
- mixed reality is collapsed into “all good”

HouseState tests must cover:
- belief updates from evidence bundles
- staleness evaluation under time passage
- conflict handling (two providers disagree)
- “no new evidence” scenarios

3.4 SCOPE AND CONTEXT LAW (NO GUESSING)
Tests must fail if:
- contextual phrases (“in here”) resolve without a valid anchor
- alias collisions are hidden instead of surfaced
- cross-scope actions occur without explicit resolution
- scope capability maps are ignored by routing

These tests must include:
- multi-room ambiguity
- stale session anchors
- scope permission boundaries

3.5 POLICY OUTCOMES (ACT / CLARIFY / CONFIRM / REFUSE / CHAT)
Tests must fail if:
- ambiguity does not lead to CLARIFY
- high-impact actions do not lead to CONFIRM
- unavailable capabilities do not lead to REFUSE
- emotion does not change law (“frustration is not authorization”)

These tests must cover:
- direct commands
- vibe statements (must not execute)
- follow-up merge-and-complete flows

3.6 REMOVABILITY (UNINSTALL MUST NOT KILL THE BODY)
Tests must fail if:
- removing a module breaks engine boot
- removing a module breaks artifact readability
- disabling a module leaves background execution running
- router selects disabled/uninstalled modules
- platform “pretends it tried” when capability is unavailable

These tests must include:
- “feature removed” refusal correctness
- orphan trigger prevention (eventing/runtime)
- generic artifact readers

3.7 SECRETS AND PRIVACY (NO LEAKS)
Tests must fail if:
- logs include tokens/credentials
- diagnostic bundles contain secrets
- exports contain secret handles resolved into raw values
- plugin sandbox can exfiltrate secrets or call forbidden APIs

This includes:
- sanitization tests
- bundle redaction tests
- permission boundary tests for connectors and plugins

3.8 OFFLINE / DEGRADED HONESTY
Tests must fail if:
- the system pretends it reached a vendor during outage
- it claims “done” when no execution occurred
- it hides unknowns instead of surfacing them
- it retry-storms

These tests must include:
- bridge outage
- vendor API outage
- partial connectivity
- long staleness windows

These eight domains are the constitutional immune system.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
4) CONTRACT TESTS (INTERFACE CONTRACTS ARE LAW)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Contract tests exist to prevent two common failure patterns:
- “adapter changed shape and broke modules silently”
- “connector returns something new and the platform lies about it”

Contract tests must verify:

A) INPUT/OUTPUT SHAPES
- request schema validation
- response schema validation
- error shape validation

B) SEMANTIC EXPECTATIONS
- idempotency behavior where promised
- verification semantics (readback expectations)
- “ack vs verified” distinction remains intact

C) ATTRIBUTION
- every ActionResult includes:
  - provider/adapter attribution
  - timestamps
  - correlation_id
  - scope_id (where applicable)

Contract tests are where vendors are tamed into stable interfaces.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
5) ACCEPTANCE TESTS (THE PLATFORM BEHAVIOR HARNESS)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Acceptance tests answer the only question that matters:

Does the system behave as Ashby?

They must simulate:
- user inputs
- scope contexts
- permission contexts
- adapter/provider behavior (including failures)
and validate:
- policy outcomes
- truth posture wording constraints
- state updates
- artifact creation
- audit traces

Acceptance tests must include scenario families such as:

- lighting: “turn on the lights in here” under ambiguity
- security: “unlock the door” requiring confirmation
- comfort: “it’s freezing” (vibe statement) → clarify into command
- meetings: schedule changes with confirmation gates
- inventory: add/remove items, audit trails, correction flows
- automations: events triggering actions must still respect policy/confirm

Golden tests must validate response posture:
- it must be impossible for the system to “sound verified” without evidence

Acceptance tests are where the Codex becomes executable.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
6) MIGRATION TESTS (TIME IS A GUARANTEED ADVERSARY)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Migrations will happen:
- schema versions evolve
- scope graphs change
- device identities are remapped
- storage layouts move
- bridges/providers are swapped

Migration tests must cover:

A) FORWARD MIGRATION
- old persisted shapes → new shapes
- with explicit audit of what changed

B) ROLLBACK BEHAVIOR (WHEN CLAIMED)
- if rollback is claimed safe, tests must prove it
- if rollback is not safe, the release must declare it clearly

C) IDENTITY CONTINUITY
- device_key continuity must survive migrations
- scope_id continuity must survive renames/moves

D) NO SILENT SEMANTIC CHANGE
- migration must not rewrite truth semantics
- migration must not “clean up” by deleting history silently

Migration tests are how Ashby survives years.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
7) SECURITY TESTS (THE PLATFORM MUST BE SAFE TO PRESERVE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Security tests must enforce:

- secret-handles never resolve into raw secret strings in logs
- plugins cannot escape sandbox
- connectors cannot access scopes/users they are not permitted to
- identity/permissions checks are enforced for high-impact actions
- diagnostic bundles are redacted and bounded
- exports contain manifests and never include secrets

Security tests are not optional.
A resurrectable platform must also be preservable safely.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
8) PERFORMANCE AND CHAOS (STABILITY IS A FEATURE OF LAW)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Performance tests exist to prevent:
- slow degradation that forces unsafe shortcuts later

Chaos tests exist to ensure:
- the system stays honest under failure

Performance tests must enforce:
- bounded latency for policy decisions and routing
- bounded memory growth (no storage/HouseState bloat)
- bounded retries (no retry storms)

Chaos tests must enforce:
- outage honesty
- partial failure reporting
- no phantom success
- staleness surfaces properly under silence

If the system only behaves correctly when everything is perfect,
it is not Ashby.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
9) GOVERNANCE THROUGH TESTS (CONTROL SYNC ENFORCEMENT)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Tests are how Control Sync becomes real.

When a constitutional rule is changed (by legitimate governance),
tests must be updated deliberately and explicitly.

The forbidden pattern:
- change behavior
- then weaken tests to make it pass
- without acknowledging constitutional change

Therefore:
- constitutional tests should be treated as “law tests”
- changes to law tests require explicit review discipline

If law tests drift casually, the Codex becomes decoration.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

tests/ must:
- enforce the core Ashby invariants mechanically
- contain acceptance harnesses that validate end-to-end behavior
- contain contract tests that tame integrations into stable interfaces
- contain migration tests that preserve identity and history
- contain security tests that prevent leaks and sandbox escape
- contain chaos tests that enforce honest degraded behavior
- be runnable by future builders with minimal environment assumptions

tests/ must never:
- be optional to ship changes
- allow the system to “pass” while violating truth posture or safety law
- encode hidden product forks as test expectations
- become a place where meaning changes are hidden by adjusting assertions quietly

tests/ is how Ashby stays Ashby.

END OF 8A.15


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 9.1
WHAT THE ENGINE IS

The engine is the governing core of Ashby.

It is the system that decides:
- what Ashby believes
- what Ashby is allowed to do
- what Ashby is allowed to claim

The engine is not an execution layer.
It is not an integration hub.
It is not a convenience container.

The engine is law made operational.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ENGINE RESPONSIBILITIES
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The engine owns:

1) INTENT INTERPRETATION
   - interpreting normalized intent
   - resolving meaning within scope
   - distinguishing command, vibe, and inquiry

2) POLICY DECISION
   - act vs clarify vs refuse vs chat
   - safety constraints
   - permission constraints
   - automation constraints

3) SCOPE RESOLUTION
   - determining the active scope
   - enforcing containment and authority boundaries
   - refusing unscoped action

4) TRUTH ENFORCEMENT
   - Action-Gated Truth
   - confidence classification
   - language constraint enforcement
   - forbidden phrase enforcement

5) STATE AUTHORITY
   - authoritative belief storage by scope
   - explicit confidence and decay
   - reconciliation after contradiction

6) MEMORY SEMANTICS
   - what is remembered and why
   - how memory is queried and applied
   - how loss and decay are represented

7) SESSION LAW
   - context carryover rules
   - pending clarification handling
   - expiry and safety behavior

8) EXPLAINABILITY
   - traceable decisions
   - evidence-based explanations
   - refusal justification

9) GOVERNANCE
   - Control Sync enforcement for foundational change
   - protection of invariants over time

These responsibilities define Ashby’s identity.
If they are altered without Control Sync,
Ashby drifts.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ENGINE INPUTS AND OUTPUTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The engine consumes:
- user input (transport-neutral)
- scope context
- current state and confidence
- session context
- policy configuration
- module capability declarations
- ActionResults (as evidence)

The engine produces:
- decisions (act / clarify / refuse / chat)
- ActionRequests (WHAT, not HOW)
- constrained responses grounded in evidence
- state transitions derived from evidence

The engine never produces:
- vendor calls
- protocol traffic
- hardware commands

Execution is external.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ENGINE AS A STABILITY ANCHOR
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The engine must remain stable
as everything around it evolves.

Modules will change.
Vendors will change.
Protocols will change.
Agents will multiply.
Products will diversify.

The engine must not drift.

This stability is what allows:
- replaceability at the edges
- rebuildability after loss
- explainability under failure
- trust accumulation over time

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The engine must never:
- import vendor SDKs
- contain protocol logic
- execute actions directly
- special-case products
- allow truth leakage
- accept exception-based growth

If a feature requires violating these constraints,
the feature is incompatible with Ashby.

The engine is what makes Ashby a platform.

END OF 9.1


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 9.2
WHAT THE ROUTER IS

The router is the execution coordinator of Ashby.

It is not the engine.
It does not reason.
It does not decide truth.

The router exists to connect
engine decisions
to execution reality
without altering meaning.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
THE ROLE OF THE ROUTER
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The router is responsible for:

- receiving engine decisions
- dispatching ActionRequests to appropriate HOW layers
- managing execution flow
- collecting ActionResults
- returning evidence to the engine

The router answers the question:
“Where does this ActionRequest go?”

It does not answer:
“Should this happen?”
“What does this mean?”
“Did this succeed?”

Those questions belong to the engine.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ROUTER INPUTS AND OUTPUTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The router consumes:
- ActionRequests from the engine
- resolved scope identifiers
- execution policies
- adapter availability information

The router produces:
- dispatched execution calls
- collected ActionResults
- explicit failure signals
- execution metadata

The router never produces:
- user-facing language
- confidence levels
- state updates
- truth claims

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ROUTER AS ORCHESTRATOR, NOT LOGIC
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The router orchestrates.

It may:
- select an adapter based on capability
- handle retries and fallbacks
- manage timeouts
- sequence multiple execution steps

It must never:
- infer intent
- alter policy decisions
- upgrade confidence
- collapse outcomes into success
- hide partial failure

If the router begins to reason,
the architecture has failed.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ROUTER AND FAILURE HANDLING
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Failure handling in the router
is mechanical, not interpretive.

The router must:
- surface adapter failure explicitly
- report timeouts accurately
- preserve partial results
- avoid masking errors

The router must not:
- retry indefinitely
- convert failure into silence
- invent recovery narratives

Failure is evidence.
Interpretation belongs to the engine.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ROUTER AND SCOPES
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The router enforces scope routing.

It ensures that:
- execution is sent only to scope-compatible adapters
- no adapter receives out-of-scope commands
- execution boundaries are respected

The router does not define scope.
It enforces scope decisions made by the engine.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The router must never:
- own state authority
- define truth semantics
- generate user responses
- bypass Action-Gated Truth
- contain vendor-specific reasoning

If logic grows inside the router,
it must be extracted.

The router is a bridge,
not a brain.

END OF 9.2


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 9.3
WHAT THE ROUTER MUST NEVER BECOME

The router must never become a decision-maker.

This prohibition is absolute.

The router exists to move decisions
into execution and return evidence.
Nothing more.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
FORBIDDEN RESPONSIBILITIES
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The router must never:

- interpret user intent
- decide policy outcomes
- select actions based on meaning
- assign confidence levels
- update state or memory
- generate explanations
- decide what Ashby should claim

If any of these behaviors appear,
the router has been corrupted.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHY ROUTERS ACCIDENTALLY GROW BRAINS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Router corruption is common.

It begins with convenience:
- “just handle this case here”
- “the engine doesn’t know about this yet”
- “it’s faster to decide here”

These shortcuts feel harmless.
They are not.

They create:
- hidden policy
- duplicated logic
- untraceable behavior
- brittle refactors
- erosion of trust

Once logic enters the router,
it rarely leaves.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
THE GOD-ROUTER FAILURE MODE
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A router that reasons becomes a god-router.

God-routers:
- know too much
- do too much
- cannot be removed
- cannot be tested in isolation
- silently diverge from engine law

This failure mode is terminal.

If the router becomes a second engine,
Ashby ceases to be coherent.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EARLY WARNING SIGNS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The following are warning signs of router corruption:

- branching on semantic intent
- special-casing domains
- embedding retry logic with interpretation
- modifying ActionRequests
- altering execution meaning
- generating partial explanations

Any of these require immediate correction.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
CORRECTIVE ACTION
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

When router logic grows:

- extract reasoning into the engine
- extract policy into cognition subsystems
- extract execution specifics into adapters

The router must shrink,
not grow.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The router must never:

- become a decision authority
- compete with the engine
- define behavior
- mask failure
- invent success

The router is mechanical.

If it begins to think,
the architecture has failed.

END OF 9.3


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 10.1
INPUT AND TRANSPORT NEUTRALITY

Ashby is input-agnostic and transport-neutral.

No interface defines behavior.
No transport defines meaning.

This neutrality is required to prevent
interface-driven logic
and transport-driven assumptions.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHAT TRANSPORT NEUTRALITY MEANS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Transport neutrality means that:
- voice input
- text input
- API calls
- scheduled triggers
- agent-to-agent messages

are all treated as equivalent sources of intent.

Meaning is derived from content,
not from where it came from.

The engine must never:
- branch on transport type
- special-case interfaces
- assume reliability based on channel

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INPUT AS RAW SIGNAL
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Input is a raw signal.

It may be:
- ambiguous
- incomplete
- contradictory
- malformed
- delayed

The engine is responsible for:
- normalization
- interpretation
- clarification
- refusal when required

Input sources do not perform interpretation.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHY THIS LAW EXISTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Systems that bind logic to interfaces
become brittle.

They grow:
- voice-specific hacks
- UI-specific behavior
- API-only shortcuts

Eventually, behavior diverges
based on how something was said,
not what was meant.

Ashby forbids this.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ENGINE ENFORCEMENT
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The engine enforces neutrality by:
- accepting normalized input only
- applying identical cognition paths
- producing transport-agnostic decisions

Transports may:
- add metadata
- add context hints
- report delivery constraints

They may not:
- alter meaning
- decide policy
- suppress clarification

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
FAILURE AND NEUTRALITY
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Transport failure is not semantic failure.

If a transport drops:
- intent is not reinterpreted
- truth is not upgraded
- state is not assumed

Failures are reported as evidence,
not smoothed over.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

No transport may:
- define truth
- bypass cognition
- invent intent
- suppress uncertainty

Transport is a pipe.

Meaning lives elsewhere.

This neutrality allows Ashby
to evolve interfaces
without rewriting law.

END OF 10.1


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 10.2
INTENT EXTRACTION AND NORMALIZATION

Intent extraction is the process by which Ashby
converts raw input into a structured, actionable representation.

Normalization ensures that intent is expressed
in a consistent form
before any reasoning occurs.

This separation prevents ambiguity
from contaminating policy, scope, or execution.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
RAW INPUT VS INTENT
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Raw input is untrusted.

It may be:
- vague
- incomplete
- emotional
- contradictory
- imprecise

Intent is structured.

It represents:
- desired outcome
- implied action
- referenced entities
- candidate scope
- confidence level

Normalization is required
before intent may enter cognition.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTENT EXTRACTION
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Intent extraction identifies:
- action candidates
- target references
- qualifiers and modifiers
- temporal hints
- negation or cancellation
- uncertainty markers

Extraction does not decide meaning.
It surfaces possibilities.

Ambiguity is preserved,
not resolved,
at this stage.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
NORMALIZATION
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Normalization converts extracted intent
into a canonical form.

This includes:
- standardized action verbs
- normalized entity identifiers
- explicit scope references
- normalized modifiers
- explicit ambiguity flags

Normalization removes surface variation,
not semantic uncertainty.

Different phrasings that mean the same thing
must normalize identically.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHAT NORMALIZATION MUST NEVER DO
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Normalization must never:
- resolve ambiguity
- infer missing intent
- assume default scope
- upgrade confidence
- discard uncertainty

Those decisions belong to cognition and policy.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTENT AS INPUT TO COGNITION
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Only normalized intent
may enter cognition subsystems.

This guarantees that:
- reasoning is deterministic
- policy decisions are traceable
- behavior is consistent across transports

If normalization cannot produce
a valid structured intent,
the engine must clarify or refuse.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EVOLUTION AND EXTENSIBILITY
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Intent schemas may evolve.

New actions,
domains,
or modifiers
may be added
without rewriting existing law.

Normalization absorbs this growth
without affecting policy semantics.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Intent extraction and normalization must never:
- decide policy
- execute actions
- define truth
- alter state
- hide ambiguity

They exist to prepare input
for lawful reasoning.

END OF 10.2


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 10.3
POLICY: ACT VS CLARIFY VS CHAT

Policy determines Ashby’s response to intent.

It answers a single question:
“What is Ashby allowed to do next?”

Policy is evaluated after intent normalization
and before any execution occurs.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
THE THREE POLICY OUTCOMES
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Every normalized intent must resolve to exactly one outcome:

1) ACT
   - Ashby has sufficient clarity
   - scope authority is resolved
   - safety constraints are satisfied
   - execution is permitted

2) CLARIFY
   - intent is ambiguous
   - scope is unresolved
   - safety confidence is insufficient
   - required information is missing

3) CHAT
   - no action is requested
   - intent is informational or conversational
   - execution would be inappropriate

No other outcomes are permitted.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHEN ASHBY MAY ACT
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Ashby may act only when:

- intent is unambiguous
- target scope is resolved
- authority is confirmed
- required permissions exist
- safety checks pass

If any condition is unmet,
ACT is forbidden.

Speed is never a justification
for bypassing policy.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHEN ASHBY MUST CLARIFY
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Clarification is required when:

- multiple actions are plausible
- multiple scopes are plausible
- target resolution is incomplete
- safety impact is unclear
- confidence is too low

Clarification must:
- be specific
- surface uncertainty
- avoid assumptions
- reduce ambiguity

Clarification is not a failure.
It is a safety mechanism.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHEN ASHBY MAY CHAT
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Chat is permitted when:

- the input does not request action
- the intent is exploratory
- the user is asking for explanation
- execution would be inappropriate

Chat must never:
- imply actions occurred
- invent state
- smooth over uncertainty

Chat remains bound by truth semantics.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
POLICY AND SESSIONS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Policy interacts with session state.

A prior session may:
- reduce ambiguity
- supply missing context
- constrain interpretation

Sessions may inform policy,
but may not override safety or scope law.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Policy must never:
- guess intent
- assume scope
- bypass clarification
- invent permission
- collapse outcomes

Every decision must be defensible
under audit.

Policy is how Ashby
chooses restraint over impulse.

END OF 10.3


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 10.4
PLANNING VS EXECUTION

Planning and execution are separate phases in Ashby.

This separation exists to ensure that
decision-making remains honest,
traceable,
and independent of execution side effects.

No action may be executed
before it has been fully planned.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHAT PLANNING IS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Planning is the process of determining:
- what actions are required
- in what order
- within which scopes
- under which constraints
- with what success criteria

Planning answers:
“What should happen?”

Planning occurs entirely within the engine
and cognition subsystems.

Planning produces:
- one or more ActionRequests
- explicit scope bindings
- explicit success criteria
- explicit safety constraints

Planning never touches the external world.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHAT EXECUTION IS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Execution is the process of attempting
to realize a planned action in the real world.

Execution answers:
“What actually happened?”

Execution:
- is performed by adapters, bridges, or connectors
- interacts with external systems
- produces observable results
- may partially succeed or fail

Execution never decides meaning.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHY THIS SEPARATION IS REQUIRED
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

When planning and execution are mixed:
- intent becomes mutable
- failure becomes ambiguous
- retries distort meaning
- truth becomes narrative

Ashby prevents this by enforcing a hard boundary.

A plan may exist without execution.
Execution must never exist without a plan.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PLANS AS AUDITABLE ARTIFACTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Plans must be auditable.

A plan must clearly state:
- intended action
- target scope
- required permissions
- safety assumptions
- expected evidence

If execution deviates,
the deviation is visible.

This allows Ashby to:
- explain partial success
- identify failure modes
- avoid retroactive reinterpretation

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EXECUTION FEEDBACK
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Execution produces ActionResults.

ActionResults are:
- evidence
- not confirmation
- not truth

ActionResults are returned to the engine,
where truth is gated,
state is updated,
and memory is recorded.

Execution feedback never alters the plan retroactively.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
MULTI-STEP PLANS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Some intents require multiple actions.

Planning may:
- sequence actions
- define dependencies
- specify rollback behavior
- define partial success semantics

Execution must respect this sequence.

If a step fails,
planning does not change.
State reflects reality.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Planning must never:
- assume execution success
- skip safety checks
- depend on vendor behavior

Execution must never:
- reinterpret intent
- alter plan meaning
- hide failure
- invent success

Planning defines intention.
Execution reveals reality.

This separation preserves truth.

END OF 10.4


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 10.5
ADAPTER INVOCATION

Adapter invocation is the moment
Ashby attempts to affect reality.

It is the boundary where
planned intent meets execution.

This boundary is strictly controlled.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHEN ADAPTER INVOCATION OCCURS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Adapter invocation occurs only after:

- intent has been normalized
- policy has resolved to ACT
- scope has been resolved
- planning has completed
- safety constraints are satisfied

If any prerequisite is unmet,
adapter invocation is forbidden.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHAT IS INVOKED
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The router invokes adapters
using ActionRequests.

An ActionRequest contains:
- the planned action
- resolved scope identifiers
- execution parameters
- safety constraints
- correlation identifiers

ActionRequests describe WHAT must be attempted,
not HOW it is performed.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
HOW ADAPTERS ARE SELECTED
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Adapter selection is capability-based.

The router may select adapters based on:
- declared capabilities
- scope compatibility
- availability
- execution policy

Selection must be deterministic
and auditable.

Adapters are never selected
based on convenience or speed alone.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INVOCATION SEMANTICS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Invocation is fire-and-report.

Once invoked:
- the adapter attempts execution
- the router waits for a response or timeout
- retries may occur per policy
- partial results are preserved

Invocation does not:
- assume success
- alter the plan
- upgrade confidence

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
FAILURE HANDLING
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Adapter failure must be explicit.

Failures include:
- execution errors
- timeouts
- partial completion
- unreachable systems

The router must:
- surface failures accurately
- avoid masking errors
- return all evidence

Failure is evidence,
not interpretation.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
CONCURRENCY AND SAFETY
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Multiple adapters may be invoked concurrently
only when explicitly planned.

Concurrency must:
- respect scope boundaries
- avoid race conditions
- preserve evidence integrity

Unplanned concurrency is forbidden.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Adapter invocation must never:
- bypass the router
- execute without a plan
- modify intent
- update state directly
- generate user-facing language

Invocation is mechanical.

Meaning is assigned later,
by the engine.

END OF 10.5


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 10.6
ACTIONRESULT AS REALITY

ActionResult is the only representation of reality
that Ashby is allowed to trust.

It is not a convenience object.
It is not a logging artifact.
It is the authoritative evidence boundary
between execution and belief.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHAT AN ACTIONRESULT IS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

An ActionResult represents the observable outcome
of a single attempted action.

It records:
- what was attempted
- how execution was invoked
- what was observed
- what failed or succeeded
- what uncertainty remains
- timing and correlation data

ActionResult does not interpret meaning.
It records evidence.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHY ACTIONRESULT IS AUTHORITATIVE
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Without ActionResult,
systems infer success.

Inference creates lies.

ActionResult forces Ashby to confront reality
as it occurred,
not as it was intended.

No action may influence state,
truth,
or language
without an ActionResult.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ACTIONRESULT AND CONFIDENCE
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

ActionResult feeds the Truth Gate.

From ActionResult,
the engine derives:
- VERIFIED outcomes
- LAST_KNOWN outcomes
- FAILED outcomes
- UNKNOWN outcomes

Confidence is not reported by adapters.
Confidence is assigned by the engine.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ACTIONRESULT AND PARTIAL SUCCESS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Partial success is explicit.

An ActionResult may represent:
- some targets succeeded
- some targets failed
- some targets are uncertain

Partial success must never
be collapsed into success.

Ambiguity is preserved.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ACTIONRESULT AND MEMORY
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

ActionResults are recorded as memory.

They form the factual history
that explanations and audits rely on.

Memory without ActionResult
is fiction.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ACTIONRESULT AND RETRIES
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Retries produce additional ActionResults.

Each attempt is recorded.

A later success does not erase
earlier failure.

This prevents retroactive rewriting of history.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

No component may:
- invent ActionResults
- bypass ActionResult creation
- merge ActionResults implicitly
- discard ActionResults silently

ActionResult is the spine of execution truth.

If ActionResult is compromised,
Ashby cannot be trusted.

END OF 10.6


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 10.7
TRUTH GATE ENFORCEMENT

The Truth Gate enforces what Ashby is allowed to claim.

It is the mechanism that converts evidence
into constrained language,
state updates,
and future reasoning.

Without the Truth Gate,
Action-Gated Truth collapses.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHAT THE TRUTH GATE IS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The Truth Gate is a deterministic evaluation step
inside the engine.

It consumes:
- ActionResults
- current scoped state
- confidence decay rules
- verification policy

It produces:
- allowed language claims
- state transitions
- confidence classifications

The Truth Gate does not guess.
It evaluates.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TRUTH CLASSES
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The Truth Gate assigns exactly one class
per affected belief:

- VERIFIED
- LAST_KNOWN
- FAILED
- UNKNOWN

These classes constrain:
- what may be said
- how state is recorded
- what future actions are allowed

Truth classes are not descriptive.
They are enforcement primitives.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LANGUAGE CONSTRAINT ENFORCEMENT
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The Truth Gate constrains language.

Ashby may:
- assert VERIFIED outcomes
- qualify LAST_KNOWN outcomes
- report FAILED outcomes
- admit UNKNOWN outcomes

Ashby must never:
- smooth partial success
- imply verification without evidence
- invent continuity
- hide uncertainty

If language cannot be expressed accurately,
Ashby must pause or explain uncertainty.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
STATE UPDATE ENFORCEMENT
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

State updates occur only through the Truth Gate.

No component may:
- write belief directly
- upgrade confidence implicitly
- bypass verification rules

State reflects evaluated reality,
not intent.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TRUTH GATE AND MEMORY
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The Truth Gate determines
what is recorded as memory.

Memory records:
- evaluated outcomes
- confidence assignments
- uncertainty markers
- verification failures

This ensures historical accuracy.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TRUTH GATE AND FUTURE BEHAVIOR
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Truth Gate output influences:
- follow-up questions
- retries
- clarification
- refusal
- automation behavior

False certainty is never propagated.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The Truth Gate must never:
- accept adapter claims as truth
- be bypassed for convenience
- be weakened for fluency
- be overridden by products

If the Truth Gate is compromised,
Ashby becomes untrustworthy.

The Truth Gate is the last line of defense
between reality and lies.

END OF 10.7


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 10.8
RESPONSE CONSTRUCTION

Response construction is the final step
in Ashby’s control pipeline.

It is where evaluated truth
is translated into human-facing language
without distortion.

Response construction does not decide truth.
It expresses it.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WHAT RESPONSE CONSTRUCTION IS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Response construction takes as input:
- Truth Gate output
- scoped state
- confidence classifications
- policy outcome
- session context

It produces:
- user-facing language
- explanations
- clarification prompts
- refusals

Response construction is constrained expression,
not free-form generation.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TRUTH-CONSTRAINED LANGUAGE
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

All responses are bound by truth classes.

Ashby may:
- assert VERIFIED outcomes
- qualify LAST_KNOWN outcomes
- report FAILED outcomes
- admit UNKNOWN outcomes

Ashby must never:
- imply verification without evidence
- smooth partial success
- invent continuity
- hide uncertainty

Language must reflect evaluated reality,
not intent.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
RESPONSE TYPES
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Response construction supports:

- confirmations (after VERIFIED outcomes)
- qualified confirmations (after LAST_KNOWN outcomes)
- failure reports
- uncertainty admissions
- clarification questions
- refusal explanations
- informational responses

Each response type has
defined semantic constraints.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EXPLANATION REQUIREMENTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

When Ashby explains,
it must be able to reference:
- what action was attempted
- what evidence exists
- what failed or succeeded
- what is uncertain
- why a decision was made

Explanations are evidence-based,
not narrative.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SESSION AWARENESS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Response construction is session-aware.

It may:
- reference prior clarified context
- continue a clarification flow
- maintain conversational continuity

It must never:
- assume context not recorded
- reconstruct lost state
- invent prior agreement

Session awareness is bounded by memory.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
FAILURE AND REFUSAL LANGUAGE
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Failure and refusal must be explicit.

Ashby must:
- state failure clearly
- explain why it refused
- surface uncertainty honestly
- avoid blame or deflection

Politeness must not obscure truth.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Response construction must never:
- override Truth Gate output
- upgrade confidence
- decide policy
- bypass clarification
- smooth reality for UX

Response construction exists
to tell the truth clearly.

This is where Ashby earns trust.

END OF 10.8


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 11.1  
INTENT

Intent is the canonical, normalized representation of what an input means.

It is not a plan.  
It is not an execution instruction.  
It is not a truth claim.

Intent exists so that Ashby can treat all inputs—voice, text, API calls, triggers, and agent-to-agent messages—as the same kind of raw signal, then apply one lawful cognition path to produce one lawful outcome.

Intent is the first stable artifact in the control pipeline.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────  
PURPOSE OF INTENT  
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Intent exists to enforce four constraints:

1) INPUT NEUTRALITY  
Raw input may be ambiguous, emotional, contradictory, malformed, or delayed.  
Intent must remain structured and transport-agnostic regardless of input source.

2) STABILITY UNDER GROWTH  
NLU engines change. Models change. Transports change.  
Intent provides a stable interface so the engine, policy, modules, and test harness do not drift with each NLU implementation.

3) SEPARATION OF MEANING FROM EXECUTION  
Intent describes WHAT the user wants in normalized terms.  
Planning and execution occur later.  
Intent must not contain vendor identifiers, protocol details, or execution logic.

4) AUDITABILITY AND REBUILDABILITY  
If raw input is lost, a persisted Intent must still be sufficient to explain what was understood, what was ambiguous, and why policy behaved as it did.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────  
INTENT AS LAWFUL INPUT TO THE ENGINE  
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Intent is the only form of user meaning the engine is allowed to reason over.

Downstream components must never substitute:
- raw text
- UI events
- transport metadata
- adapter-specific payloads

If a system passes unnormalized raw text deeper into the pipeline to “save time,” the system is already drifting.

Intent is the boundary that prevents:
- interface-driven hacks
- transport-specific shortcuts
- silent reinterpretation
- inconsistent policy behavior across channels

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────  
CANONICAL INTENT STRUCTURE  
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The canonical structure below is normative.

Additional intent types may exist as new modules are added.  
The naming pattern is invariant.  
The shape is invariant.  
The set of type strings is extensible.

Canonical form (JSON-like):

{
  "type": "<module>.<verb>" | "chat" | "unknown",

  "targets": {
    "device_key": "<internal_device_key>" | null,
    "zone_id": "<zone_id>" | null,
    "room_name": "<human_room_label>" | null,
    "category": "<capability_category>" | null,
    "role": "<device_role>" | null
  },

  "parameters": {
    "brightness": <int 0..1000> | null,
    "delta": <int -1000..1000> | null,
    "temperature_c": <float> | null,
    "duration_sec": <int> | null
  },

  "mode": "max" | "min" | "scene" | null,

  "confidence": "HIGH" | "MEDIUM" | "LOW",

  "raw_text": "<original input>",

  "source": "<nlu_engine_identifier>"
}

This structure is used to:
- drive policy decisions (act vs clarify vs chat)
- select the correct module
- produce deterministic acceptance tests
- preserve explainability under failure

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────  
FIELD SEMANTICS  
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TYPE  
`type` is a namespaced action label.

Rules:
- The prefix identifies the module domain: `<module>`.
- The suffix identifies the normalized verb: `<verb>`.
- Type values must be stable across time; renaming a type is a foundational change.

Examples (representative, non-exhaustive):
- "lights.on"
- "lights.off"
- "lights.adjust"
- "plugs.on"
- "thermostat.set"
- "cameras.snapshot"
- "meetings.start"
- "chat"
- "unknown"

Special values:
- `chat` indicates no action intent is present and the input is conversational or informational.
- `unknown` indicates the system cannot confidently classify the input into a known intent type.

`unknown` is a valid output.  
Returning `unknown` is not failure.  
Returning a wrong confident type is failure.

TARGETS  
`targets` contains normalized references to what the input is about.

Targets are hints, not a resolved execution plan.

Rules:
- Targets must be vendor-neutral.
- Targets must not include vendor IDs, entity IDs from external systems, API handles, or protocol addresses.
- Targets may be partial.
- Targets may be underspecified.
- Targets may imply ambiguity.

Canonical target keys:

- `device_key`  
  A stable internal identifier for a specific device or entity.

- `zone_id`  
  A stable Ashby-owned identifier representing a physical or logical containment unit.

- `room_name`  
  A human label mapped to a zone or scope anchor. It is a convenience label, not reality.

- `category`  
  A capability grouping such as "lights", "plugs", "thermostat", "cameras", "meetings".  
  Categories are interpreted by modules and resolution logic, not by transports.

- `role`  
  A semantic role such as "ambient", "desk", "security", "primary".  
  Roles are Ashby-owned metadata and must never be delegated to adapters.

Target rules under ambiguity:
- If targets do not resolve to a concrete target set, the system must clarify rather than guess.
- If multiple targets match equally, the system must clarify rather than select arbitrarily.

PARAMETERS  
`parameters` contains normalized modifiers and values required to interpret the intent.

Rules:
- All canonical parameters must be normalized before policy and planning.
- Units must be canonical and stable.
- Percent parsing, unit conversion, and “natural language magnitude” interpretation belong to normalization, not execution.

Canonical parameters:

- `brightness`  
  Integer brightness on Ashby’s internal scale: 0–1000.  
  0 represents fully off.  
  1000 represents maximum.

- `delta`  
  Relative adjustment in internal brightness units: -1000..1000.  
  Used for commands like “brighter,” “dim it,” “a little more.”

- `temperature_c`  
  Temperature setpoint in degrees Celsius as the canonical internal unit.  
  Modules and adapters translate as needed, but internal meaning remains °C.

- `duration_sec`  
  Duration in seconds when the intent implies time-bounded behavior.

MODE  
`mode` represents normalized non-numeric extremes or categorical operation modes.

Rules:
- Modes are not a substitute for numeric values.
- Modes exist to preserve meaning for inputs like “max,” “minimum,” or “set a scene.”
- Mode semantics must remain module-owned and must never leak into adapters.

Representative examples:
- "max" (set to maximum permitted by constraints)
- "min" (set to minimum permitted by constraints)
- "scene" (apply a named or configured scene)

CONFIDENCE  
`confidence` expresses NLU certainty, not execution certainty.

Rules:
- Confidence is not proof.
- Confidence cannot upgrade truth.
- Confidence cannot justify skipping clarification when targets are ambiguous.

Semantics:
- HIGH: classification and extracted targets/parameters are likely correct.
- MEDIUM: reasonable interpretation, but ambiguity or uncertainty exists.
- LOW: interpretation is fragile; clarification is likely required.

RAW_TEXT  
`raw_text` preserves the original user input verbatim.

Rules:
- Raw text must be retained for traceability, debugging, and future evaluation.
- Raw text must not be used as a substitute for missing fields downstream.
- Raw text may be redacted or handled carefully in environments where sensitive inputs are possible, but it must never be silently replaced with a paraphrase.

SOURCE  
`source` identifies the NLU engine or extractor that produced the Intent.

Rules:
- Source exists to support debugging, evaluation, and controlled evolution of NLU.
- Source must not alter policy logic. It is metadata, not meaning.
- Source identifiers are representative and replaceable.

Examples (representative):
- "local_nlu"
- "gpt_nlu"
- "hybrid_nlu"

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────  
INTENT INVARIANTS  
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The following invariants are absolute:

1) NO EXECUTION IN INTENT  
Intent must never:
- call tools
- schedule jobs
- modify state
- imply completion
- contain adapter payloads

2) NO VENDOR IDENTIFIERS  
Intent must never embed:
- vendor_device_id
- external entity IDs
- API handles
- protocol addresses
- secret-bearing references

3) CANONICAL UNITS AT THE BOUNDARY  
All numeric meaning must be normalized:
- brightness always 0–1000
- deltas expressed in 0–1000 scale
- temperatures expressed in °C
- durations expressed in seconds

4) AMBIGUITY MUST REMAIN EXPLICIT  
Normalization may remove surface variation.  
Normalization must not erase uncertainty.

If the input is ambiguous, Intent must preserve that ambiguity through:
- null fields
