- repeating actions that were not verified or were partially failed without saying so

If the prior action was FAILED or UNKNOWN, “do it again” is allowed,
but the response must not pretend it succeeded previously.
It must present the posture honestly.

If Ashby cannot identify what “before” refers to, it must CLARIFY:
- “When you say ‘same as before’, which action are you referring to?”

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
7) COMPARATIVES: “BRIGHTER / DIMMER / WARMER / COOLER”
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Comparatives require:
- a target anchor (what device/system changes)
- a baseline (relative to what)
- a step model (how much change)

Ashby may use a default step model only if the step model is explicitly defined as part of platform law
(or as a module configuration), and the system can justify it.

Example lawful approach:
- define “brighter” as +10% brightness unless the module defines a different step,
  and treat that as an explicit, logged rule.

Forbidden approach:
- “brighter means whatever looks right”
- “brighter means max”
- “warmer means +5 degrees always” (unless defined and logged)

If the baseline is unknown (e.g., current brightness unknown),
Ashby can still attempt a relative change if the device supports it,
but must speak honestly about verification and state confidence.

If the action is high-impact (security, money, access), comparatives are not accepted without clarity.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
8) CONTINUATIONS: “ALSO”, “AND THE REST”, “DO THE OTHERS”
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Continuation phrases can expand scope unintentionally.
They are a blast-radius risk.

Rules:
- “also” applies only to the last explicitly defined group unless clarified
- “the rest” requires a defined set (“rest of what?”) unless an explicit candidate set exists
- “do the others” requires the system to know what “others” means within the anchor set

If continuation expands to many targets or high-impact changes,
Ashby must require confirmation.

If the set is undefined, Ashby must CLARIFY.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
9) LOGGING REQUIREMENT (CONTEXT RESOLUTION MUST BE RECONSTRUCTABLE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

For every contextual resolution, the system must be able to reconstruct:
- the phrase that was resolved
- the anchor used (clarification/session/last-target/UI scope/evidence location)
- the resulting target(s) or parameters
- any decay or staleness considerations
- whether confirmation was required due to expansion

If context resolution is not observable, it becomes silent guessing by another name.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Contextual phrases are allowed.
Guessing is forbidden.

Ashby may resolve contextual phrases only when:
- the anchor is explicit and within bounds
- the resulting interpretation is unique enough to be safe
- the resolution is justified and reconstructable

Ashby must CLARIFY when:
- “here” is undefined
- pronouns do not map uniquely
- “before” is unspecified
- continuations expand an undefined set
- resolution would rely on default assumptions

Natural language is messy.
Ashby stays honest by refusing to pretend it is not.

END OF 15.2


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 15.3
MERGE-AND-COMPLETE FLOW

Most real user commands arrive incomplete.

Not because the user is careless,
but because humans speak in fragments that assume shared context:

- “turn the lights on” (which lights?)
- “order more” (what product, how many, from where?)
- “send it to them” (who is them, what is it?)
- “same as last time” (which time?)
- “set it up” (what is “it”?)

Ashby must support completion without guessing.
That requires a disciplined multi-turn transaction model.

This section defines the merge-and-complete flow:
how Ashby safely resumes an incomplete intent, merges new information,
and completes the original request with full auditability.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1) THE CORE MODEL: A CLARIFICATION IS AN INCOMPLETE CONTROL TRANSACTION
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

When Ashby asks a clarification question, it is not “chatting.”
It is pausing an incomplete control transaction.

Therefore:
- the system must preserve the incomplete intent
- the system must preserve what is missing
- the system must preserve candidates already discovered
- the system must preserve the safety posture already determined

This is the job of pending clarification sessions.

The merge-and-complete flow is “resume transaction,” not “start over.”

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2) REQUIRED ARTIFACT: PENDING CLARIFICATION SESSION
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

When policy outcome is CLARIFY (or CONFIRM with missing parameters),
the engine must create a pending clarification session artifact.

Minimum required fields (conceptual; exact schema may evolve under law):

- clarify_id (stable identifier for this transaction)
- created_at and expires_at
- original intent (raw + normalized)
- missing fields:
  - targets
  - scope
  - parameters
  - safety_confirmation
- candidates (if discovered):
  - target candidate sets
  - scopes or zones
  - parameter candidate lists
- constraints:
  - required confirmation posture
  - blast-radius limits
  - disallowed defaults

The artifact exists so Ashby can complete without guessing,
and so future builders can reconstruct why the system asked what it asked.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
3) REPLY CLASSIFICATION: IS THE USER ANSWERING OR CHANGING TOPIC?
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

When a new user message arrives and a pending clarification session exists,
the engine must classify the message as one of:

A) ANSWER (resume the transaction)
The message plausibly answers the pending question.
Examples:
- “kitchen”
- “the desk lamp”
- “the second one”
- “2 boxes”
- “tomorrow morning”

B) MODIFICATION (answer plus change)
The message answers the question but also changes the request.
Examples:
- “kitchen, and make them 50%”
- “actually bedroom, not kitchen”
- “two of them, and cancel the other order”

C) NEW INTENT (do not merge)
The message is a new request.
Examples:
- “never mind”
- “what time is it?”
- “turn off the hallway lights instead”
- “also, add milk to the list” (could be new intent unless the pending intent was shopping)

D) INVALID / UNCLEAR (clarify the clarification)
The message cannot be confidently interpreted as an answer.
Examples:
- “yes”
- “that”
- “whatever”
when candidates are not uniquely defined.

This classification must be conservative.
Misclassifying a new intent as an answer is how accidental execution happens.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
4) MERGE RULES: FILL MISSING FIELDS, DO NOT INVENT
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

When the message is classified as ANSWER or MODIFICATION,
Ashby merges the new info into the pending transaction.

Rules:

4.1 Fill only what was asked for (first)
Start by filling the explicitly missing fields.
Do not opportunistically infer additional fields unless they are explicit.

4.2 Use the candidate sets
If the pending session contains candidate options,
resolve answers against those options first.
This prevents drift and guessing.

4.3 Apply contextual phrase resolution law
If the user uses contextual phrases (“that one”, “in here”, “same as before”),
resolve them only using the context anchor stack (Section 15.2 law).
If resolution is not unique, ask again.

4.4 Explicit overrides win
If the user explicitly contradicts earlier assumptions (“actually bedroom”),
the explicit statement overrides the pending selection.

4.5 Do not merge across scope boundaries casually
If the new answer implies a different scope,
Ashby must ensure authority checks and scope validity are re-evaluated.

4.6 Re-run planning after merge
After merge, the engine must re-run:
- validation
- policy evaluation (if risk changed)
- planning (ActionRequests may change)
before execution.

Merging is not execution.
Merging is preparation for lawful execution.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
5) THE “COMPLETE WHAT YOU CAN” QUESTION (ATOMICITY VS PARTIAL EXECUTION)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Sometimes a user request contains multiple actions and only one is missing data.

Example:
- “turn off the kitchen lights and lock the front door”
If “kitchen lights” is ambiguous but “front door” is unambiguous,
should Ashby lock the door now?

Ashby must not silently split execution.
It may split only under explicit, predictable rules.

Allowed approach:
- If the actions are independent and low-risk, Ashby may execute the unambiguous part
  only if it explicitly states:
  - what it executed
  - what it is waiting on
  - what question remains

Disallowed approach:
- executing a subset silently and pretending the full request was completed
- executing a high-impact subset while waiting on a low-impact clarification
  without explicit confirmation posture

High-impact actions (security, access, money) should bias toward:
- hold until the whole transaction is safe and confirmed

This keeps the system from “helpfully” doing the wrong thing.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
6) CONFIRMATION IN THE MERGE FLOW (WHEN THE ANSWER CHANGES RISK)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A merge can change blast radius.

Example:
- “turn off the lights” → clarify: “which ones?”
- user answers: “all of them”

That answer expands scope.
Ashby must:
- re-evaluate whether confirmation is required
- if required, transition the pending session into CONFIRM posture:
  - summarize the intended action in bounded, audit-friendly language
  - require explicit approval
  - then execute

The merge flow must not treat “answering the question” as implicit consent for expanded impact.

Answers provide information.
Consent is separate.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
7) COMPLETION: EXECUTE, VERIFY, AND SPEAK WITH TRUTH POSTURE
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Once the transaction is complete:
- generate ActionRequests
- execute via adapters/providers
- collect ActionResults
- apply Truth Gate
- update HouseState
- respond with lawful posture

Critical rule:
The response must reflect reality evidence, not conversational closure.

A completed merge flow is not:
- “okay done”
It is:
- “attempted X; evidence indicates VERIFIED/LAST_KNOWN/UNKNOWN/FAILED for Y.”

This is where most assistants lie.
Ashby must not.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
8) EXPIRY AND STALENESS (WHEN TO REFUSE TO MERGE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Pending clarification sessions expire.

If the pending transaction is expired or stale, Ashby must not merge blindly.
It must re-establish context.

Examples of required re-establishment:
- “Are you still trying to turn on the kitchen lights?”
- “Just confirming — which device did you mean?”

The exact expiry window is configurable, but the rule is invariant:
old context is not a license to guess.

Expired sessions must fail safe.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
9) IDEMPOTENCY AND DUPLICATE PREVENTION (MERGE SHOULD NOT DOUBLE-EXECUTE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Users repeat themselves.
Networks duplicate messages.
Surfaces resend.

The merge flow must prevent accidental double execution.

Minimum requirements:
- the pending session tracks whether execution already occurred
- action_ids and correlation_id are used to detect duplicates
- if a user repeats the same answer after completion,
  Ashby should not re-execute automatically without clear intent

If the user wants repetition, they can say so explicitly (“do it again”).

This protects the user from “helpfulness as harm.”

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
10) LOGGING REQUIREMENT (TRANSACTION RESUMPTION MUST BE RECONSTRUCTABLE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The system must log:
- the clarify_id
- the question asked
- the user answer classification (answer/modification/new intent/unclear)
- the merge result (fields filled, candidates chosen)
- whether policy posture changed (clarify → confirm → act)
- what executed, with provider attribution
- what evidence returned
- what truth class was claimed

If a future builder cannot reconstruct why the merge happened,
they will replace it with “whatever feels right,” and the platform will drift.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The merge-and-complete flow must:
- treat clarifications as incomplete control transactions
- preserve incomplete intent and missing fields in a session artifact
- merge answers conservatively using explicit anchors and candidate sets
- re-run validation, policy, and planning after merge
- require confirmation when answers expand risk
- execute only with evidence-based truth posture
- expire safely and refuse to merge stale context blindly
- prevent duplicate execution

The merge-and-complete flow must never:
- guess missing fields
- treat an answer as consent for expanded blast radius
- silently execute subsets of a compound request
- “complete” by narrative closure instead of verified evidence posture

Completion is not a feeling.
Completion is a lawful control transaction.

END OF 15.3


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 15.4
COMMANDS VS VIBE STATEMENTS

Humans do not always speak in commands.

They speak in:
- observations (“it’s cold”)
- feelings (“I hate when it’s this bright”)
- complaints (“this keeps turning off”)
- hopes (“I wish the kitchen lights were on”)
- sarcasm (“great, the alarm again”)
- narrative (“we’re heading out soon”)

Some of these are actionable.
Most are not.

If Ashby treats vibes as commands, it becomes unsafe.
If Ashby treats commands as vibes, it becomes useless.

This section defines the classification boundary between:
- a command (actionable intent)
- a vibe statement (non-actionable signal)
and how Ashby behaves in each case without guessing or overstepping.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1) DEFINITIONS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

COMMAND (ACTIONABLE INTENT)
A command is an utterance that expresses a user’s intent for Ashby to cause a change
(or perform a bounded operation), and contains enough structure to:
- identify an action type (capability)
- identify or resolve the target (scope + identity)
- satisfy safety policy (clarify/confirm as required)

Commands may be imperative (“turn on the lights”),
interrogative (“can you turn on the lights?”),
or declarative with explicit intent (“I want you to turn on the lights now.”).

VIBE STATEMENT (NON-ACTIONABLE SIGNAL)
A vibe statement expresses:
- a state preference,
- an emotional reaction,
- a complaint,
- a narrative,
- or an observation,
without explicitly requesting an action from Ashby.

A vibe statement may be useful context,
but it is not execution authorization.

Examples:
- “It’s too dark in here.”
- “This room feels stuffy.”
- “Ugh, it’s freezing.”
- “The lights are annoying.”
- “I hate how loud it is.”
- “It keeps doing that.”

Vibe statements may indicate:
- a problem
- a desire
- a likely follow-up command

But they are not a command unless the user actually asks.

ACTIONABLE QUESTION (CHECK/REPORT)
Some statements are not commands but are still actionable as a non-mutating operation:
- “Is the door locked?”
- “What’s the temperature in here?”
- “Did the lights turn off?”

These are requests for information.
They are not permission to change anything.

Ashby may answer them using HouseState/evidence with correct truth posture.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2) WHY THIS MATTERS (THE ASSISTANT FAILURE MODE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Most assistants fail in one of two ways:

A) OVER-ACTING
They treat vibes as permission and execute without explicit intent.
This creates “helpfulness harm.”

B) UNDER-ACTING
They refuse to recognize real commands unless phrased perfectly.

Ashby’s definition of correctness is:
- no guessing,
- no silent execution,
- and no denial of clear intent.

Therefore classification is governed by explicit rules.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
3) THE CLASSIFICATION RULE (THE ONE-LINE LAW)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Ashby must act only when it has:
- an explicit command OR an explicit actionable question,
- plus sufficient target resolution,
- plus policy permission (ACT vs CLARIFY vs CONFIRM vs REFUSE).

If the utterance is a vibe statement, Ashby must not execute.

Ashby may:
- respond,
- ask a clarifying question,
- propose options,
- or ask for explicit confirmation to convert a vibe into a command.

But it must not silently convert vibes into execution.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
4) COMMAND DETECTION: WHAT COUNTS AS “EXPLICIT”
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Ashby treats an utterance as a command when at least one of the following is true:

A) DIRECT IMPERATIVE
- “turn on the kitchen lights”
- “set the thermostat to 72”
- “lock the front door”
- “pause the music”

B) POLITE REQUEST WITH EXECUTION VERB
- “can you turn on the lights?”
- “could you lock the door?”
- “please set it to 72”

C) DECLARATIVE WITH DIRECTIVE INTENT
- “I want you to turn on the lights.”
- “I need you to lower the temperature.”
- “I’m asking you to do X.”

D) CONFIRMED PROPOSAL (VIBE → PROPOSAL → CONFIRM)
If the user confirms a proposed action:
- user: “it’s freezing”
- ashby: “Do you want me to raise the thermostat in here?”
- user: “yes”
Then the confirmation creates an explicit command.

What does not count as explicit:
- “I wish the lights were on.”
- “I’d love it if it was warmer.”
- “It’s too dark.”
These are vibes until the user explicitly requests action.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
5) VIBE STATEMENT HANDLING (WHAT ASHBY DOES INSTEAD OF ACTING)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

When the input is a vibe statement, Ashby must choose one of:

A) CLARIFY INTO A COMMAND
Ask whether the user wants an action, and if so, which one.

Example:
- user: “it’s too dark in here.”
- ashby: “Do you want me to turn on the lights in this room, or brighten them?”

The clarifier must:
- propose a small set of relevant actions
- remain scope-bound (“in this room” is a context phrase that must be anchored safely)
- avoid claiming that the system already knows the intended target if ambiguous

B) REPORT / DIAGNOSE (IF THE VIBE IS ABOUT A FAILED PRIOR ACTION)
If the user’s vibe statement is plausibly feedback on a prior attempt:
- “it’s still dark”
- “that didn’t work”
- “it turned back off”
Ashby must:
- refer to the most recent ActionResult evidence if available
- report what is known with correct truth posture
- ask whether to retry or troubleshoot
- never claim success if the evidence does not support it

C) OFFER NON-EXECUTING HELP
If the vibe is not resolvable into a small action set safely, Ashby may respond with:
- “Want me to change something?”
- “Do you want lights, temperature, or something else adjusted?”
without selecting a specific action.

The key: Ashby must not act.
It must convert the vibe into explicit intent first.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
6) “COMMAND-LIKE VIBES” (THE AMBIGUOUS MIDDLE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Some vibes are phrased like indirect commands:
- “It would be nice if the lights were on.”
- “I’d appreciate it if you made it warmer.”
- “It’d be great if the alarm stopped doing that.”

Ashby must treat these as incomplete commands:
- they imply a preference
- they do not explicitly authorize execution

Rule:
- treat as CLARIFY by default
- convert to a command only after explicit confirmation or follow-up request

Example:
- user: “It would be nice if the lights were on.”
- ashby: “Do you want me to turn on the lights in this room?”
- user: “yes” → now actionable

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
7) SAFE ACTION PROPOSALS (HOW MANY OPTIONS, HOW SPECIFIC)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

When converting vibe → command, Ashby must propose:
- the smallest set of plausible actions (usually 1–3)
- at the correct scope level
- without pretending the target is unique if it is not

Rules:
- If the target is uniquely anchored (e.g., “in here” with a confirmed active scope),
  proposals may be concrete (“turn on the living room lights”).
- If the target is not uniquely anchored, proposals must include a clarification step
  (“Which room—living room or kitchen?”).

Avoid:
- presenting a long menu
- executing a “best guess”
- asking vague questions that fail to reduce ambiguity

The goal is to elicit explicit intent, not to interrogate.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
8) RISK AND CONSENT (VIBE DOES NOT LOWER SAFETY THRESHOLDS)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A vibe statement must never reduce safety requirements.

If a proposed action is high-impact:
- access changes
- security mode changes
- money movement
- bulk modifications
Ashby must require confirmation even if the user “sounds frustrated.”

Emotional intensity is not authorization.

Examples:
- user: “This is driving me insane, just fix it!”
Ashby must:
- clarify what “fix” means
- identify the intended action
- require confirmation if the action is high-impact

Safety policy outranks tone.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
9) LANGUAGE POSTURE (HOW ASHBY SPEAKS ABOUT WHAT IT WILL DO)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

When input is a vibe:
- Ashby must not use completion language (“Done.”) because no action was taken.
- Ashby must not imply execution (“I’ll handle it.”) unless it is asking to do it.

Correct posture:
- “Do you want me to…?”
- “I can… if you want.”
- “If you mean X, I can do that.”

When input is a command but target is ambiguous:
- Ashby must CLARIFY, not pretend it knows.

When input is a question:
- Ashby answers with truth posture based on evidence and staleness.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
10) EDGE CASES (COMMON HUMAN PATTERNS)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A) RHETORICAL QUESTIONS
- “Why is it so cold in here?”
This may be:
- a genuine question about sensors/thermostat
- or a vibe statement
Ashby should:
- offer a report if it can measure (“I’m seeing 66°F in this room (LAST_KNOWN).”)
- then ask if the user wants an adjustment

B) SARCASM
- “Great, the lights are off again.”
Treat as feedback:
- check recent evidence
- ask if the user wants a corrective action

C) NARRATIVE CONTEXT
- “We’re going to bed.”
This is not a command.
Ashby may respond with:
- a suggested routine proposal (“Do you want me to run bedtime mode?”)
But must not execute without explicit confirmation unless a pre-authorized automation exists
(and that automation must be explicit and governed elsewhere in the Codex).

D) COMPLAINTS ABOUT AUTOMATIONS
- “It keeps turning off.”
This is not a command to disable.
Ashby must clarify:
- what is turning off
- whether the user wants it disabled
- and whether disabling changes security posture (confirmation may be required)

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Ashby must:
- distinguish commands from vibe statements deterministically
- act only on explicit commands or explicit actionable questions
- convert vibes into commands via clarification/confirmation, not guessing
- keep safety thresholds intact regardless of emotion or urgency
- use correct language posture: no implied execution when none occurred
- treat feedback vibes about prior attempts as evidence review + next-step clarification

Ashby must never:
- execute a best guess based on a vibe statement
- claim completion when no action occurred
- treat frustration as authorization
- smooth ambiguity into confident targeting
- reduce confirmation requirements because the user “sounds like they want it”

END OF 15.4


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 16.1
DEFINITION AND RESPONSIBILITIES

A module is an organ of Ashby.

The platform body is the engine, truth law, state law, scopes, identity, and execution seams.
Modules plug into that body to add meaning and capability without rewriting the body.

This section defines what a module is, what it is responsible for,
and what it is forbidden from doing.

If modules are allowed to:
- execute directly,
- store competing truth,
- bypass policy,
- or import vendor logic,
then Ashby collapses into a pile of special cases.

Modules exist to prevent that collapse.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1) DEFINITION: WHAT A MODULE IS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A MODULE is a bounded, removable capability component that:
- interprets intents within a domain
- plans actions as ActionRequests (WHAT)
- asks domain-specific clarifications when required
- never executes actions directly (HOW is elsewhere)
- never claims truth directly (Truth Gate is elsewhere)
- does not own long-term platform state (HouseState is elsewhere)

A module is not a provider.
A module is not a bridge.
A module is not an agent brain.
A module is not the engine.

A module is a disciplined translator from:
intent + context → plan + clarification needs.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2) WHAT MODULES OWN (THEIR RESPONSIBILITIES)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A module owns:

A) DOMAIN INTERPRETATION
- mapping user language and synonyms into domain concepts
- recognizing intents within its capability surface
- determining which capability contract(s) apply

B) PLANNING AS WHAT
- producing ActionRequests that describe intended effects
- choosing plan structure within its domain:
  - single action vs multi-step
  - required preconditions
  - expected verification requirements (as a request, not as truth)

C) CLARIFICATION QUESTIONS (DOMAIN-SPECIFIC)
- identifying what missing information prevents safe planning
- asking minimal, targeted questions that reduce ambiguity
- producing a “pending completion state” artifact for merge-and-complete flow

D) RISK ANNOTATION (INPUT TO POLICY, NOT POLICY ITSELF)
Modules may produce structured hints such as:
- “this action is high-impact”
- “this action changes access/security”
- “this action affects money”
These hints help policy choose CONFIRM vs ACT.

However:
- policy outcome selection belongs to the engine policy system
- modules must not override policy outcomes

E) CAPABILITY DECLARATION
A module must declare:
- the capability types it handles
- the interface contracts it uses
- the required evidence/verification expectations for success
(These expectations are still evaluated through Truth Gate.)

F) ERROR SURFACE NORMALIZATION (IN DOMAIN TERMS)
Modules may map low-level failure categories into domain-safe explanations,
but they must not invent causes.

“Why it failed” must remain evidence-derived.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
3) WHAT MODULES DO NOT OWN (THEIR FORBIDDEN RESPONSIBILITIES)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Modules must not own:

A) EXECUTION (HOW)
- modules must not call providers
- must not call bridges
- must not import vendor SDKs
- must not perform network IO as part of planning
Execution belongs to adapters/providers under controlled seams.

B) TRUTH (CERTAINTY)
- modules must not decide truth class
- must not use forbidden phrases as if verified
- must not upgrade weak evidence into strong claims
Truth belongs to Truth Gate.

C) PLATFORM STATE AUTHORITY
- modules must not write HouseState directly
- modules must not maintain a competing “module memory” that overrides HouseState
Modules may read HouseState to plan.
They may not redefine it.

D) SECURITY AND SECRETS
- modules must not read raw secrets
- modules must not store credentials
- modules must not log sensitive payloads
Modules may request that a connector/provider be used,
but secrets handling stays in secret systems.

E) PRODUCTIZATION LOGIC
- modules must not branch on product tiers
- modules must not implement entitlements checks as ad-hoc behavior
Availability is handled by profiles/flags/entitlements gates outside module meaning.

F) GOVERNANCE
- modules must not modify invariants
- modules must not bypass Control Sync requirements
Constitutional changes do not occur in modules.

If a module is doing any of the above, it is not a module.
It is a fork mechanism.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
4) MODULE INTERFACE: INPUTS AND OUTPUTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A correct module interface is shaped like:

INPUTS (READ-ONLY CONTEXT)
- normalized Intent (and any merge-completed payload)
- scope context (current scope, permissions)
- target resolution context (registry matches, ambiguity set)
- HouseState snapshot or scoped view (beliefs with staleness)
- profile/flag/entitlement availability (as “is this module available?”, not as behavior rewrite)
- session context (if this is a multi-turn continuation)

OUTPUTS (PLANNING ARTIFACTS)
- PlanCandidate:
  - one or more ActionRequests (WHAT)
  - constraints and prerequisites (e.g., required target binding)
  - required confirmations (as hints)
- ClarifyRequest:
  - a minimal question set
  - a structured “missing fields” schema
  - a pending completion artifact for merge-and-complete

The module does not output:
- “done”
- “executed”
- “verified”
Those are downstream consequences.

Modules plan.
They do not perform.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
5) DETERMINISM AND RECONSTRUCTABILITY
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Modules must be reconstructable from text and logs.

Rules:
- given the same normalized intent and the same context snapshot,
  a module should produce the same plan shape
- if a module uses heuristics, the heuristics must be explicit and testable
- any probabilistic behavior must be bounded and recorded (and is generally discouraged)

A module’s decisions must be explainable through:
- the intent
- the context
- the module rules

If a module’s plan cannot be explained, it cannot be trusted.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
6) MODULE REGISTRATION AND DISCOVERY
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Modules must be discoverable and manageable as discrete units.

At minimum, each module should have:
- module_key (stable identifier)
- version
- declared capabilities
- declared interface contract dependencies
- declared required connectors (if any)
- declared safety classification hints
- owner/maintainer metadata (if applicable)

The engine/router uses this registry to:
- determine which modules are available
- route intents to candidate modules
- respect profiles/entitlements without rewriting module meaning

Modules must not self-enable by side effect.
Availability is a gate, not a module behavior.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
7) HOW MODULES COOPERATE (WITHOUT BECOMING ENTANGLED)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Modules may coordinate through the platform body:
- by reading HouseState
- by emitting ActionRequests that affect shared devices/targets
- by producing artifacts through standard storage channels

Modules must not:
- call each other directly as dependencies
- assume another module is present
- import another module’s internal structures

Direct inter-module dependencies destroy removability.

If collaboration is needed, it must happen through:
- shared domain models
- shared interface contracts
- explicit router orchestration
not through hidden coupling.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Modules must:
- express WHAT only (planning)
- remain removable and isolated
- produce ActionRequests and/or clarification requirements
- read HouseState but never replace it
- respect scope and identity law
- provide domain interpretation without vendor/protocol leakage
- be auditable and explainable via logged inputs/outputs

Modules must never:
- execute actions directly or import providers/vendors
- decide truth class or claim verification
- bypass policy outcomes or confirmation requirements
- store secrets or leak sensitive data into logs/artifacts
- encode product-tier behavior as hidden forks
- create direct dependencies on other modules that break removability

END OF 16.1


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 16.2
REMOVABILITY GUARANTEES

Ashby is built to grow.
Growth without removability creates a monolith.
A monolith eventually becomes unmaintainable, un-migratable, and unrebuildable.

Removability is not “nice.”
It is a core resurrection constraint.

This section defines what Ashby guarantees when modules or agents are removed,
and the architecture rules required to make that guarantee true.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1) DEFINITION: WHAT “REMOVABILITY” MEANS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A component is removable when the platform can:
- disable it,
- uninstall it,
- or omit it from a build/profile,
without breaking core operation or corrupting core truth/state.

Removability does not mean:
- deleting history
- erasing artifacts
- pretending it never existed

Removability means:
- the body survives
- the intent router remains coherent
- the evidence and state systems remain lawful
- the system fails cleanly when a removed capability is requested

A removable module may stop existing.
Ashby must still function.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2) WHAT MUST REMAIN TRUE AFTER REMOVAL (THE GUARANTEES)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

After removing a module/agent, the platform guarantees:

A) ENGINE INTEGRITY
- policy outcomes still work
- Truth Gate still applies
- HouseState still persists and decays correctly
- logs and correlation still exist

B) ROUTING INTEGRITY
- the router will not route to missing modules
- missing modules are treated as unavailable surfaces, not errors in core

C) TRUTH INTEGRITY
- the system does not pretend it can execute removed capabilities
- it does not fabricate explanations
- it refuses or clarifies honestly

D) STATE INTEGRITY
- HouseState is not corrupted by removal
- beliefs remain as beliefs, with staleness
- any beliefs that relied on removed evidence sources degrade naturally

E) SECURITY INTEGRITY
- removal does not leak secrets
- removal does not leave privileged actions enabled accidentally

F) ARTIFACT DURABILITY
- artifacts previously created remain stored and exportable (subject to retention policy)
- removal does not destroy history silently

These guarantees prevent “uninstall means amnesia” and “uninstall breaks the platform.”

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
3) REMOVAL MODES (HOW REMOVAL HAPPENS)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

There are multiple legal removal modes:

A) DISABLE (SOFT REMOVAL)
- module is present on disk
- profile/flags/entitlements mark it unavailable
- router will not use it
- artifacts remain readable

B) UNINSTALL (HARD REMOVAL)
- module code is absent
- registry entry removed or marked absent
- router cannot load it

C) BUILD OMIT (DISTRIBUTION REMOVAL)
- a product profile excludes the module entirely
- used for product variants or minimal deployments

All modes must produce the same platform guarantee:
the body remains intact and behavior remains honest.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
4) USER-FACING BEHAVIOR WHEN A REMOVED CAPABILITY IS REQUESTED
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

When a user requests something that maps to a removed module:

Ashby must not:
- attempt execution
- attempt “partial execution” through unrelated modules
- claim failure due to device/provider if it never tried

Ashby must choose a lawful outcome:

A) REFUSE (FEATURE NOT AVAILABLE)
When the user clearly requests the removed capability:
- “I can’t do that here because that module isn’t enabled.”

B) CLARIFY (ALTERNATIVE PATH EXISTS)
If the user’s language could refer to a different available capability:
- “Do you mean X (available) or Y (not available)?”

C) CHAT (EXPLANATION / GUIDANCE)
Ashby may explain what would be required to enable the capability
without implying it can self-enable it.

The response must be explicit:
unavailable means unavailable.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
5) WHAT HAPPENS TO ARTIFACTS AND CONFIG AFTER REMOVAL
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Removal must not silently destroy stored material.

Rules:

A) ARTIFACTS REMAIN, BUT BECOME INERT
- previously generated reports, transcripts, exports, and logs remain in storage
- they remain accessible through generic viewers/exports
- the removed module is not required to read them

B) CONFIGURATION MAY REMAIN, BUT MUST NOT EXECUTE
- connector configurations, schedules, automation rules, etc.
  must be disabled or placed into a dormant state
- the platform must not continue running background execution on behalf of a removed module

C) ORPHANED REFERENCES MUST BE SAFE
If other components reference a removed module’s artifact:
- the reference must degrade gracefully (“artifact unavailable”)
- not crash the system
- not invent content

D) RETENTION POLICY CONTINUES TO APPLY
Removal does not bypass retention or privacy rules.
If artifacts must expire, they expire by policy, not by removal.

This preserves resurrection history without requiring the removed code.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
6) DEPENDENCY RULES THAT MAKE REMOVABILITY POSSIBLE
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Removability is guaranteed only if these rules are enforced:

A) NO MODULE MAY BE REQUIRED BY CORE
- engine/ must not depend on any module
- core boot must not require optional organs

B) NO INTER-MODULE HARD DEPENDENCIES
- modules must not import each other
- modules must not assume other modules are present
- cooperation happens through shared contracts and the router

C) EXECUTION IS SWAPPABLE
- modules do not embed provider logic
- providers can be removed/replaced without changing module meaning

D) STORAGE IS GENERIC
- artifacts are stored in common, stable formats
- artifacts are not stored as “module-private binary blobs” that only that module can read

E) IDENTITY IS PLATFORM-OWNED
- device_keys and mappings remain valid even if a module disappears
- removal does not “delete the world model”

If any of these rules are violated, removal becomes a breaking event.
Breaking removability violates Ashby’s definition.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
7) SAFE UNINSTALL CHECKLIST (WHAT MUST BE VERIFIED)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Before and after removal, the platform must be able to verify:

- the module is no longer routable
- no scheduled tasks/triggers created by the module remain active
- no provider routes remain pointing to removed components
- no secret handles are left referenced by active execution paths
- artifacts remain readable/exportable under policy
- HouseState remains coherent and decays normally
- logs still reconstruct outcomes correctly

Removal must be observable:
- a removal event should be logged with correlation_id and scope context
- without leaking sensitive configuration or secrets

A platform that cannot verify removal cannot guarantee it.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Ashby’s removability guarantees require:
- core boot and core law to be independent of any specific module
- strict isolation and no inter-module hard dependencies
- stable interface contracts and swappable providers
- generic, durable storage formats for artifacts
- identity continuity independent of module presence
- explicit disabling of background execution tied to removed modules
- honest refusal/clarification when removed capabilities are requested

Ashby forbids:
- modules becoming “core by accident”
- removal causing silent loss of history or state corruption
- orphan schedules continuing to execute after module removal
- using removal as a way to hide failures or erase traces
- coupling artifact readability to the presence of the original module code

Removability is the proof that Ashby is a platform, not a pile of features.

END OF 16.2


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 16.3
Module Lifecycle

A module is not “some code in a folder.”
A module is a governed unit of meaning and capability that must survive:
- installs
- upgrades
- deprecations
- removals
- profile changes
- provider swaps
- and time

If the lifecycle is vague, modules become:
- permanent by accident (violates removability)
- fragile under updates (breaks resurrection)
- or unsafe under drift (truth posture erodes)

This section defines the lifecycle of a module as a first-class platform concern.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1) WHAT “LIFECYCLE” MEANS (FOUR DISTINCT THINGS)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A module has four distinct “existences” that must not be confused:

A) MODULE CODE
- the implementation of domain interpretation and planning (WHAT)
- versioned, testable, removable

B) MODULE DEFINITION (REGISTRY IDENTITY)
- module_key (stable, permanent identifier)
- declared capability surface
- dependency declarations (contracts, connectors, providers by interface)
- safety classification hints (inputs to policy)

C) MODULE ENABLEMENT (AVAILABILITY STATE)
- whether the module is enabled in a given profile/scope
- governed by profiles, flags, entitlements
- never by “code presence alone”

D) MODULE EFFECTS (ARTIFACTS + STATE IMPACT)
- artifacts created by module-driven actions
- HouseState beliefs that were updated based on evidence produced through module plans
- logs and audit trails associated with module execution

Lifecycle discipline exists to keep these four layers consistent.

Removing code must not erase history.
Changing enablement must not mutate meaning.
Upgrading a module must not silently rewrite truth.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2) LIFECYCLE STATES (THE REQUIRED STATE MACHINE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A module must be describable as moving through these states:

1) DESIGNED
- module’s domain, capabilities, and boundaries are defined
- interface contracts chosen (but no execution code in module)

2) IMPLEMENTED
- module planning logic exists
- no provider calls, no vendor SDKs, no IO in module code

3) VERIFIED (MODULE-LEVEL)
- module passes:
  - boundary enforcement tests (no forbidden dependencies)
  - deterministic planning tests (same input → same plan shape)
  - clarification correctness tests (asks, doesn’t guess)
  - safety hint correctness tests (high-impact annotations)

4) REGISTERED
- module is added to the platform module registry with:
  - module_key
  - version
  - capability declarations
  - contract dependencies
  - connector requirements (by handle, not by secret)

5) AVAILABLE (BUILD-TIME)
- module exists in the distribution (packaged into the build)
- but may still be disabled by profile or entitlement

6) ENABLED (RUNTIME / PROFILE-SCOPED)
- profile allows it
- scope permits it
- router can select it for matching intents

7) ACTIVE (IN USE)
- module is being routed to
- it is producing plans and/or clarifications
- it is generating ActionRequests that lead to execution through providers

8) DEPRECATED (SOFT EXIT)
- module remains present but is discouraged or scheduled for removal
- module may refuse new setups while still supporting existing artifacts
- module may point to successor modules (through router policy/aliasing)

9) DISABLED (SOFT REMOVAL)
- module not routable (profile/flags/entitlements)
- code may still exist
- all background tasks and triggers owned by the module must be disabled

10) UNINSTALLED (HARD REMOVAL)
- module code absent from build
- registry marked absent
- artifacts remain readable (generic storage)
- platform remains intact (see 16.2)

This state model is not “process.”
It is how Ashby avoids accidental permanence and accidental drift.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
3) INSTALLATION: WHAT “ADDING A MODULE” REQUIRES
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A module is not “installed” when code is merged.
A module is installed when the platform can safely route to it.

Minimum install requirements:

A) REGISTERED IDENTITY
- module_key created and recorded
- version set
- capabilities declared explicitly
- contract dependencies declared (interfaces only)

B) BOUNDARY COMPLIANCE
- module imports must not cross into:
  - providers/
  - bridges/
  - vendor SDKs
  - secrets
  - network IO
- module may import:
  - domain models
  - schemas/contracts
  - safe utilities (pure logic)

C) HARNESSED EVIDENCE EXPECTATIONS
- module must declare what success looks like as evidence expectations
- the module does not “decide truth,” but it must state verification intent
  (e.g., “after turning on, readback should reflect ON”)

D) ROUTER DISCOVERY
- router must be able to:
  - discover the module
  - understand its capability coverage
  - decide when to call it

E) SAFE DEFAULT ENABLEMENT
- newly installed modules are not globally enabled by default
- enablement must be profile-scoped and explicit

If these are not satisfied, adding the module is incomplete design, not progress.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
4) ENABLEMENT: HOW A MODULE BECOMES ROUTABLE
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Enablement is where platforms quietly fork if not governed.

Rules:

A) ENABLEMENT IS EXTERNAL
- modules do not self-enable
- availability is controlled by profiles/flags/entitlements, not module code paths

B) ENABLEMENT IS SCOPE-BOUND
- a module may be enabled in one scope and disabled in another
- scope capability maps must be explicit

C) ENABLEMENT DOES NOT CHANGE MEANING
- enabling/disabling changes what can be executed
- it does not change truth posture
- it does not change WHAT/HOW law
- it does not change how evidence is classified

D) ENABLEMENT MUST BE AUDITABLE
- a routing decision should be explainable as:
  “this module was eligible and enabled under this profile/scope”
- not as:
  “it just happened”

Enablement is a gate. Not a behavioral rewrite mechanism.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
5) RUNTIME USE: HOW MODULES PARTICIPATE IN EXECUTION
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

At runtime, modules participate only through planning.

A) INPUTS
A module receives:
- normalized intent
- current scope context
- target ambiguity set (if any)
- HouseState snapshot (beliefs, staleness)
- availability gating context (enabled/disabled)

B) OUTPUTS
A module outputs:
- ActionRequest plan(s) (WHAT)
- ClarifyRequest if required
- structured hints to policy (risk categories), never policy outcomes

C) EXECUTION IS OUTSIDE THE MODULE
A module must never:
- call a provider
- call a bridge
- run network IO
- mutate HouseState directly

The adapter/provider layer executes ActionRequests and returns ActionResults.
Truth Gate evaluates.
HouseState updates as belief.

This preserves the sacred separation: modules define meaning, not reality.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
6) UPGRADES: VERSIONING WITHOUT AMNESIA
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Modules will change. That is expected.

The lifecycle problem is avoiding silent semantic rewrites.

A) MODULE_KEY NEVER CHANGES
- module_key is permanent
- if meaning changes so radically that it is “a different thing,”
  it is a new module_key (and the old one is deprecated)

B) VERSION INCREMENTS ARE MEANINGFUL
- patch: implementation fixes, no semantic changes
- minor: expanded capabilities, backward compatible
- major: potentially breaking changes in planning semantics or contracts

C) UPGRADE MUST PRESERVE ARTIFACT READABILITY
Artifacts created earlier must remain readable and interpretable after upgrade.
If the module previously stored proprietary blob formats, that was already a violation
(see 16.2 storage durability rules).

D) UPGRADE MUST PRESERVE ROUTER DISCOVERY
Capability declarations and contract dependencies must remain valid or be migrated.

E) UPGRADE MUST HAVE TEST PROOF
At minimum:
- old intents still route correctly (or fail with explicit deprecation language)
- clarification behavior remains safe (no new guessing)
- high-impact actions remain confirmation-gated by policy inputs
- boundary tests remain passing

Upgrades are not “ship and pray.”
They are governed changes with regression immunity.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
7) DEPRECATION: HOW A MODULE DIES WITHOUT BREAKING THE BODY
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Deprecation is a planned exit, not an emergency removal.

A module becomes deprecated when:
- a better module replaces it
- an integration path is being removed
- the domain meaning needs re-architecture
- the module violates new invariants (rare; requires governance elsewhere)

Deprecation rules:

A) DEPRECATION MUST BE DECLARED
- explicit deprecated flag in module registry
- optional sunset date or conditions

B) DEPRECATION MUST PRESERVE HISTORY
- artifacts remain accessible
- previous logs remain reconstructable
- HouseState beliefs remain beliefs and decay normally

C) DEPRECATION SHOULD PROVIDE A SUCCESSOR PATH
- router may prefer successor modules
- user-facing language may recommend successor capability
- no silent rerouting without explanation when behavior changes

D) DEPRECATION MUST NOT HIDE FAILURES
- “deprecated” is not a way to avoid fixing a safety bug
- safety bugs are fixed; deprecation is separate

Deprecation is the civilized alternative to “we ripped it out and everything broke.”

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
8) DISABLEMENT AND UNINSTALL: THE REMOVAL CONTRACT (LINK TO 16.2)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Disablement and uninstall are governed by removability guarantees.

When disabling/uninstalling a module, the platform must ensure:

A) IT IS NO LONGER ROUTABLE
- router will never select it
- “available but disabled” is treated as unavailable

B) IT HAS NO ACTIVE BACKGROUND EFFECTS
- schedules, triggers, long-running loops, watchers created by the module’s domain
  must be disabled or reassigned
- no orphan execution continues after removal

C) CONFIG BECOMES DORMANT
- module-related connector configs may remain but must not execute
- secret handles must not be referenced by live execution paths

D) USER-FACING BEHAVIOR IS HONEST
- refusal or clarification based on unavailability
- no “I tried and it failed” if it never tried

E) THE BODY REMAINS WHOLE
- engine, Truth Gate, HouseState, routing, logging remain intact

If removal breaks the body, the architecture already violated removability.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
9) MIGRATIONS AND SEMANTIC REALIGNMENT (WHEN MODULE CHANGE AFFECTS STATE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Modules are not supposed to own state.
But module-driven meaning can affect how beliefs are interpreted.

When a module change affects:
- entity naming rules
- target resolution heuristics (within allowed boundaries)
- capability mapping expectations
- verification readback interpretation
it may require structural realignment playbooks.

Rule:
- if state meaning changes, the change must be explicit and auditable
- migrations must be rehearsed and testable
- identity continuity must be preserved

Modules do not “rewrite the world model” casually.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
10) REQUIRED TEST SURFACES (THE IMMUNE SYSTEM FOR MODULE LIFECYCLE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A module lifecycle is only as strong as its tests.

Minimum required tests:

A) BOUNDARY TESTS
- fail if module imports forbidden layers (providers, bridges, vendor SDKs)

B) INTENT→PLAN TESTS
- representative intents produce expected plan shape
- avoids silent semantic drift

C) CLARIFICATION TESTS
- ambiguous targets cause CLARIFY
- contextual phrases are anchored safely (see 15.2)
- vibe statements do not trigger execution (see 15.4)

D) SAFETY HINT TESTS
- high-impact actions are annotated correctly
- policy can choose CONFIRM when required

E) UPGRADE/DEPRECATION TESTS
- ensure older artifacts remain readable
- ensure router discovery remains stable
- ensure deprecation language is honest

If a module cannot be tested, it cannot be safely evolved.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A module lifecycle must:
- preserve module_key permanence and meaningful versioning
- keep enablement external and scope-bound
- keep execution outside modules (WHAT/HOW separation)
- preserve truth/state integrity across installs, upgrades, and removals
- preserve artifact readability and history durability
- disable background effects when a module is disabled/uninstalled
- remain auditable: routing and availability decisions must be explainable
- be enforced by boundary and regression tests

A module lifecycle must never:
- allow a module to become “core by accident”
- allow upgrades to silently rewrite meaning or truth posture
- erase history under removal
- leave orphan execution running after disable/uninstall
- use enablement as a hidden product fork mechanism

END OF 16.3


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 17.1
SIMPLE REACTIVE MODULES (LIGHTS-LIKE)

A simple reactive module is the archetype of “instant control”:
a user issues a command, the module produces an ActionRequest,
the adapter attempts execution immediately, and Ashby reports the outcome
under Action-Gated Truth posture.

“Lights-like” is representative, not exclusive.
This archetype covers any domain where:
- actions are quick
- actions are stateless in the sense of “start → end” (no long-running lifecycle)
- the primary control surface is a small set of discrete attributes
  (on/off, level, color, mode, etc.)
- users iterate through short follow-ups (“a little brighter”, “turn it off”)

Simple reactive modules exist to make Ashby feel immediate without becoming reckless.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
CORE CHARACTERISTICS (DEFINING TRAITS)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A module qualifies as simple reactive when:

1) SINGLE-SHOT EXECUTION
The module’s primary actions complete as one execution attempt.
There is no ongoing “running” state owned by the module.

2) DISCRETE, LOW-ENTROPY STATE SHAPE
The domain state is described by a small attribute set, such as:
- power: on/off
- level: 0..N (brightness, volume, etc.)
- mode: one of a small set

3) FOLLOW-UP HEAVY INTERACTION
Users often issue follow-ups that are incomplete without continuity:
- “brighter”
- “again”
- “turn it off”
This requires session support, but sessions do not change law.

4) HIGH MULTI-TARGET FREQUENCY
Users commonly target groups:
- “bedroom lights”
- “all lights”
This requires per-target reporting and blast radius safety.

5) EVIDENCE IS OFTEN LIMITED
Readback may be unavailable or delayed.
Truth posture must remain honest even when verification is imperfect.

A simple reactive module is judged by behavior and contracts, not by vendor.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
CANONICAL RESPONSIBILITIES OF A SIMPLE REACTIVE MODULE
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A simple reactive module must:

- Declare supported intent types and action types (vendor-neutral)
- Normalize domain parameters into canonical internal units
- Validate requests against domain constraints and policy inputs
- Produce ActionRequests with explicit targets and parameters
- Interpret ActionResults into truthful outcomes (VERIFIED / LAST_KNOWN / FAILED / UNKNOWN)
- Provide session semantics appropriate for iterative follow-ups (14.x law applies)
- Never execute vendor calls directly
- Never claim truth outside Truth Gate

A simple reactive module does not own governance.
It participates in governance.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
REPRESENTATIVE INTENTS AND ACTIONS (LIGHTS-LIKE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

These are representative examples; they do not define a required inventory.

Common intent shapes:
- power.on / power.off
- level.set(value)
- level.adjust(delta)
- color.set(hue/sat/ct) (optional)
- scene.activate(name) (optional, if declared)

Common action types:
- lights.turn_on
- lights.turn_off
- lights.set_level
- lights.adjust_level

Rules:
- intent shapes must be stable labels (tests depend on them)
- action types must be stable labels (adapters depend on them)
- action types must carry canonical parameters, not raw text

“Adjust” must not be treated as “set” unless explicitly converted by policy.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
CANONICAL PARAMETER NORMALIZATION
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Simple reactive modules must normalize user language into canonical units.

Representative normalization patterns:

A) LEVEL SCALE
If the platform uses a canonical scale (e.g., 0..1000):
- convert percentages, “half,” “max,” and other phrases into that scale
- clamp or gate extreme values using policy

B) DELTA STEPS
Relative phrases (“a little brighter”) must map to policy-defined step sizes.
They must not map to invented deltas.

C) CAPABILITY-AWARE NORMALIZATION (VENDOR-NEUTRAL)
Normalization may consult internal capability flags (not vendor metadata) such as:
- supports_level
- supports_color
- supports_temperature
If a capability is unsupported:
- the module must refuse or degrade safely (e.g., offer on/off only)
- the module must not pretend to set an unsupported attribute

Normalization is about meaning, not about hardware.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TARGETING AND GROUPS (SCOPE-BOUND)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Simple reactive modules often act on multiple targets.

Rules:
- targets must be resolved inside a scope
- target resolution must obey the priority order (15.x law)
- groups (“bedroom lights”, “all lights”) must be declared or resolvable safely
- per-target execution and reporting are mandatory

A single user command may result in:
- one ActionRequest with multiple targets, or
- multiple ActionRequests if policy requires separation

However:
- splitting must not be silent
- splitting must not change meaning
- the user must not be misled into thinking one thing happened when another did

If max_targets is exceeded:
- the correct output is CONFIRM or CLARIFY, not partial execution.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SESSIONS FOR SIMPLE REACTIVE MODULES
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Simple reactive modules are session-heavy.

A lights-like session exists to anchor follow-ups:
- “it”
- “again”
- “brighter”
- “the other one”

Rules:
- sessions are scope-bound and expirable (14.6)
- sessions do not upgrade truth (14.1)
- expired sessions cannot anchor pronouns (15.1)
- sessions must not expand target sets

A simple reactive module may define:
- session_type = “lights”
- stored target_set
- last_intent shape (bounded)
- last requested parameters (interaction continuity only)

If no lawful anchor exists, the module must CLARIFY.
“Helpful guessing” is forbidden.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
HOUSESTATE INTERACTION (BELIEF, NOT ASSUMPTION)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Simple reactive modules often update a small set of state attributes.

Representative attributes:
- power: on/off
- level: integer scale
- color: optional

Rules:
- HouseState updates must be evidence-bound
- successful command issuance does not automatically mean VERIFIED
- LAST_KNOWN may be used only when justified by the Truth Gate
- failures and uncertain delivery must degrade confidence appropriately
- NO-OP must not upgrade belief

If readback exists:
- use it to verify
If readback does not exist:
- prefer LAST_KNOWN posture with explicit uncertainty
- degrade to UNKNOWN as freshness decays

State integrity is how simple modules remain honest over time.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
VERIFICATION REALITY (COMMON LIMITATION)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

In many lights-like domains, verification may be:
- immediate (device reports state)
- delayed (eventually consistent)
- unavailable (write-only)

A simple reactive module must never:
- present write-only outcomes as VERIFIED
- use “done” language under LAST_KNOWN or UNKNOWN
- collapse mixed truth into one narrative

Per-target truth posture is required.

The module’s job is to preserve honesty under imperfect evidence.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
FAILURE MODES AND SAFE REPORTING
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Common failure classes in simple reactive domains include:
- timeout/unreachable
- auth_failed (integration needs re-auth)
- capability_mismatch (unsupported attribute)
- invalid_target (stale registry)
- partial success across targets

Rules:
- failures must be reported explicitly (13.4)
- unknown delivery must not be spoken as certain state
- partial successes must be reported per-target
- provide safe next steps when possible:
  retry, refresh, narrow targets, re-auth path

A simple reactive module must never smooth failure into success.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SAFETY AND CONSENT (BLAST RADIUS)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Lights-like actions seem harmless until they are broad.

Turning off “all lights” can be disruptive or unsafe in some contexts.

Rules:
- blast radius policy applies to simple reactive modules
- confirmation gates apply when configured thresholds are exceeded
- defaults must not broaden actions invisibly
- “all” must be declared or confirmed, not inferred

Simple reactive does not mean “low risk.”
It means “short-lived.”

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
IDEMPOTENCY AND DUPLICATE HANDLING (REACTION STORMS)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Reactive domains are prone to duplicate submissions.

Rules:
- idempotency must exist at the platform layer
- duplicate suppression must not claim repeated execution
- deliberate retries are allowed when asked
- duplicate handling must remain auditable

A reactive module must remain safe under retries and resubmits.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
REPRESENTATIVE MODULE CONTRACT (NON-BINDING SHAPE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

This is representative, not prescriptive.

A lights-like module typically exposes:

- can_handle(intent) → bool
- normalize(intent) → normalized_intent
- plan(normalized_intent, scope, policy, sessions, state) → plan_outcome
  where plan_outcome is:
  - ACT with ActionRequest(s), or
  - CLARIFY with a pending gate, or
  - CONFIRM with a pending gate, or
  - REFUSE with reason label

The engine remains the arbiter of policy outcomes.
The module provides domain understanding.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Simple reactive modules must:
- remain vendor-neutral and removable
- normalize parameters deterministically
- produce explicit targets and parameters in ActionRequests
- support safe follow-ups via sessions without guessing
- preserve Action-Gated Truth per target
- respect blast radius, confirmation, and clarification law
- remain auditable

Simple reactive modules must never:
- execute vendor actions directly
- treat write-only outcomes as VERIFIED
- upgrade truth due to fluency or convenience
- expand targets through contextual language
- smooth partial failure into blanket success
- bypass policy, scope, session expiry, or Truth Gate constraints

This archetype is the baseline.
If Ashby cannot do this safely, Ashby cannot scale.

END OF 17.1


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 17.2
TIME-BOUND CONTROL MODULES (IRRIGATION-LIKE)

A time-bound control module governs actions that start, persist over time,
and terminate later.

“Irrigation-like” is representative, not exhaustive.
This archetype covers any domain where:
- the user initiates a run
- the run has a duration or an expected end condition
- “stop” and “how long is left” are first-class follow-ups
- duplicate execution can cause real harm

Time-bound control modules exist to make long-running actions safe,
auditable, and interruptible without pretending certainty.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DEFINING TRAITS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A module qualifies as time-bound control when the domain has these traits:

1) START/STOP SEMANTICS
Actions are not instantaneous toggles.
They have lifecycle: start → running → stop.

2) TIME AS A PARAMETER
Duration or end time is part of the action meaning.
“Water for 15 minutes” is materially different from “water.”

3) INTERRUPTIBILITY IS REQUIRED
The user must be able to:
- stop
- extend
- shorten
- query remaining time
without re-specifying everything.

4) SAFETY BOUNDARIES ARE STRONGER
Overruns, duplicates, or missing stop commands can be dangerous.

5) STATE EVOLUTION WITHOUT USER INPUT
Time itself changes the correct belief:
a run that began 10 minutes ago is different from one that began now.

Time-bound modules must model time explicitly, not implicitly.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
CANONICAL RESPONSIBILITIES
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A time-bound control module must:

- Define stable intent shapes and action types (vendor-neutral)
- Normalize time expressions into canonical units (seconds, timestamps)
- Enforce domain constraints as policy inputs (max duration, max zones, cooldowns)
- Produce ActionRequests with explicit:
  - target_set
  - start/stop intent
  - duration / end_time (when applicable)
- Maintain a domain session type for follow-ups (14.3 archetype)
- Update HouseState with evidence-bound run state and confidence
- Treat “expected end” as expectation, not as verified reality
- Preserve Action-Gated Truth posture for:
  - start success
  - stop success
  - current running state

A time-bound module never claims “it is still running” without evidence.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
REPRESENTATIVE INTENTS AND ACTIONS (IRRIGATION-LIKE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

These are representative, not exhaustive.

Intent shapes commonly required:
- run.start(duration | end_time)
- run.stop()
- run.extend(delta)
- run.shorten(delta)
- run.status()

Representative action types:
- irrigation.start
- irrigation.stop
- irrigation.extend
- irrigation.query_status

Rules:
- “extend” and “shorten” must remain bounded by safety policy.
- “status” is not an action on the world; it is an observation request.
  It must follow truth posture rules for evidence and freshness.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TIME NORMALIZATION (HUMAN LANGUAGE TO CANONICAL TIME)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Time-bound modules must normalize time deterministically.

Required behaviors:

A) DURATION PARSING
Examples (representative):
- “for 10 minutes” → duration_seconds = 600
- “for an hour” → duration_seconds = 3600
- “for a bit” → forbidden unless policy defines a step; otherwise CLARIFY

B) END-TIME PARSING
Examples:
- “until 7pm” → end_time = timestamp
- “until I say stop” → requires explicit policy support; otherwise CLARIFY

C) RELATIVE ADJUSTMENTS
“add 5 minutes” / “a little longer”:
- map deltas to configured step sizes or explicit numeric deltas
- never invent deltas

D) SAFETY CLAMPING AND GATING
If duration exceeds configured maximum:
- outcome is CONFIRM (if policy allows with consent) or REFUSE
- never silently clamp and proceed

Time semantics must be explicit to be safe.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
RUN STATE, EXPECTATION, AND TRUTH
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Time-bound modules must separate:

- COMMAND ISSUANCE (an attempt to start/stop)
- BELIEF ABOUT RUNNING (HouseState)
- EXPECTED END (time-derived expectation)
- VERIFIED STATE (evidence-derived truth)

Rules:

1) START DOES NOT IMPLY VERIFIED RUNNING
A successful start request may yield:
- VERIFIED running only if a readback confirms run state
- otherwise LAST_KNOWN running, with confidence decay

2) EXPECTED END IS NOT EVIDENCE
A timer reaching “expected end” does not prove the run stopped.
It only changes what is plausible.

At expected end:
- if there is no stop verification, belief must degrade.
- the system must not claim “it stopped” without evidence.

3) STOP IS ITS OWN ACTION WITH ITS OWN VERIFICATION
Stopping must be modeled as:
- an ActionRequest
- an ActionResult
- a truth posture outcome

4) RUNNING QUERIES MUST BE HONEST
If the system cannot verify current run state:
- it must present uncertainty and offer a refresh
- it must not narrate assumed continuity

Time-bound control makes lying tempting.
This archetype forbids it.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
HOUSESTATE INTERACTION (TIME-SENSITIVE BELIEFS)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Time-bound modules typically update HouseState attributes such as:

- run.active: true/false/unknown
- run.started_at: timestamp
- run.expected_end: timestamp (expectation, not verification)
- run.last_verified_at: timestamp (if applicable)
- run.confidence: level per platform law

Rules:
- “expected_end” may exist even when verification is unavailable.
- confidence must decay over time.
- restart must degrade confidence or force refresh (12.4, 12.6 law applies).
- mixed reality across targets is common and must be preserved.

HouseState must remain resilient to missed stop events and manual intervention.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SESSIONS FOR TIME-BOUND CONTROL
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Time-bound modules must support a time-bound session archetype.

A session exists to anchor:
- “stop”
- “extend by 10”
- “how long is left”
- “do the next zone”
without requiring restatement of targets.

Rules:
- sessions are scope-bound and expirable (14.6)
- expired sessions cannot authorize stop/extend actions
- sessions must not broaden target sets
- sessions must not override safety limits

If the user says “stop” with no lawful anchor:
- CLARIFY which run or which target_set.

A time-bound session is continuity, not control authority.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DUPLICATE PREVENTION (CRITICAL IN THIS ARCHETYPE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Duplicate “start” requests are hazardous.

Rules:
- idempotency must suppress duplicate starts within a policy window
- suppression must not claim the run restarted if it did not
- deliberate retries must be explicit (“start it again anyway”)

If a start request arrives while a run is believed active:
- the module must treat it as:
  - a duplicate request, or
  - a modification request (extend/shorten),
  depending on policy and phrasing.
- it must not silently start a second overlapping run unless explicitly supported.

Overlapping runs are high-risk by default.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
FAILURE MODES AND SAFE HANDLING
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Common failure modes include:
- start failed (valve unreachable)
- stop failed (cannot terminate safely)
- partial success across zones
- stale registry targets
- inability to verify current run state

Rules:
- failures must be reported per target
- “unknown” delivery must not be narrated as “running”
- stopping failures must be elevated as safety-critical when appropriate
- safe next steps may include:
  refresh, retry, narrow, or operator intervention

Time-bound domains require honest escalation, not smoothing.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SAFETY AND CONSENT
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Time-bound actions require stronger safety boundaries.

Representative policy inputs:
- max_duration per target and per group
- cooldown windows between runs
- max simultaneous targets
- confirmation thresholds for broad actions
- incident-mode tightening

Rules:
- long durations require confirmation when configured
- “until I say stop” must be explicitly allowed; otherwise CLARIFY/REFUSE
- defaults must never select targets for irrigation-like actions
  unless the default is explicitly configured and disclosed

Time-bound control is not a convenience domain.
It is a “damage if wrong” domain.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
REPRESENTATIVE MODULE CONTRACT (NON-BINDING SHAPE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Representative capabilities for a time-bound module:

- normalize_time(intent) → canonical_time_params
- plan_run(intent, scope, policy, sessions, state) → plan_outcome
- summarize_status(state, freshness) → status_outcome

Where plan_outcome is:
- ACT with ActionRequest(s), or
- CLARIFY with a pending gate, or
- CONFIRM with a pending gate, or
- REFUSE with reason label

The engine remains the arbiter of policy and truth.
The module supplies domain semantics.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Time-bound control modules must:
- model run lifecycle explicitly (start/running/stop)
- normalize time deterministically
- treat expected end as expectation, not verification
- preserve truth posture under missing readback
- prevent unsafe duplicates and overlapping runs by default
- support safe follow-ups through sessions without expanding targets
- remain auditable and vendor-neutral

Time-bound control modules must never:
- claim “still running” without evidence
- claim “stopped” because a timer elapsed
- silently clamp durations and proceed
- permit overlapping runs without explicit consent and policy support
- smooth partial success into blanket success
- bypass safety, scope, session expiry, or Truth Gate constraints

This archetype is where “helpful assumptions” cause harm.
Ashby forbids them.

END OF 17.2


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 17.3
SENSOR-DRIVEN MODULES

A sensor-driven module is a module whose primary output is evidence.

It ingests observations (from sensors, telemetry, or derived signals),
updates HouseState, and enables:
- truthful answers about the world
- policy decisions based on evidence
