- stale data causing wrong action

Canonical response behavior:
- preserve partial results explicitly
- never claim completion when ActionResult indicates failure/unknown
- never retry an irreversible mutation without idempotency safeguards
- never hide which system succeeded vs failed
- if a mutation is ambiguous, require clarification/confirmation

Business domains require explicitness.
Fluency is not acceptable as a substitute.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
12) REMOVABILITY (THE INVENTORY AGENT IS OPTIONAL)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The inventory agent must be removable.

Rules:
- disabling it removes inventory-specific intents and workflows
- disabling it must not break Ashby core or unrelated modules
- artifacts remain accessible only through explicit storage tooling
- connector integrations may remain for other modules, but inventory logic is gone

Ashby is a platform.
Inventory is one product surface.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

An inventory-style agent must:
- operate as a removable module inside Ashby
- use connectors/adapters for external system interaction
- preserve audit trails and reasons for all mutations
- use idempotency safeguards for irreversible operations
- represent inventory state as evidence-bearing belief with staleness
- surface contradictions explicitly (mixed reality)
- require confirmation for high-impact actions when policy dictates
- store artifacts via StorageAdapter and keep secrets out of artifacts/logs

An inventory-style agent must never:
- bypass connector interfaces to call vendor APIs directly
- treat one external system as unquestionable truth
- silently overwrite counts without evidence and audit
- place orders or post accounting changes without required confirmations
- hide partial failures or collapse mixed outcomes into a single narrative
- rely on stale data while presenting certainty

END OF 30.2


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 31.1
Bridge-First Strategy

Bridges exist to ship.

Native ownership exists to endure.

Bridge-first strategy is the disciplined path that allows Ashby to:
- deliver capability quickly
- remain modular and truth-bound
- avoid permanent dependency on third-party integration engines
- migrate toward native vendor integrations over time without collapse

Bridge-first is not a shortcut.
It is a deliberately temporary posture with a defined exit.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1) DEFINITIONS (BRIDGE, NATIVE, ADAPTER)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

BRIDGE
A temporary integration engine that already supports many vendors or protocols,
used as an early capability source.

Examples are illustrative:
- Home Assistant
- protocol aggregators
- ecosystem hubs
- intermediary cloud platforms

Bridges live in bridges/.
They are explicitly expected to shrink over time.

NATIVE VENDOR INTEGRATION
An Ashby-owned implementation of a vendor/provider interface, stored in native_vendor/.
Native ownership is the long-term path.

ADAPTER
A capability-layer executor selected by the router.
Adapters do not define reality or reason.
They translate ActionRequests into execution attempts and return ActionResults.

Canonical relationship:
- modules define WHAT
- adapters execute HOW
- bridges and native_vendor are HOW sources behind providers
- the engine remains vendor-agnostic and bridge-agnostic

A bridge must never become “part of the engine.”
A native integration must never force vendor logic into the module.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2) WHY BRIDGE-FIRST IS REQUIRED (EARLY REALITY)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Early-stage systems fail when they attempt native ownership too soon.

Reasons:
- vendor ecosystems are large and inconsistent
- protocol details are sharp and time-consuming
- hardware diversity creates endless edge cases
- shipping requires breadth before depth

Bridge-first allows:
- broad device coverage quickly
- rapid iteration on domain modeling and policy
- early field learning without locking architecture into vendor assumptions

However, bridge-first is safe only under strict constraints.
Without those constraints, the bridge becomes the platform.

Ashby must never become a front-end to a bridge.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
3) THE BRIDGE-FIRST INVARIANTS (NON-NEGOTIABLE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A) OPTIONALITY
A bridge must be optional.
Disabling the bridge must not break:
- the engine
- the Truth Gate
- HouseState
- unrelated modules

It may reduce available capabilities.
It must not collapse the platform.

B) ISOLATION
Bridge code must remain isolated in bridges/.
No engine code may import bridge internals.
No module code may import bridge internals.

If a module references a bridge, architecture has failed.

C) VENDOR-AGNOSTIC SURFACES
The presence of a bridge must not leak vendor identity into:
- engine policy logic
- module meaning logic
- canonical Intent shapes
- capability labels

Vendors are replaceable.
Capabilities are stable.

D) EVIDENCE, NOT TRUTH
Bridge outputs are evidence only.
They must be converted into platform truth by Truth Gate.

A bridge is not allowed to define:
- what exists
- what happened
- what is verified

E) STABLE DEVICE IDENTITY
Ashby must own device identity (device_keys) independent of bridge entity ids.

When the bridge changes, Ashby’s identity must remain stable.

F) EXIT PATH IS REQUIRED
Every bridge integration must have:
- a defined native replacement path
- a migration plan for identities and state
- a “strangler” strategy (replace piece by piece)

Bridge-first without an exit is permanent delegation.
Ashby refuses permanent delegation.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
4) THE ROLE OF BRIDGES IN THE ARCHITECTURE SHAPE
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Bridges sit below the domain model, below the engine, and below modules.

They are execution sources behind provider interfaces.

Canonical layering:
- domain/ defines what exists (entities, capabilities, scopes)
- engine/ interprets intent and plans actions
- modules/ implement domain features (WHAT)
- interfaces/ define capability contracts
- adapters/ implement those contracts
- bridges/ may act as one provider behind adapters
- native_vendor/ may act as another provider behind adapters

The key point:
modules call interfaces, not bridges.
adapters call providers, not modules.

Bridge presence should be invisible to module logic.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
5) STRANGLER THINKING (HOW BRIDGES ARE REPLACED SAFELY)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Bridge-first is implemented as a strangler strategy.

Meaning:
- the bridge is used initially as the broad integration surface
- over time, native_vendor implementations replace the bridge per capability or vendor
- the bridge footprint shrinks until it can be removed

Replacement occurs along stable seams.

Stable seams include:
- provider interfaces
- capability-based adapter routing
- stable device_keys
- scope-bound registry ownership
- standardized ActionResult evidence

A) REPLACEABILITY REQUIREMENT
For any capability, the router must be able to select:
- a bridge-backed adapter
OR
- a native-backed adapter
without changing module code.

B) INCREMENTAL MIGRATION
Migration must occur incrementally:
- choose one vendor or capability
- implement native provider
- route a small set of targets to native
- verify parity via acceptance tests and field observation
- expand coverage

C) IDENTITY CONTINUITY
During migration:
- device_keys remain stable
- the mapping from device_key → provider identity changes
- HouseState beliefs remain attached to device_keys
- Truth Gate semantics remain unchanged

The bridge may change.
Ashby identity must not.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
6) THE MOST COMMON FAILURE: “BRIDGE DEFINES REALITY”
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The bridge-defines-reality failure mode is structural.

It occurs when:
- the bridge’s entity model becomes Ashby’s domain model
- the bridge’s room/group naming becomes Ashby’s scope model
- the bridge’s last-known state is treated as verified truth
- module logic starts keying off bridge-specific fields

Consequences:
- replacing the bridge becomes impossible
- truth semantics degrade (“the bridge said it’s on”)
- state becomes non-portable
- the platform becomes a disguised integration wrapper

Canonical prevention:
- domain model remains bridge-agnostic
- scopes remain Ashby-owned
- bridge readbacks are evidence with verification posture
- module logic remains capability- and scope-based

Bridges accelerate execution.
They do not own meaning.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
7) OPERATIONAL RULES (WHAT MUST BE OBSERVABLE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Bridge-first requires observability.

Every bridge-backed execution must:
- carry correlation_id
- log bridge invocation boundaries
- preserve sanitized vendor errors as evidence, not as truth
- expose health signals for routing preference (when multiple providers exist)

When a bridge becomes unreliable:
- routing may prefer native providers where available
- but this must be auditable and policy-governed
- fallback must preserve truth posture (no false “done”)

The platform must be able to answer:
- “Which provider executed this?”
- “What evidence did we receive?”
- “Was it verified or last-known?”
- “What failed and where?”

Without that, migration is guesswork.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
8) SECURITY POSTURE (BRIDGES ARE POWERFUL)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Bridges often centralize credentials and device authority.
This makes them dangerous if treated casually.

Rules:
- bridge credentials are secrets handled by platform secret law
- bridge logs must never leak credentials or raw payloads
- bridge access must be least-privilege where possible
- bridge API access must be scoped and auditable

Bridge-first never permits “just connect everything and hope.”

Security is part of correctness.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Bridge-first strategy must:
- treat bridges as optional, replaceable, isolated integration engines
- keep bridges out of engine and module dependencies
- preserve vendor-neutral capabilities and interfaces
- treat bridge outputs as evidence, never as truth
- maintain stable device_keys independent of bridge ids
- include a defined exit path toward native_vendor ownership
- support incremental strangler migration with acceptance-test parity
- remain auditable and secure

Bridge-first strategy must never:
- allow the bridge to become the platform
- allow module logic to depend on bridge internals
- allow scope/domain identity to be defined by bridge naming
- treat bridge readbacks as verified truth by default
- erase migration feasibility through hidden coupling

Bridges exist to ship.
Native ownership exists to endure.
Ashby exists to outlast both.

END OF 31.1


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 31.2
Home Assistant as Strangler Layer

Home Assistant (HA) is treated as a bridge.

It is not a platform Ashby depends on.
It is a temporary integration engine used to accelerate capability while Ashby’s
native ownership expands.

Ashby’s meaning, truth, state, and authority remain Ashby-owned.
HA is an execution substrate and evidence source.

This subsection defines how HA is used without allowing HA to become “the system.”

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1) WHAT “HOME ASSISTANT AS STRANGLER” MEANS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A strangler layer is a temporary wrapper used to ship breadth early while building
native systems behind stable seams.

In Ashby terms:

- Ashby owns:
  - the domain model (devices, capabilities, scopes)
  - the engine and router
  - the Truth Gate and HouseState
  - policy outcomes (Act vs Clarify vs Confirm vs Refuse)
  - device identity and scope ownership

- Home Assistant provides:
  - broad vendor coverage via its integrations
  - a uniform service-call interface for execution
  - a state readback surface (entity states/attributes)
  - event streams (state_changed, etc.)
  - discovery and registry information (entity registry)

Strangler means:
- HA is used now because it is fast.
- HA is replaced later because Ashby must own its long-term fate.
- Replacement happens incrementally, capability-by-capability or vendor-by-vendor,
  without changing module logic.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2) NON-NEGOTIABLE INVARIANTS (HA MUST NOT DEFINE ASHBY)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A) HA IS OPTIONAL
Ashby must boot, route, and enforce truth with HA absent.

B) HA IS ISOLATED
No engine/ code imports HA.
No module/ code imports HA.

Only provider implementations in execution layers may depend on HA.

C) HA DOES NOT DEFINE REALITY
HA outputs are evidence, never truth.
Truth is assigned by Ashby’s Truth Gate based on evidence and verification posture.

D) HA DOES NOT DEFINE SCOPES
HA “areas,” “rooms,” “groups,” or “labels” are hints at best.
Ashby scopes are authoritative.

E) HA DOES NOT OWN IDENTITY
HA entity_id is never the canonical identity.
Ashby device_keys remain stable independent of HA.

F) HA DOES NOT OWN POLICY
Whether an action requires confirmation, clarification, or refusal is not HA’s choice.
It is Ashby’s.

These invariants prevent the bridge from becoming the platform.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
3) LAYERING AND DEPENDENCY DIRECTION
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Canonical layering for HA integration:

- domain/ defines:
  - Device
  - Capability
  - Scope
  - Registry identity rules
  - External ID mapping structures (bridge ids → Ashby ids)

- modules/ define WHAT:
  - “turn on the kitchen lights”
  - “set living room temperature to 72”
  - “arm security mode”
  Modules do not know about HA.

- interfaces/ define HOW contracts:
  - set_state, set_level, set_temperature, etc.
  Interface contracts remain stable even when providers change.

- adapters/ execute via providers:
  - choose an IntegrationProvider based on routing policy
  - perform execution attempts
  - return ActionResult evidence

- bridges/home_assistant/ provides one IntegrationProvider:
  - discovery / sync
  - service-call execution
  - state readback
  - event subscription

This arrangement ensures that replacing HA requires changing providers/adapters,
not changing module meaning.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
4) DEVICE IDENTITY: STABLE KEYS OVER BRIDGE IDS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

HA exposes multiple identity surfaces:
- entity_id (human-readable, mutable)
- unique_id (stable when present)
- device_id (registry device)
- integration domain and platform metadata
- hardware identifiers (sometimes available)

Ashby must own stable identity.

Canonical identity rule:
- Ashby creates and stores a device_key for every device it recognizes.
- External identifiers are stored as mappings under that device_key.

Recommended mapping strategy:

A) PRIMARY MAPPING (PREFERRED)
If HA provides a stable unique_id and/or registry device_id:
- store that as the primary external id
- treat entity_id as a mutable alias

B) SECONDARY MAPPING (COMPATIBILITY)
If stable unique identifiers are missing:
- store entity_id but mark it as “weak identity”
- increase the likelihood that the system will require re-resolution after rename
- prefer manual user confirmation during migrations

C) DEVICE VS ENTITY SPLIT
A single physical device may expose multiple entities:
- light + sensor + switch + diagnostics

Ashby must model:
- one device_key for the physical unit
- one or more entity_keys (or capability instances) attached to that device_key

The stable unit is the device_key.
Entities are attachable surfaces.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
5) ENTITY SYNC (DISCOVERY WITHOUT SCOPE TAKEOVER)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

HA entity sync is the process of importing HA’s entity registry into Ashby’s registry
without importing HA’s worldview.

Canonical goals of sync:
- discover what controllable targets exist
- map them to Ashby devices and capabilities
- record external ids for execution
- record lightweight metadata for user-facing resolution

Canonical non-goals of sync:
- adopting HA areas as Ashby scopes automatically
- adopting HA group logic as Ashby group law
- treating HA states as verified truth by default

Practical sync behavior:

A) SCAN
- fetch registry/device list
- fetch entity list
- fetch capability-relevant attributes

B) NORMALIZE
- determine capability type for each entity
  (light, switch, thermostat, sensor, lock, camera, etc.)
- record supported features as capability metadata
  (brightness, color_temp, hvac_modes, etc.)

C) REGISTER
- create/maintain device_key records in Ashby
- create/maintain external_id mappings:
  device_key ↔ (ha_device_id, ha_unique_id, ha_entity_id set)

D) SCOPE HINTING (NON-AUTHORITATIVE)
- record HA area name as a hint field
- require explicit user confirmation before binding to an Ashby scope
- if no confirmation exists, keep the device unscoped or in a default scope,
  depending on platform policy

E) CHANGE TRACKING
- entity rename, removal, or migration is recorded as a registry change event
- registry changes must be auditable and reversible

This keeps discovery useful while preventing HA worldview takeover.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
6) EXECUTION THROUGH HA (SERVICE CALLS AS “HOW”)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Execution via HA occurs through adapters calling the HA provider.

Canonical execution pathway:

1) module produces an ActionRequest (WHAT)
2) router selects an adapter by capability
3) adapter selects a provider (HA, native_vendor, etc.)
4) provider attempts execution:
   - HA service call
   - optional readback
   - return ActionResult evidence
5) Truth Gate assigns truth class and language constraints
6) HouseState is updated only through Truth Gate-approved evidence

Execution constraints:

A) HA SERVICE CALLS ARE NOT PROOF
A service call returning success/ack is not the same as the device being in the desired state.

B) VERIFICATION IS AN ASHBY DECISION
Verification semantics are governed by Ashby’s Truth Gate and adapter rules.
The provider supplies evidence: ack, readback state, and timing.

C) READBACK IS PREFERRED
When possible:
- call the service
- then read back the entity state
- compare to desired
- report VERIFIED only when readback matches under allowed tolerance

D) TOLERANCE MUST BE EXPLICIT
Some capabilities are continuous (temperature, dimmer levels).
Verification must define:
- acceptable tolerance
- time window for stabilization
- what constitutes a mismatch

E) HA DELAYS MUST BE HANDLED
HA event/state propagation may be delayed.
Adapters should:
- wait for state change event up to a bounded window
- fall back to explicit state fetch
- report failure honestly when readback cannot be obtained

These rules prevent “HA said okay” from becoming a lie.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
7) STATE INPUTS FROM HA (EVIDENCE PIPELINE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

HA provides two primary state input forms:
- polling (fetch state snapshot)
- event stream (state_changed events)

Ashby treats HA state as evidence with staleness.

Canonical pipeline:

A) EVENT SUBSCRIPTION (PREFERRED)
- subscribe to HA events
- for each event:
  - attach correlation_id if known (when event is causally linked to an action)
  - update HouseState beliefs with appropriate confidence
  - record timestamp and source ("home_assistant")

B) POLLING (FALLBACK AND BASELINE)
- periodic snapshot fetch to detect missed events
- used to repair drift after reconnects

C) STALENESS
If HA stops delivering events (disconnect, crash, network outage):
- beliefs sourced from HA decay
- previously verified beliefs can become LAST_KNOWN
- eventually become UNKNOWN if decay continues

This ensures Ashby does not “keep speaking as if reality is current”
when the bridge has gone silent.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
8) HA AREAS/ROOMS VS ASHBY SCOPES (HINTS, NOT OWNERSHIP)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

HA has its own organizational model:
- areas
- labels
- device registry groups
- dashboards

Ashby uses scopes as the world model.

Rule:
- HA organization data may be ingested as hints only.
- Scope binding is an Ashby action governed by user confirmation and policy.

Practical scope binding approach:

- When a device is discovered with an HA area:
  - record ha_area_name as a hint
  - ask the user to confirm scope placement if ambiguous
  - allow bulk mapping only through explicit onboarding flows

- If the user later changes HA area labels:
  - do not automatically move Ashby scope bindings
  - treat the new label as a hint update
  - require confirmation for any authoritative change

This preserves Ashby’s world model independence.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
9) ROUTING POLICY: HA VS NATIVE PROVIDERS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Bridge-first means HA may be the default provider early.
Native Vendor Expansion means HA stops being the default over time.

Provider selection is routing policy, not module logic.

Canonical provider selection criteria include:

- reliability (measured by success rates and latency)
- verification strength (readback fidelity)
- offline capability (local vs cloud dependencies)
- feature completeness (capability coverage)
- security posture (credential scope, failure isolation)
- maintenance burden (bridge fragility vs native stability)

Allowed routing behaviors:

A) PREFER NATIVE WHEN AVAILABLE
If a native provider exists for the capability and target:
- route to native by default
- fall back to HA only if policy permits and the failure mode is safe

B) PREFER HA FOR COVERAGE
For unsupported vendors/capabilities:
- route to HA

C) DUAL-PATH SAFETY (OPTIONAL)
For critical actions, the adapter may:
- execute through one provider
- verify through a second evidence source when possible
This must remain bounded and policy-governed.

Routing must always be observable:
- which provider was chosen
- why it was chosen
- what evidence was produced

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
10) MIGRATION MECHANICS (HA AS STRANGLER TARGET)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Strangler migration is incremental.

Canonical migration mechanics:

A) STABLE IDENTITY FIRST
Before switching providers:
- ensure device_key is stable
- ensure external id mappings exist for both:
  - HA identifiers
  - native vendor identifiers
- ensure HouseState beliefs are attached to device_key, not HA entity_id

B) PARITY THROUGH ACCEPTANCE TESTS
Migration must preserve behavior.
Compare:
- execution success/failure
- latency
- verification class (VERIFIED vs LAST_KNOWN)
- error surfaces
- state updates

C) GRADUAL ROUTE SHIFT
- begin with a subset of devices or scopes
- observe
- expand
- keep rollback available

D) HA SHRINKS OVER TIME
As native provider coverage grows:
- fewer actions route through HA
- HA becomes a fallback or legacy bridge
- eventually HA can be removed or restricted

The bridge is a means to ship, not a permanent dependency.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
11) FAILURE MODES (WHAT GOES WRONG WITH HA)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Common failure modes:

- HA unavailable (host down, restart, network outage)
- stale states (events missed, delayed updates)
- entity renames (identity drift)
- inconsistent integration behavior (vendor quirk via HA)
- service call accepted but device never changes
- HA reports optimistic states while devices are offline

Canonical Ashby response requirements:

- do not claim success without evidence
- surface partial outcomes explicitly
- degrade truth posture under staleness
- require clarification when identity drift occurs
- log provider failures with correlation_id and sanitized error fields
- preserve removability (Ashby remains coherent without HA)

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
12) SECURITY AND SECRETS (HA TOKENS ARE POWERFUL)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

HA credentials grant wide authority.
They must be treated as secrets.

Rules:
- HA tokens live only in secrets handling subsystems
- tokens are never logged
- tokens are never stored in artifacts
- scopes/permissions are least-privilege when possible
- revocation and rotation must be supported

Bridge integration is not an excuse for relaxed security.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Home Assistant as a strangler layer must:
- be optional, isolated, replaceable, and auditable
- feed Ashby through provider interfaces only
- provide evidence, not truth
- preserve stable Ashby device identity independent of HA ids
- treat HA organization data as hints, not scope authority
- support verification readback and honest failure semantics
- enable incremental migration toward native_vendor implementations

Home Assistant as a strangler layer must never:
- be imported by engine/ or modules/
- define scope boundaries or canonical identity
- be treated as verified truth by default
- silently mask staleness, failures, or partial execution
- become the platform behind Ashby

END OF 31.2


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 31.3
Protocol Bridges (Zigbee, Matter)

Protocol bridges are bridges.

They provide device connectivity and execution through standardized protocols,
but they are still execution infrastructure (HOW), not domain meaning (WHAT).

In Ashby, Zigbee and Matter are treated as replaceable protocol execution layers.
They may be accessed via a general bridge (such as Home Assistant) or directly
through protocol-specific bridge providers.

The defining rule remains:
- modules do not know protocols
- protocols do not define reality
- protocol outputs are evidence evaluated by the Truth Gate

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1) WHY PROTOCOL BRIDGES EXIST
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Zigbee and Matter are not brands.
They are ecosystems of devices and capabilities.

Protocol bridges exist to:
- connect to diverse devices without vendor SDK sprawl
- provide local, offline-capable execution
- normalize basic device control pathways
- offer discovery and pairing flows
- provide event-driven state changes

Protocol bridges accelerate device coverage without hardcoding vendors into Ashby.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2) PROTOCOL ≠ DOMAIN MODEL (THE BOUNDARY)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Protocols express:
- clusters, endpoints, attributes (Matter)
- clusters, endpoints, commands, reports (Zigbee)
- node ids, ieee addresses, network addresses
- device types and descriptors

Ashby domain expresses:
- devices
- capabilities
- targets
- scopes
- state beliefs with staleness
- policy outcomes

Rule:
- protocol-specific structures are confined to provider implementations and
  normalization logic under bridges/.
- no protocol data structures may leak into module meaning or engine policy.

If a module starts “thinking in clusters,” architecture has failed.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
3) IDENTITY: STABLE DEVICE_KEYS OVER NETWORK IDENTIFIERS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Protocols provide identifiers that are stable within protocol contexts,
but not necessarily stable across fabrics/coordinators/resets.

ZIGBEE IDENTIFIERS (REPRESENTATIVE):
- IEEE address (globally unique for the radio)
- network address (changes)
- endpoints (per device)
- clusters (capability surface)

MATTER IDENTIFIERS (REPRESENTATIVE):
- node id (fabric-scoped)
- fabric id / fabric index
- endpoints
- clusters and attributes
- commissioning identifiers (setup code / discriminator)

Ashby identity rule:
- Ashby device_key is the canonical identity.
- protocol identifiers are stored as external mappings under that device_key.

Additional identity rules:

A) MULTI-ENTITY DEVICES
A single device may expose multiple endpoints and clusters.
Ashby models one device_key with multiple capabilities.

B) FABRIC/COORDINATOR MIGRATION
If a device is re-commissioned into a new Matter fabric or moved between Zigbee
coordinators, protocol identifiers may change.
Ashby must preserve device_key and update external mappings through explicit
migration flows, not silent replacement.

C) DEVICE FACTORY RESET
Factory reset is an identity hazard.
Ashby must treat it as potential identity discontinuity and require explicit
re-binding when ambiguity exists.

Identity continuity is essential for long-lived HouseState.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
4) DISCOVERY AND PAIRING (ONBOARDING AS A GOVERNED FLOW)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Protocol onboarding is not “automatic magic.”
It is a privileged operation that changes what exists in a scope.

Rules:
- pairing/commissioning requires explicit user confirmation
- pairing must bind devices into a scope
- pairing artifacts (QR codes, setup codes, network keys) are secrets and must
  never be logged or stored in artifacts in plaintext
- onboarding must produce auditable records:
  - what was added
  - when
  - by whom
  - under what scope

Zigbee pairing (representative):
- coordinator enters permit-join mode
- device joins
- endpoints/clusters discovered
- device_key assigned
- capability mapping produced

Matter commissioning (representative):
- controller commissions device into fabric
- node id assigned
- endpoints/clusters enumerated
- device_key assigned
- capability mapping produced

Onboarding must be safe enough for product shipping, not hobby scripting.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
5) EXECUTION SEMANTICS (COMMANDS, ACKS, READBACKS)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Protocol bridges execute actions by sending protocol commands.

Critical rule:
- a command being sent is not proof the device state changed.

Canonical execution evidence sources include:
- command acknowledgement (if available)
- attribute readback after execution
- event/report stream confirming state change
- device online/offline signals

ZIGBEE EXECUTION CHARACTERISTICS:
- some commands may not be acknowledged
- some devices report state changes asynchronously
- routing and mesh health affect latency and reliability

MATTER EXECUTION CHARACTERISTICS:
- more consistent acknowledgement model
- attribute subscriptions are common
- device reachability and fabric health still matter

Verification law:
- adapters request verification posture
- providers supply evidence
- Truth Gate determines whether VERIFIED, LAST_KNOWN, UNKNOWN, FAILED applies

Readback is preferred when feasible.
When readback is unavailable or unreliable, posture must degrade.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
6) STATE UPDATES (REPORTS AND SUBSCRIPTIONS AS EVIDENCE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Protocols provide state updates:

Zigbee:
- attribute reporting (periodic or change-driven)
- coordinator event signals
- device availability and LQI/mesh indicators

Matter:
- attribute subscriptions
- events
- reachability signals

Ashby consumption rules:

A) EVENT-DRIVEN IS PREFERRED
Subscriptions/reports are preferred to polling for responsiveness and energy.

B) STALENESS IS TRACKED
If subscriptions stop:
- beliefs decay
- VERIFIED → LAST_KNOWN over time
- LAST_KNOWN → UNKNOWN if stale enough

C) SOURCE IS RECORDED
HouseState beliefs should record source attribution (protocol bridge) and timestamp.

D) CONTRADICTIONS ARE PRESERVED
If two evidence sources conflict (e.g., readback says OFF, report stream says ON):
- preserve mixed reality
- request clarification or trigger re-read
- do not “smooth” the conflict with confident language

Protocols are fast, not infallible.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
7) CAPABILITY NORMALIZATION (FROM CLUSTERS TO CAPABILITIES)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Capability normalization is the translation layer that prevents protocol leakage.

Normalization responsibilities include:
- mapping device types and clusters into Ashby capability labels
- mapping control surfaces into interface contract methods
- preserving protocol-specific feature flags as metadata
  (without requiring modules to understand them)

Examples (illustrative only):
- Zigbee OnOff cluster → capability: switchable
- Zigbee LevelControl → capability: dimmable
- Matter On/Off cluster → capability: switchable
- Matter Thermostat cluster → capability: thermostat_control

Normalization must be:
- deterministic
- auditable
- stable over time unless revised via Control Sync

Changing normalization is a behavior change.
It must be handled carefully.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
8) COEXISTENCE WITH HOME ASSISTANT
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Home Assistant may itself operate Zigbee and Matter stacks.

Ashby supports both strategies:

A) HA-INDIRECT (BRIDGE-ON-BRIDGE)
- Ashby talks to HA
- HA talks to Zigbee/Matter
- faster to ship, lower ownership
- less direct control over protocol semantics

B) DIRECT PROTOCOL BRIDGE (ASHBY-OWNED EXECUTION)
- Ashby talks to Zigbee coordinator or Matter controller directly
- better verification fidelity and routing control possible
- higher maintenance burden
- preferred when it provides leverage and stability

Routing policy decides which provider path is used for a given device_key.

The architecture must allow switching without changing module logic.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
9) SECURITY (PROTOCOL KEYS ARE CRITICAL)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Protocol security is not optional.

ZIGBEE:
- network keys and coordinator credentials are secrets
- joining permissions must be gated
- coordinator access must be restricted

MATTER:
- fabric keys/certificates are secrets
- commissioning codes are secrets
- controller state is sensitive and must be protected
- device attestation results may be stored only as non-secret evidence artifacts

Rules:
- secrets are stored only in secrets handling systems
- secrets never appear in logs
- secrets never appear in artifacts
- backups must protect secrets and support restoration safely

A protocol bridge that leaks keys compromises the entire environment.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
10) FAILURE MODES (REALITY OF PROTOCOL NETWORKS)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Common Zigbee failures:
- devices fall off the mesh
- coordinator resets
- routing instability causes delays
- inconsistent reporting behavior across vendors

Common Matter failures:
- fabric instability during commissioning
- device reachability changes (Wi‑Fi thread issues)
- subscription dropouts
- API/library drift in the Matter controller stack

Canonical Ashby behaviors:
- never claim success without evidence
- degrade truth posture under uncertainty
- make disconnects visible in state and language
- keep execution attempts bounded (timeouts/retries)
- preserve removability (protocol bridges can be disabled)

Protocol reliability is managed, not assumed.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Protocol bridges must:
- remain in execution layers (HOW) under bridges/
- be replaceable and isolated
- preserve Ashby device_keys as canonical identity
- treat protocol outputs as evidence evaluated by the Truth Gate
- support explicit onboarding flows with permission and scope binding
- normalize protocol structures into Ashby capabilities without leakage
- track staleness and preserve mixed reality under contradictions
- maintain strict secret hygiene for protocol keys and commissioning data

Protocol bridges must never:
- be referenced by modules or engine as dependencies
- define Ashby scopes or domain meaning
- treat acknowledgements as verified truth by default
- leak keys, setup codes, or controller secrets through logs/artifacts
- collapse conflicts into confident narratives

END OF 31.3


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 31.4
Native Vendor Expansion

Native Vendor Expansion is the disciplined growth of Ashby-owned integrations
under native_vendor/ that progressively replace bridge dependencies.

Native expansion is not “own everything.”
It is the deliberate acquisition of ownership where it provides leverage:
- stronger verification
- better offline capability
- improved reliability and latency
- deeper features
- reduced dependency risk
- improved security posture
- long-term product stability

Bridges exist to ship.
Native ownership exists to endure.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1) NATIVE VENDOR INTEGRATION: DEFINITION
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A native vendor integration is:
- an Ashby-owned provider implementation for a specific vendor/ecosystem
- isolated in its own folder under native_vendor/
- implementing the same provider interface as bridge-backed providers
- never imported by engine/ or modules/
- selected by routing policy at execution time

Native integration is still HOW, not WHAT.

Modules continue to describe intent in vendor-neutral terms.
Native providers execute those intents against vendor APIs or local devices.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2) WHY NATIVE OWNERSHIP IS STRATEGIC (AND WHEN IT IS REQUIRED)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Native ownership is justified when it enables one or more of:

A) VERIFICATION FIDELITY
- reliable readbacks
- consistent state semantics
- fewer optimistic acknowledgements
- better confirmation of action outcomes

B) OFFLINE OR LOCAL CONTROL
- bridge/cloud layers introduce dependency and latency
- local vendor APIs or LAN protocols can improve resilience

C) FEATURE DEPTH
- bridges often expose a lowest-common-denominator capability surface
- native integrations may unlock advanced features and diagnostics

D) SECURITY
- direct control over token handling, scopes, and credential isolation
- reduced third-party data exposure

E) RELIABILITY AND MAINTAINABILITY
- bridges can be fragile when upstream changes occur
- native ownership reduces “unknown unknowns” in execution pipelines

Native ownership is not pursued for ego.
It is pursued for stability and leverage.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
3) THE NATIVE VENDOR RULES (NON-NEGOTIABLE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A) ONE FOLDER PER VENDOR
Each vendor integration lives in:
native_vendor/<vendor_name>/

B) NO CROSS-VENDOR LOGIC
Vendor-specific behavior is isolated.
No “mega integration” folder that handles multiple vendors.

C) PROVIDER INTERFACE COMPLIANCE
Native providers implement the same IntegrationProvider interface used by bridges.
They produce the same evidence shapes.

D) MODULES STAY VENDOR-NEUTRAL
No module changes are allowed to “support the vendor.”
Vendor support belongs in providers and adapters only.

E) SECRETS ARE STRICT
OAuth tokens, API keys, and device credentials remain secret.
No secrets in logs, configs, or artifacts.

F) OBSERVABILITY IS MANDATORY
Native providers must emit execution traces and failures in a way that supports
debugging, audits, and migration decisions.

Native ownership must not become a second architecture.
It must occupy the same seams.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
4) THE STANDARD NATIVE PROVIDER SHAPE (REFERENCE PATTERN)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A native vendor folder typically contains (representative, not exhaustive):

- oauth.py
  - authorization flow handlers
  - refresh token logic
  - token storage hooks (through secret store)

- client.py / api_client.py
  - HTTP client or LAN client
  - retries, timeouts, rate limiting
  - sanitized error mapping

- discovery.py
  - device discovery and enumeration
  - metadata gathering

- normalizer.py
  - mapping vendor device models → Ashby capabilities
  - mapping vendor state → canonical state belief inputs
  - mapping vendor commands → interface contract actions

- provider.py
  - implements IntegrationProvider
  - exposes:
    - execute(ActionRequest) → ActionResult
    - read_state(target) → evidence
    - subscribe() / poll() as needed

This pattern keeps vendor integrations legible and replaceable.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
5) IDENTITY AND MIGRATION (THE MOST IMPORTANT PART)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Native expansion must not break identity.

Canonical identity rules:

- device_key remains the canonical identity.
- vendor ids are mapped under device_key as external identifiers.

Migration requirement:
- migrating from bridge → native must not change device_key.
- HouseState beliefs remain attached to device_key.
- only the provider mapping changes.

Preferred migration process:

A) DISCOVER AND MAP
- discover vendor devices
- map them to existing device_keys using:
  - stable hardware ids (preferred)
  - unique vendor ids
  - user confirmation when ambiguous
- store external id mapping for native provider

B) PARITY VALIDATION
- confirm that native provider can:
  - execute key capabilities
  - read back state
  - subscribe/poll reliably
- compare with bridge path outcomes for the same device_key

C) ROUTE SWITCH (INCREMENTAL)
- switch routing for a subset of devices/scopes
- observe
- expand gradually

D) ROLLBACK PATH
- preserve bridge mapping until native is proven stable
- rollback must be immediate and safe

Identity continuity is the difference between migration and replacement.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
6) VERIFICATION RESPONSIBILITY (NATIVE MUST RAISE THE BAR)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Native ownership is not merely “faster execution.”
It is an opportunity to strengthen verification.

Native providers should aim to improve:

- readback latency and accuracy
- state subscription reliability
- mismatch detection
- failure classification
- recovery guidance (when safe and factual)

However, native ownership must still obey Truth Gate law.
Native providers supply evidence.
They do not declare truth.

Verification improvement must be explicit:
- define what qualifies as VERIFIED for each capability
- define how readbacks are performed
- define what happens when readback is unavailable

Native ownership is valuable when it produces stronger evidence.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
7) ACCEPTANCE TESTS AND BEHAVIOR PARITY
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Native expansion must not change behavior unexpectedly.

Canonical parity requirements:
- a light that worked before must work identically after
- confirmation/clarification requirements must remain the same
- truth posture must remain correct (no upgraded certainty without evidence)
- failures must remain honest and observable

Native expansion should be validated by:
- harness tests for core capabilities
- golden action flows for a set of representative devices
- regression checks for state updates and staleness behavior
- controlled rollout and observability metrics

If parity cannot be proven, routing must not switch.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
8) STRANGLER PATH: HOW HA SHRINKS AS NATIVE GROWS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Native expansion and bridge-first are designed to coexist.

The strangler path is:

1) HA provides broad coverage early.
2) Native providers are built for high-value vendors.
3) Routing prefers native when available.
4) HA becomes fallback for unsupported devices or emergency recovery.
5) HA usage shrinks over time.
6) HA becomes removable, optional legacy infrastructure.

Important rule:
- HA is not “kept forever just in case.”
If HA is kept, it must remain isolated, optional, and auditable.

The platform must be able to survive without the bridge.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
9) OPERATIONS AND MAINTENANCE (NATIVE OWNERSHIP IS A COMMITMENT)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Owning a vendor integration means owning:
- API drift and version changes
- credential refresh and revocation handling
- rate limiting and backoff behavior
- vendor outages and degraded modes
- device model diversity
- security posture over time

Native vendor expansion must include:
- versioned contracts
- robust error taxonomy mapping
- observability hooks
- documented recovery procedures
- clear disabling/removal behavior

Native ownership without maintenance discipline is worse than bridging.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
10) SECURITY POSTURE (NATIVE MUST NOT INCREASE RISK)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Native providers handle powerful secrets.

Rules:
- secrets are stored only via secret-handling subsystems
- tokens never appear in logs
- logs are sanitized and structured
- permissions are least-privilege where possible
- audit trails record:
  - which user authorized the integration
  - when authorization occurred
  - when tokens were refreshed or revoked
  - which executions occurred

Native expansion must improve security posture, not degrade it.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Native Vendor Expansion must:
- expand native_vendor/ deliberately, vendor-by-vendor
- maintain stable device_keys and preserve identity continuity
- implement provider interfaces without leaking vendor logic into modules/engine
- improve evidence quality and verification fidelity where possible
- preserve behavior parity through tests and controlled rollout
- enable strangler migration where bridges shrink over time
- remain auditable, removable, and secure

Native Vendor Expansion must never:
- require module rewrites to “support the vendor”
- introduce cross-vendor mega logic that collapses boundaries
- upgrade truth posture without evidence
- break identity continuity during migration
- weaken secret hygiene or observability
- turn native ownership into a second platform architecture

END OF 31.4


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 32.1
Profiles vs Forks

Ashby is a platform.

Productization must not fracture the platform.
The platform survives shipping only if shipping is constrained by law.

This subsection defines the difference between:
- profiles (allowed)
- forks (forbidden)

A profile is how Ashby becomes many products without becoming many Ashbys.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1) DEFINITIONS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

PROFILE
A profile is a declarative packaging and configuration layer that selects:
- which modules are enabled
- which adapters/providers are available
- which policies and defaults apply
- which UI surfaces are exposed
- which entitlements are required

A profile changes what is offered.
It must not change what Ashby is.

FORK
A fork is a divergence of source, semantics, or invariants such that:
- two builds of “Ashby” no longer behave as one platform
- they can no longer share the same Codex law and still be correct

Forks do not begin as “big rewrites.”
Forks usually begin as “just one special case” inside core.

Forks are how platforms die.

PRODUCT
A product is a bundle: profile + distribution + entitlement rules + user-facing packaging.
Products are built from profiles.
Products are not allowed to rewrite the platform.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2) WHY PROFILES EXIST (THE REAL PROBLEM)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Shipping creates pressure:
- different users want different experiences
- different markets demand different integrations
- different security postures exist (offline-only vs cloud-allowed)
- different hardware environments exist (single home vs multi-site)
- different budgets exist (free vs paid)

If each shipping surface becomes a separate codebase, the platform fractures.
Fracture produces:
- inconsistent truth posture
- inconsistent safety policy
- inconsistent state semantics
- irreconcilable migrations
- incomplete rebuildability

Profiles exist to absorb shipping pressure without altering platform law.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
3) THE CANONICAL PROFILE MODEL (WHAT A PROFILE MAY CONTROL)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A profile may control:

A) MODULE SET
- which modules exist as selectable capability surfaces
- whether certain domain modules are enabled by default
- whether some modules are completely absent (removable modules remain removable)

B) CONNECTOR AVAILABILITY
- which connectors can be configured (inventory connectors, storage connectors, etc.)
- whether connectors are enabled per scope

C) EXECUTION PROVIDER PREFERENCES
- default routing preferences (prefer native vs prefer bridge)
- whether cloud fallback is allowed or forbidden (policy-controlled)
- default verification strategy per capability (within Truth Gate law)

D) POLICY DEFAULTS
- default confirmation thresholds
- default safety posture for risky actions
- default session expiry windows
- default retention and redaction posture for sensitive artifacts
- default failure handling posture (e.g., refuse vs clarify in certain cases)

E) UI / SURFACE AREAS
- which intents are exposed through which UI surfaces
- which dashboards exist
- what labels and wording are used

F) ENTITLEMENT REQUIREMENTS
- what requires licensing/entitlements vs what is free

These are legitimate productization levers.
They do not change the engine, truth, state, or invariants.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
4) WHAT A PROFILE MUST NEVER CONTROL (THE FORK BOUNDARY)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A profile must never change:

A) TRUTH CLASSES AND LANGUAGE LAW
- VERIFIED/LAST_KNOWN/UNKNOWN/FAILED semantics
- forbidden phrases
- “ActionResult as Reality”
- how evidence becomes truth

B) HOUSESTATE SEMANTICS
- belief confidence behavior
- staleness and decay law
- mixed reality preservation
- restart and persistence posture

C) WHAT vs HOW SEPARATION
- modules remain meaning (WHAT)
- adapters remain execution (HOW)
- no vendor logic leaks into meaning due to profile

D) ENGINE POLICY CATEGORIES
The platform must still produce coherent outcomes:
- ACT / CLARIFY / CONFIRM / REFUSE / CHAT
Profiles may adjust thresholds and defaults.
Profiles must not invent new policy classes or undermine safety logic.

E) SECURITY AND SECRETS LAW
Profiles may limit which integrations are possible.
Profiles must not relax secret handling or logging hygiene.

If a profile changes any of the above, it is no longer a profile.
It is a fork.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
5) PROFILE AS A DECLARATIVE MANIFEST (NOT “IF STATEMENTS”)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Profiles must be declarative.

A profile is defined as a manifest that can be inspected, diffed, and audited.
The manifest is configuration, not code.

The platform must avoid:
- scattering “if profile == …” conditions throughout engine/
- encoding product differences as hidden special cases
- embedding product assumptions in modules

Instead:
- profiles declare which modules/adapters/providers are enabled
- profiles declare policy defaults and constraints
- profiles declare available surface areas

Code paths remain stable.
Behavior changes by selection and configuration at the edges.

This preserves rebuildability and reduces accidental drift.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
6) PROFILE INHERITANCE (HOW MANY PRODUCTS STAY ONE PLATFORM)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Profiles should be composable and inheritable.

A recommended structure:

- base_profile
  - the minimal lawful Ashby platform
  - contains core invariants and required components

- derived profiles
  - “home”
  - “pro”
  - “business”
  - “offline_only”
  - etc.

Derived profiles may add or remove modules and set defaults.
Derived profiles must not override platform invariants.

Inheritance prevents duplication.
Duplication is where drift hides.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
7) PROFILE COMPATIBILITY AND MIGRATION
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Profiles evolve.
Shipping requires versioning.

Rules:
- a profile has a version
- a profile change that alters data shape requires a migration (pack #5 law)
- a profile change that alters invariants requires Control Sync (not a normal release)

Important distinction:
- changing what is enabled is not the same as changing what exists.

Example:
- removing a module from a profile does not erase artifacts it previously created
- disabling a connector does not delete its stored records
- profiles must define how dormant artifacts are handled (accessible vs archived)

A correct platform can:
- change profiles
- migrate safely
- preserve identity and history
without rewriting core meaning.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
8) SUPPORT AND OBSERVABILITY REQUIREMENT
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Profiles must be observable.

Given a correlation_id, the platform must be able to answer:
- which profile was active
- which flags and entitlements were in effect
- which modules/adapters were enabled
- why an action was allowed or refused

If productization cannot be reconstructed from logs, the system becomes un-debuggable.
Undebuggable systems are not rebuildable.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
9) THE CORE FAILURE MODE: “PRODUCTIZATION INSIDE CORE”
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Forks begin when product decisions leak into core systems:

Examples of forbidden drift:
- engine/ contains “if business_profile then …”
- Truth Gate behaves differently under different products
- HouseState changes schema based on product
- adapters alter evidence posture based on product convenience
- modules assume the presence of a specific bridge/vendor

These changes create incompatible Ashbys.

The platform must resist the temptation to encode product identity in core logic.

Profiles belong at the packaging boundary.
The platform remains singular.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Profiles must:
- be declarative manifests, not scattered special cases
- control what is enabled, exposed, and defaulted
- remain auditable and reconstructable
- remain compatible with migrations and acceptance tests
- preserve a single Ashby platform law across all products

Profiles must never:
- alter Truth Gate semantics, language law, or HouseState law
- alter WHAT vs HOW separation
- weaken security, secrets, or logging invariants
- introduce product-specific behavior inside core subsystems
- create divergent “Ashby variants” that cannot share the same Codex

Profiles are how Ashby ships.
Forks are how Ashby dies.

END OF 32.1


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 32.2
Feature Flags

Feature flags are a shipping tool.
They are not an architecture.

They exist to:
- stage capability gradually
- run controlled rollouts
- perform safe experiments
- enable emergency shutdown of unstable features

Feature flags must never become a second policy system.
They must never become a secret fork mechanism.

This subsection defines how flags are used without compromising Ashby’s invariants.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1) DEFINITIONS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FEATURE FLAG
A runtime-controlled toggle that enables or disables a behavior or surface area
without requiring a full release cycle.

Flags are evaluated at runtime and are observable.

KILL SWITCH
A high-priority flag whose purpose is to disable a risky pathway immediately.
Kill switches are safety tools.

EXPERIMENT FLAG
A flag used to compare behaviors between cohorts, with strict logging.
Experiments must not alter truth law.
They may alter UI, workflow ordering, or non-critical defaults.

PROFILE DEFAULT
A profile may define the default value of flags.
Flags may override defaults at runtime under strict audit.

ENTITLEMENT GATE
A licensing/entitlement check that decides whether a feature is allowed at all.
Flags may control rollout within an entitled population.
Flags must not bypass entitlements.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2) WHY FLAGS ARE DANGEROUS (THE FAILURE MODE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Flags become dangerous when:
- they hide behavior changes
- they create untracked divergence
- they are scattered across core logic
- they alter data schemas without migration
- they modify truth posture or safety posture silently

A “flagged platform” that cannot be reconstructed from logs
is indistinguishable from a forked platform.

Flags must be governed.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
3) WHAT FLAGS MAY CONTROL (SAFE SURFACES)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Flags may control:

A) UI AND SURFACE EXPOSURE
- enable/disable a new screen
- enable/disable a new intent alias
- show/hide a product surface

B) WORKFLOW ORDERING (NON-TRUTH-ALTERING)
- which adapter is preferred when multiple are equivalent
- which summary format is default (without claiming different truth)
- which onboarding flow is used

C) ROLLOUT OF NEW MODULES OR CONNECTORS
- enabling a new module for a subset of users/scopes
- enabling a new connector integration progressively

D) PERFORMANCE BEHAVIORS (SAFE)
- caching strategies that do not change correctness
- logging verbosity (increase only; never reduce critical audit fields)

E) EMERGENCY DISABLEMENT
- disable cloud fallback globally if a provider becomes unsafe
- disable a specific adapter/provider if it misbehaves
- disable a problematic connector action while preserving read-only capability

Flags are allowed to change “what path is used,” not “what truth is.”

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
4) WHAT FLAGS MUST NEVER CONTROL (FORBIDDEN USES)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Flags must never change:

A) TRUTH LAW
- they must not allow the system to claim VERIFIED without evidence
- they must not relax forbidden phrases
- they must not change truth classes or language constraints

B) HOUSESTATE SEMANTICS
- they must not change belief schemas in-place without migration
- they must not change staleness/decay rules
- they must not change mixed reality behavior

C) SAFETY POLICY CATEGORIES
- flags must not invent new policy outcomes
- flags must not bypass confirmation requirements for high-risk actions
- flags must not suppress clarifications when ambiguity exists

D) SECURITY AND SECRETS HANDLING
- flags must not enable “debug logging” that leaks secrets
- flags must not relax sanitization
- flags must not open privileged pathways

E) DATA SHAPE WITHOUT MIGRATION
- flags must not create dual schema realities unless explicitly designed
- if a flag changes persisted data shape, migration discipline is required

If a flag does any of the above, it is not a flag.
It is a fork disguised as a toggle.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
5) FLAG GOVERNANCE (A FLAG IS A CONTRACT)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Each flag must have:

- name (stable)
- owner (accountable)
- purpose (why it exists)
- scope of evaluation (global / per user / per scope / per device / per module)
- default value (usually defined by profile)
- expiration policy (when it should be removed or locked on/off)
- logging requirements (how it is recorded)
- safety classification:
  - experiment
  - rollout
  - kill switch

Flags without ownership and expiration become permanent debt.
Permanent flag debt is drift.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
6) EVALUATION MODEL (WHERE FLAGS ARE READ)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Flags must be evaluated centrally.

Rules:
- core decisions should not rely on scattered checks
- feature evaluation should occur in well-defined gate points

Canonical gate points include:
- surface exposure (UI/router pre-validation)
- module availability gate
- connector availability gate
- provider routing preference gate
- kill-switch override gate (highest priority)

Avoid:
- flags sprinkled throughout engine logic
- flags that change control pipeline steps without explicit documentation

The platform must remain comprehensible.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
7) SCOPING AND COHORTS (HOW FLAGS APPLY)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Flags may apply to:

- GLOBAL: affects all instances
- USER: affects a single user identity
- SCOPE: affects an Ashby scope (home, business location, org)
- DEVICE: affects a specific device_key
- MODULE: affects a module_key
- COHORT: affects a defined group for experiment/rollout

Rules:
- scoping must be explicit and logged
- precedence order must be stable and documented
  (e.g., kill-switch overrides all; then global; then profile default; then scope; etc.)
- cohort membership must be deterministically computed or explicitly assigned
- cohort algorithms must be stable (no random drift)

Flags are not “magic.”
They are deliberate control surfaces.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
8) OBSERVABILITY (FLAGS MUST BE RECONSTRUCTABLE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

For every correlation_id, logs must be able to show:
- which flags were evaluated
- what values were used
- what scope/user/device context applied
- whether a kill switch altered routing or execution

Logs must not expose:
- secret flag payloads (flags should not contain secrets)
- raw entitlement tokens
- vendor credentials

If a behavior changed due to a flag but is not visible in logs,
the platform becomes un-debuggable and unrebuildable.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
9) FLAGS VS PROFILES VS ENTITLEMENTS (THE HIERARCHY)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Correct hierarchy:

- ENTITLEMENTS decide: “is this allowed at all?”
- PROFILES decide: “is this part of the product?”
- FLAGS decide: “is this rolled out or staged right now?”

Flags do not override entitlements.
Flags do not redefine profiles.
Profiles may set defaults for flags.

This hierarchy preserves product truth.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
10) RETIREMENT AND CLEANUP (FLAGS MUST NOT LIVE FOREVER)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Every flag should be temporary.

End states:
- remove the flag once behavior is stable and canonical
- or lock the flag permanently on/off via profile and delete the runtime control

Rules:
- abandoned flags must be cleaned up
- dead code paths must be removed
- experiments must conclude

A platform littered with flags is not understandable.
Understandability is a requirement.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Feature flags must:
- be governed, owned, and auditable
- be evaluated at defined gate points
- support rollout, experiments, and kill switches safely
- be reconstructable from logs per correlation_id
- respect the hierarchy: entitlements → profiles → flags

Feature flags must never:
- alter Truth Gate semantics, HouseState law, or safety categories
- bypass confirmation/clarification requirements
- relax secrets handling or sanitization
- alter persisted schemas without migration discipline
- become a hidden fork mechanism

Flags ship safely only when they remain tools, not architecture.

END OF 32.2


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 32.3
Licensing and Entitlements

Ashby is a platform.
Products built on Ashby may be free, paid, or tiered.

Licensing and entitlements exist to control:
- which product surfaces are available
- which integrations may be configured
- which advanced capabilities are allowed
- which usage limits apply

Entitlements must never compromise safety.
Entitlements must never lie about reality.
Entitlements must never become “security by obscurity.”

This subsection defines licensing and entitlements as a governed gate,
separate from scope authority and separate from truth law.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1) DEFINITIONS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

LICENSE
A commercial or contractual grant that enables a bundle of entitlements.

ENTITLEMENT
A concrete, machine-evaluable permission to use a feature surface.

Examples (illustrative only):
- enable_cloud_fallback
- enable_meetings_agent
- enable_inventory_connector_shopify
- enable_multi_site_scopes
- enable_advanced_automation
- enable_audit_exports

LIMIT
A quantitative constraint applied to an entitlement.
Examples (illustrative only):
- max_scopes
- max_connectors
- max_devices
- max_monthly_actions
- max_storage_gb

SCOPE AUTHORITY (NOT LICENSING)
Scope authority is the world-model permission system:
- who can control what
- within which scope
- with which permissions

Licensing is not the authority model.
Licensing determines what product surfaces exist.

SECURITY (NOT LICENSING)
Security and secrets handling protect credentials and sensitive data.
Licensing does not excuse weaker security.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2) WHY ENTITLEMENTS MUST BE EXPLICIT
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Productization pressure creates a temptation:
- “just hide the button”
- “just block it in the UI”
- “just let it fail quietly”

Those approaches are incompatible with Ashby because:
- they create confusing user experiences
- they create hidden forks (different behavior depending on invisible state)
- they make support impossible
- they encourage the system to invent reasons (“it didn’t work”)
- they harm rebuildability

Entitlements must be explicit, deterministic, and visible in logs.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
3) THE ENTITLEMENT CHECK POINTS (WHERE ENFORCEMENT OCCURS)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Entitlements must be enforced at defined gates, not scattered.

Canonical gates:

A) SURFACE GATE (DISCOVERY/UI)
- whether a feature surface is shown or offered
- prevents users from forming expectations about unavailable features

B) INTENT GATE (ROUTER PRE-PLANNING)
- whether an intent is valid under the current product/entitlements
- if not, the correct outcome is:
  - REFUSE with honest explanation
  - or CLARIFY if the user might be asking for a different available action

C) ACTIONREQUEST GATE (PRE-EXECUTION)
- whether an ActionRequest may be executed at all
- critical for preventing “silent no-op” behavior

D) CONFIG GATE (SETUP/ONBOARDING)
- whether a connector or module can be configured
- prevents storing unusable configurations

These gates ensure that entitlements affect availability without corrupting truth.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
4) ENTITLEMENTS VS TRUTH (A CRITICAL SEPARATION)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Entitlements control permission, not reality.

Rules:
- if an action is not entitled, Ashby must not claim it attempted execution
- if an action is not entitled, Ashby must not claim it failed “due to a device”
- the system must speak plainly: “This feature isn’t enabled in your product.”

Entitlements must not change:
- what VERIFIED means
- what LAST_KNOWN means
- what UNKNOWN means
- what FAILED means

If entitlements alter truth posture, the platform becomes untrustworthy.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
5) ENTITLEMENTS VS SAFETY (ENTITLEMENT DOES NOT EQUAL APPROVAL)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Having an entitlement does not mean an action should execute immediately.

Entitlements answer:
- “is this feature allowed to exist?”

Safety policy answers:
- “should this action happen now?”
- “is clarification required?”
- “is confirmation required?”
- “is refusal required?”

Example:
A user may be entitled to “inventory.adjust.”
That does not remove confirmation requirements for high-impact mutations.

Entitlements may not bypass safety gates.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
6) ENTITLEMENT SCOPE (WHO OR WHAT IS LICENSED)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Entitlements must be scoped.

Possible entitlement scopes (illustrative):
- instance-wide (the whole Ashby instance)
- per user
- per organization scope
- per business scope
- per physical site
- per device class

Rules:
- entitlement scope must be explicit
- entitlement enforcement must respect Ashby scopes
- entitlement decisions must be logged with the applicable scope_id

Licensing often aligns to organizational boundaries.
Ashby already has scope boundaries.
Entitlements must bind to them.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
7) OFFLINE CAPABILITY (LICENSING MUST NOT REQUIRE CONSTANT CLOUD)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Ashby is designed to be offline-capable.

Licensing and entitlements must not assume constant cloud access.

Requirements:
- entitlement evaluation must have an offline-valid mode
- caches must have explicit freshness rules (staleness is real)
- if an entitlement check cannot be performed due to offline state:
  - the system must choose a safe posture
  - it must not silently “grant everything”
  - it must not falsely “deny everything” without stating uncertainty

The safe posture must be explicit in policy and logged.

This preserves integrity under connectivity loss.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
8) SECURITY AND PRIVACY (LICENSE KEYS ARE SENSITIVE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Licensing systems often involve:
- license keys
- entitlement tokens
- signed claims
- renewal credentials

Rules:
- licensing secrets are treated as secrets
- license keys and entitlement tokens must never appear in logs
- license keys must never be embedded in artifacts
- entitlement decisions may be logged as:
  - entitlement name
  - allowed/denied
  - reason code
  - scope_id
  - correlation_id
without any secret payload

Licensing must never become an avenue for secret leakage.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
9) OBSERVABILITY AND SUPPORT (WHY WAS IT DENIED?)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

When a feature is denied, support must be able to reconstruct why.

Logs must allow answering:
- what entitlement was required
- whether it was present
- whether a limit was exceeded
- whether the check was stale/unknown due to offline state
- what gate denied it (surface, intent, pre-exec, config)
- what the user was trying to do (intent type, not raw secrets)

Denials must be deterministic and explainable.
Mystery denials create distrust.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
10) MIGRATION AND TIER CHANGES (UPGRADES MUST NOT CORRUPT STATE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Users may upgrade or downgrade.

Rules:
- upgrading enables new surfaces; it must not rewrite old artifacts
- downgrading disables new surfaces; it must not destroy data
- disabled modules may leave artifacts behind; those artifacts remain accessible
  only through governed storage access paths
- connectors disabled by tier must not continue to execute silently
- any persistent configuration changes must follow schema and migration law

Product tier shifts are not an excuse for state corruption.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
11) THE CORE FAILURE MODE: ENTITLEMENTS AS HIDDEN FORKS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Entitlement systems fail when they are implemented as scattered exceptions.

Examples of forbidden drift:
- adapters behave differently under different tiers without logs
- Truth Gate claims change under different tiers
- state schemas change under different tiers
- safety prompts disappear under “pro” tier

Tiers may change what is available.
They may not change what is true or safe.

Entitlements must remain a clean gate, not a behavioral rewrite.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Licensing and entitlements must:
- be explicit, deterministic, and auditable
- gate feature availability at defined points (surface/intent/pre-exec/config)
- respect scope boundaries and record scope_id in decisions
- remain offline-capable with explicit safe posture when checks are stale/unknown
- keep license secrets out of logs and artifacts
- preserve truth posture and safety behavior regardless of tier
- support upgrades/downgrades without corrupting data or state

Licensing and entitlements must never:
- modify Truth Gate semantics or HouseState law
- bypass confirmation/clarification requirements
- treat UI hiding as the enforcement mechanism
- scatter hidden tier-specific behavior throughout core logic
- leak license keys/tokens through logs, errors, or artifacts
- destroy artifacts or state on downgrade

Entitlements decide what may be used.
Ashby still decides what is true.

END OF 32.3


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 33.1
Required Build Order

Rebuilding Ashby is not a feature checklist.
It is the re-establishment of invariants in the correct dependency order.

Ashby must be rebuilt as a platform body first.
Only after the body is correct may modules, agents, bridges, and products be added.

The build order below is required because later layers assume the correctness of earlier layers.
Skipping the order creates a system that may appear functional while violating truth, state, and safety law.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
FOUNDATIONAL PRINCIPLE: BUILD THE BODY BEFORE THE ORGANS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Ashby’s “body” is the shared substrate:
- engine and policy
- router and execution orchestration
- Truth Gate enforcement
- HouseState authority and staleness/decay
- scopes and identity
- security and secrets handling
- logging and observability
- acceptance tests and migration discipline

Modules and agents are organs:
- they add capability surfaces (WHAT)
- they consume the body’s execution and truth pipeline (HOW)
- they are removable

The build order therefore proceeds:
BODY → INTERFACES → EXECUTION → MODULES → BRIDGES/NATIVE → PRODUCTS.
