──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PHASE 0 — CANONICAL MATERIAL INTEGRITY (READ BEFORE CODE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Before implementing anything, establish the reconstruction corpus:

0.1 Canonical structure
- the Table of Contents (numbering, titles, ordering, part boundaries)

0.2 Canonical invariants
- preservation packs (mechanical rules)
- architecture tree vision (folder boundaries and growth rules)

0.3 Canonical law
- platform principles
- truth law and state law

Required outcome of Phase 0:
- the builder understands that “working code” is not sufficient;
  only invariant-compliant code is Ashby.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PHASE 1 — REPO SKELETON AND BOUNDARY ENFORCEMENT (ARCHITECTURE FIRST)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Create the directory skeleton and enforce dependency direction before implementing logic.

Required actions:
1.1 Instantiate the architecture tree (folders and boundaries)
- engine/
- scopes/
- domain/
- modules/
- adapters/
- bridges/
- native_vendor/
- connectors/
- io/
- storage/
- packaging/
- support/
- tests/

1.2 Enforce import boundaries early
- engine/ must not import bridges/ or native_vendor/
- modules/ must not import bridges/ or native_vendor/
- domain/ must not import adapters/ or bridges/ or vendors
- adapters/ may depend on provider implementations, but providers must be swappable

1.3 Create “boundary tests”
- automated checks that fail if forbidden dependencies appear
  (static analysis, build rules, or explicit import allowlists)

Why this phase must be first:
- the easiest way to accidentally fork Ashby is to let execution and vendor logic
  leak upward into meaning and policy.
- boundary violations become harder to remove after features ship.

Required outcome of Phase 1:
- a repo that is structurally incapable of collapsing WHAT into HOW by accident.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PHASE 2 — MECHANICAL PRIMITIVES (SCHEMAS + DATA MODELS + VALIDATION)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Implement the mechanical primitives that all later layers depend on.

2.1 Config schemas
- implement the config schema system and strict validation
- define canonical configuration shapes for:
  - profiles
  - connectors
  - provider credentials references (not secrets)
  - routing preferences
  - logging levels
  - retention/redaction policies

2.2 Canonical data models
- implement canonical structures and validators for:
  - Intent
  - ActionRequest
  - ActionResult
  - verification semantics
  - failure taxonomy

2.3 Serialization and persistence shapes
- define stable JSON representations for these models
- ensure they are forward-compatible under migration discipline

Why this phase must be early:
- without strict schemas and data models, later behavior becomes “whatever the code happened to do.”
- Ashby must be rebuildable from text alone; the data shapes are part of that text.

Required outcome of Phase 2:
- the system cannot accept malformed intents or produce unstructured, unverifiable execution outcomes.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PHASE 3 — OBSERVABILITY AND SECURITY (BASELINE SAFETY INFRASTRUCTURE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Implement the infrastructure that makes later debugging and safety possible.

3.1 Logging and observability
- correlation_id generation and propagation must exist
- structured logs must exist for:
  - intent normalization outcome
  - policy outcome
  - plan generation
  - execution attempts
  - evidence returned
  - Truth Gate classification
  - HouseState update decisions
- log export must exist as a support capability

3.2 Security and secrets handling
- secrets must be storable and retrievable via a secret system
- secrets must never appear in logs or artifacts
- connectors/providers must reference secret handles, not embed credentials
- sanitization rules must exist for errors and traces

Why this phase must be early:
- if security and logs are added later, early integrations will bake in leaks and invisible failures.
- resurrection depends on auditability.

Required outcome of Phase 3:
- every action can be traced, and nothing sensitive leaks through the trace.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PHASE 4 — SCOPES AND IDENTITY (WORLD MODEL FOUNDATION)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Implement the world model before device control.

4.1 Scopes
- define scope types and containment rules
- implement scope-bound authority evaluation
- implement scope-aware registry lookup for targets

4.2 Identity
- implement stable identity keys:
  - device_key and/or entity_key (platform-owned)
  - external id mappings (bridge ids, vendor ids, connector ids)
- implement name/label handling as human aliases, not canonical identity

Why this phase must precede execution:
- execution without scope and identity becomes “commands against unknown targets.”
- migrations become impossible if identity is not stable from the start.

Required outcome of Phase 4:
- Ashby can name what exists and bound authority to a scope without relying on any vendor’s naming.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PHASE 5 — TRUTH GATE + HOUSESTATE (REALITY AND MEMORY LAW)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Truth and state must exist before you allow behavior to present certainty.

5.1 Truth Gate enforcement
- implement truth classes and language constraints
- implement the rule: ActionResult is reality evidence, not narrative
- implement forbidden phrase prevention and posture enforcement

5.2 HouseState
- implement belief storage with:
  - confidence
  - staleness/decay
  - mixed reality support (partial success, contradictions)
  - persistence and restart behavior

5.3 Sync and staleness discipline
- implement how evidence sources update HouseState
- implement decay when evidence streams go silent
- implement explicit UNKNOWN when reality is not currently observable

Why this phase must precede product UI:
- a UI can make lies look polished.
- Ashby’s core promise is truth integrity, even when inconvenient.

Required outcome of Phase 5:
- the system can only speak with the certainty the evidence supports,
  and it can persist that posture across restarts.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PHASE 6 — THE CANONICAL CONTROL PIPELINE (ENGINE + ROUTER SHAPE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Now implement the core operating loop.

6.1 Input normalization and intent extraction
- transport-neutral input ingestion
- normalized Intent production

6.2 Policy
- policy must choose outcomes (ACT / CLARIFY / CONFIRM / REFUSE / CHAT)
- policy must be consistent and auditable
- policy must not be overridden by modules

6.3 Planning
- convert intent + context into ActionRequests
- enforce WHAT vs HOW separation
- enforce that plans do not contain vendor-specific execution logic

6.4 Routing and adapter invocation
- router selects adapters by capability and availability
- adapters execute through providers and return ActionResults

6.5 Truth and response construction
- Truth Gate evaluates ActionResults and HouseState updates
- response is constructed with correct language posture

Why this phase must occur after truth/state:
- an engine without truth/state becomes a chatbot.
- an engine with truth/state becomes Ashby.

Required outcome of Phase 6:
- a complete closed loop where:
  input → policy → plan → execute → evidence → truth/state → response
  is consistent and auditable.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PHASE 7 — INTERFACE CONTRACTS AND ADAPTER SYSTEM (HOW DISCIPLINE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Before writing many features, lock the HOW layer discipline.

7.1 Interface contracts
- define capability contracts
- define ActionRequest and ActionResult fields for execution attempts
- define verification responsibility expectations

7.2 Adapter system
- implement capability-based adapter grouping
- enforce “adapters never reason, never store memory, never decide”
- implement offline-first preference and cloud-allowed fallback under policy
- implement idempotency handling where required

7.3 Provider isolation
- ensure providers (bridge-backed or native) can be swapped without module changes
- ensure providers cannot inject meaning or truth upgrades

Required outcome of Phase 7:
- execution pathways are disciplined, replaceable, and verifiable.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PHASE 8 — MINIMUM MODULE SET (PROVE THE PLATFORM)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Now add a minimal set of modules to prove the platform loop.

8.1 Choose representative modules (examples only)
- a simple reactive module (lights-like)
- a time-bound control module (irrigation-like)
- a sensor-driven module (optional)
The specific modules do not matter.
The module pattern does.

8.2 Ensure module behavior is strictly WHAT
- modules output ActionRequests
- modules do not call vendors
- modules do not write HouseState directly

8.3 Implement sessions where required
- session primitives exist so modules can maintain safe multi-turn flows

Required outcome of Phase 8:
- at least one end-to-end capability works without violating invariants.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PHASE 9 — BRIDGES AND NATIVE VENDOR OWNERSHIP (SHIP WITHOUT DELEGATING MEANING)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Only after the platform loop is correct should bridges be added.

9.1 Bridge-first integration (optional early, never permanent)
- add bridge providers (e.g., Home Assistant) behind adapter interfaces
- maintain identity independence and evidence posture

9.2 Protocol bridges
- Zigbee and Matter (direct or via bridge) remain execution sources
- preserve device_key stability across coordinators/fabrics

9.3 Native vendor expansion
- add native providers for high-value vendors
- migrate gradually using the strangler strategy
- preserve identity and behavior parity

Required outcome of Phase 9:
- breadth can be shipped without allowing bridges to become the platform.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PHASE 10 — PRODUCTS: PROFILES, FLAGS, ENTITLEMENTS (SHIP WITHOUT FORKS)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Once the platform is proven, productization can occur.

10.1 Profiles
- declarative manifests that select modules/adapters/providers
- must not alter truth/state law

10.2 Feature flags
- controlled rollouts and kill switches
- must not become hidden forks

10.3 Licensing and entitlements
- gate availability at defined points
- must never alter truth posture or safety policy categories

Required outcome of Phase 10:
- Ashby can ship as many products without becoming many Ashbys.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PHASE 11 — ACCEPTANCE TESTS, MIGRATION, AND GOVERNANCE (KEEP IT ALIVE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Finally, lock longevity.

11.1 Acceptance tests and harness
- implement the harness that verifies invariants, evidence posture, and behavior parity
- ensure every major execution surface has regression coverage

11.2 Migration discipline
- implement schema migrations and structural realignment playbooks
- migrations must be explicit, versioned, and reversible where possible

11.3 Governance hooks
- implement Control Sync triggers and enforcement boundaries
- prevent foundational changes from occurring as “implementation panic”

Required outcome of Phase 11:
- the platform can evolve without collapse.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

This build order must be followed because:
- truth and state must exist before claims are made
- identity and scopes must exist before execution can be safe
- observability and secrets must exist before integrations scale
- interfaces and adapter law must exist before vendor breadth is added
- profiles must exist instead of forks if shipping is to survive

A system that violates the build order may “work.”
It will not be Ashby.

END OF 33.1


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 33.2
What Must Exist First

This subsection defines the minimum kernel that must exist before any “feature work”
is allowed to proceed.

If these prerequisites do not exist, any subsequent implementation becomes:
- untestable
- un-auditable
- migration-hostile
- truth-corrupting

This is not a suggestion.
These are stop-lines.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
0) THE FIRST RULE: INVARIANTS BEFORE CAPABILITIES
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The platform must be lawful before it is useful.

“Useful” code that violates invariants produces a false Ashby:
- it can act
- but it cannot be trusted
- and it cannot be rebuilt

Therefore the first existence requirements are the ones that enforce law.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1) A CANONICAL REPO SHAPE WITH ENFORCED BOUNDARIES
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Before writing core logic, the builder must have:

- the architecture tree folders instantiated
- a dependency direction policy enforced
- automated boundary checks

Minimum boundary guarantees:
- modules cannot import bridges or vendors
- engine cannot import bridges or vendors
- domain cannot import execution layers
- providers are swappable behind interfaces

Stop-line:
If forbidden dependencies are possible, do not proceed.
You are building a fork factory.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2) CANONICAL DATA MODELS + STRICT VALIDATION
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The system must have canonical data models for:
- Intent
- ActionRequest
- ActionResult
- verification semantics
- failure taxonomy

And it must enforce validation:
- parsing must reject malformed inputs
- execution outputs must be structurally constrained
- failures must be classified, not hand-waved

Stop-line:
If ActionRequests and ActionResults are not canonical and validated,
do not proceed to real execution.
You will produce evidence that cannot be trusted or migrated.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
3) CORRELATION AND TRACEABILITY (OBSERVABILITY BASELINE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Before any adapter/provider integration:

- correlation_id exists
- it propagates through the pipeline
- logs are structured
- logs record:
  - input intent (sanitized)
  - policy outcome
  - plan summary (WHAT)
  - execution attempts (HOW providers called)
  - returned evidence
  - truth classification
  - state update decisions

Stop-line:
If an execution attempt cannot be reconstructed from logs,
do not integrate a vendor or bridge.
Debugging without traces becomes mythology.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
4) SECRETS HANDLING (SECURITY BASELINE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Before any connector/provider integration:

- a secret store exists
- credentials are referenced by handles, not stored in config
- sanitization prevents secrets from leaking into logs/errors/artifacts
- rotation and revocation are possible

Stop-line:
If secrets can appear in logs or artifacts, do not connect anything.
A leaked key compromises the entire environment.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
5) SCOPES + IDENTITY (WORLD MODEL MUST EXIST)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Before you control anything:

- scope model exists
- permissions/authority evaluation exists
- stable identities exist:
  - device_keys (or equivalent platform-owned ids)
  - external id mapping structures

Stop-line:
If targets cannot be resolved into a scoped, stable identity,
do not execute.
You will create irreversible actions against ambiguous reality.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
6) TRUTH GATE ENFORCEMENT (NO CLAIMS WITHOUT EVIDENCE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Before the system is allowed to say “done”:

- Truth Gate exists and is applied to all outcomes
- truth classes are enforced
- forbidden phrases are blocked
- inference is not upgraded into certainty
- ActionResult evidence is the sole basis of reality claims

Stop-line:
If the system can claim success without evidence,
do not proceed.
You have built a liar.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
7) HOUSESTATE AUTHORITY (STATE MUST BE SINGULAR AND DECAYING)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Before the system is allowed to “remember”:

- HouseState exists as a canonical belief store
- beliefs have confidence and staleness/decay
- mixed reality and partial success are representable
- restart and persistence behavior is defined and tested

Stop-line:
If state is implemented as ad-hoc caches or agent memory,
do not proceed.
You have built fragmentation.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
8) A MINIMUM ENGINE LOOP (POLICY → PLAN → EXECUTE → EVIDENCE → TRUTH)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Before adding modules:

- the canonical control pipeline exists end-to-end
- policy produces bounded outcomes (ACT/CLARIFY/CONFIRM/REFUSE/CHAT)
- planning produces ActionRequests without vendor leakage
- execution produces ActionResults
- Truth Gate constrains response language and state updates

Stop-line:
If the loop cannot run end-to-end with a stub adapter,
do not add features.
You will accumulate “modules” that cannot be trusted.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
9) TEST HARNESS SKELETON (EVEN IF SMALL)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Before real integrations:

- a test harness exists
- at least one golden flow exists end-to-end
- boundary tests exist
- truth posture tests exist

Stop-line:
If you cannot test invariants, you cannot preserve them.
A system without tests will drift.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
10) THE FIRST VALID DEMONSTRATION (MINIMUM PROOF)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Only after all prerequisites exist should the system demonstrate:

- a single module (lights-like) that:
  - takes intent
  - produces ActionRequests
  - executes via an adapter
  - returns ActionResults
  - updates state through Truth Gate
  - responds with correct truth posture

This proof is required because it demonstrates:
- policy outcomes are bounded
- evidence pipeline is real
- state is authoritative
- language is constrained

If this proof cannot be achieved, do not proceed to breadth.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

What must exist first is the platform kernel:
- boundaries
- schemas
- models
- correlation and logs
- secrets handling
- scopes and identity
- Truth Gate
- HouseState
- the closed-loop pipeline
- a minimal test harness

Everything else can be added later.
If these do not exist, everything added later will be wrong.

END OF 33.2


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 33.3
What Must Never Be Skipped

Rebuilding Ashby fails in predictable ways.

Most failures do not look like crashes.
They look like “it works” while the system’s promises quietly rot.

This subsection lists the non-skippable elements that preserve Ashby’s identity.

These are not “engineering best practices.”
They are Ashby’s definition.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1) WHAT vs HOW SEPARATION (THE PLATFORM’S SPINE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Never skip:
- modules express meaning (WHAT) only
- adapters/providers express execution (HOW) only
- vendors and protocols never enter meaning logic
- bridges and native integrations remain swappable providers

If skipped:
- the platform becomes vendor-shaped
- migrations become impossible
- every new integration becomes a new fork

This is the most common irreversible failure.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2) ACTIONRESULT AS REALITY (THE EVIDENCE LAW)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Never skip:
- ActionResult is the authoritative evidence object
- claims about the world are derived from ActionResult + Truth Gate rules
- “I did it” is forbidden without evidence

If skipped:
- the platform becomes fluent rather than truthful
- success becomes a narrative style, not an observed outcome

Ashby dies the moment it claims reality without evidence.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
3) TRUTH GATE ENFORCEMENT (LANGUAGE MUST BE CONSTRAINED)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Never skip:
- truth classes (VERIFIED / LAST_KNOWN / UNKNOWN / FAILED)
- forbidden phrase prevention
- consistent language posture per truth class
- explicit mixed reality when evidence conflicts

If skipped:
- outputs become “confident summaries”
- users are misled even when the system is internally uncertain
- contradictions are smoothed into lies

Truth Gate is not a module.
It is a law.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
4) HOUSESTATE AS THE SINGLE STATE AUTHORITY (NO SHADOW REALITIES)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Never skip:
- a single, scoped HouseState belief store
- confidence and decay
- restart/persistence behavior
- explicit representation of partial success and contradictions

If skipped:
- each module/agent creates its own memory
- the platform fragments into competing realities
- long-lived consistency becomes impossible

A multi-agent Ashby without shared state is not Ashby.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
5) SCOPES AND IDENTITY (SAFE TARGETING)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Never skip:
- scopes as the world model
- scope-bound authority checks
- stable platform-owned identity (device_keys)
- external id mappings for bridges/vendors/connectors

If skipped:
- actions are executed against ambiguous targets
- “the kitchen light” becomes a guess
- migrations destroy continuity

Identity continuity is resurrection continuity.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
6) SECURITY AND SECRETS HANDLING (NO LEAKS, NO BACKDOORS)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Never skip:
- secrets are handled only by secret systems
- secrets never appear in logs or artifacts
- sanitization is mandatory
- least-privilege integration posture where possible

If skipped:
- credentials leak
- the system becomes unsafe to operate
- rebuild becomes impossible because records cannot be shared safely

A platform that leaks secrets cannot be preserved in print.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
7) LOGGING AND OBSERVABILITY (RECONSTRUCTABILITY)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Never skip:
- correlation_id everywhere
- structured logs across the pipeline
- ability to export logs for audit and support
- explicit provider attribution (which path executed)

If skipped:
- debugging becomes folklore
- failures become irreproducible
- migrations become blind
- trust cannot be maintained

A system that cannot explain itself cannot be resurrected.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
8) ACCEPTANCE TESTS AND HARNESS (DRIFT PREVENTION)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Never skip:
- a harness that validates:
  - truth posture
  - state updates
  - boundary enforcement
  - execution evidence integrity
- regression tests for core flows

If skipped:
- the platform drifts silently
- new integrations degrade old ones
- policy and truth semantics erode over time

Ashby without tests becomes “another assistant” slowly.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
9) MIGRATION DISCIPLINE (LONGEVITY LAW)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Never skip:
- explicit schema versioning
- migrations for persisted shapes
- identity-preserving structural realignment
- reversible changes where possible

If skipped:
- updates destroy continuity
- old artifacts become unreadable
- state becomes unrecoverable after changes

A system without migrations is a disposable toy.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
10) BRIDGES AS TEMPORARY AND REPLACEABLE (NO PLATFORM DELEGATION)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Never skip:
- bridge isolation
- bridge optionality
- bridge outputs treated as evidence
- strangler exit path toward native ownership

If skipped:
- the bridge becomes the platform
- Ashby becomes a UI over someone else’s worldview
- replacement becomes impossible

Shipping via bridges is allowed.
Delegating identity is forbidden.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
11) PROFILES OVER FORKS (PRODUCTIZATION WITHOUT FRACTURE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Never skip:
- declarative profiles
- flags and entitlements as gates (not hidden behavior rewrites)
- a single platform law shared by all products

If skipped:
- each product becomes a different Ashby
- support becomes impossible
- upgrades become incompatible

Forks are a slow death, not a dramatic one.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
12) CONTROL SYNC GOVERNANCE (FOUNDATIONAL CHANGE LAW)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Never skip:
- the rule that foundational changes require deliberate governance
- the separation between:
  - implementation iteration
  - and constitutional revision

If skipped:
- panic changes rewrite invariants
- the Codex becomes untrue
- the platform loses its anchor

Ashby changes only by Control Sync.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

What must never be skipped are the identity-defining laws:
- WHAT vs HOW separation
- ActionResult as evidence
- Truth Gate enforcement
- HouseState authority + decay
- scopes and stable identity
- secrets handling
- logs and reconstructability
- tests and harness
- migrations
- bridges as temporary
- profiles over forks
- governance by Control Sync

If any of these are missing, the system may still appear functional.
It will not be Ashby.

END OF 33.3


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 34.1
When Control Sync Is Required

Control Sync is constitutional governance.

It is the only lawful mechanism for changing Ashby’s foundational definition:
the invariants that make Ashby remain Ashby across time, teams, and implementations.

Control Sync is not a meeting.
It is not a refactor.
It is not a release.
It is a deliberate revision of the platform’s constitution.

This subsection defines exactly when Control Sync is required.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1) DEFINITION: WHAT CONTROL SYNC IS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Control Sync is the process used to revise and realign the canonical sources of truth:

- Table of Contents (structure authority)
- Continuity Lock (authorial and output constraints)
- Platform Principles (identity law)
- Architecture Tree Vision (folder boundaries and growth law)
- Preservation Packs (mechanical invariants: schemas, models, truth gate, tests, migration, security, logging, interface contracts, state sync, multi-agent coexistence)
- Acceptance Tests and Harness (drift prevention)
- Migration Playbook (continuity through change)

Control Sync exists because foundational drift does not announce itself.
It accumulates invisibly until Ashby stops being Ashby.

Control Sync is required when the platform constitution must change.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2) THE CORE TRIGGER: ANY CHANGE TO AN INVARIANT
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Control Sync is required for any change that alters an invariant.

An invariant is any rule that defines:
- what Ashby is
- what Ashby is allowed to do
- how Ashby must represent truth and uncertainty
- how Ashby must store and decay state
- how Ashby must separate meaning from execution
- how Ashby must remain rebuildable from the Codex

If the change would make an older Codex section “no longer correct,”
Control Sync is required.

If the change would make a preservation pack “no longer binding,”
Control Sync is required.

If the change would make the architecture tree “no longer valid,”
Control Sync is required.

If the change would require changing the Table of Contents to remain coherent,
Control Sync is required.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
3) SPECIFIC CATEGORIES THAT ALWAYS REQUIRE CONTROL SYNC
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Control Sync is always required for changes to any of the following:

A) STRUCTURE AUTHORITY
- renumbering, renaming, or reordering the Codex
- adding, removing, or merging top-level Parts
- redefining what a section number represents

B) TRUTH LAW
- changing truth classes or semantics (VERIFIED / LAST_KNOWN / UNKNOWN / FAILED)
- changing the meaning of “verification”
- changing forbidden phrase law or response posture constraints
- changing the rule that ActionResult evidence governs claims
- changing how mixed outcomes and contradictions are represented

C) HOUSESTATE LAW
- changing what HouseState may store
- changing staleness/decay rules
- changing confidence rules
- changing restart persistence semantics
- changing how contradictions and partial success are encoded

D) WHAT vs HOW SEPARATION
- allowing vendor/protocol logic to enter engine or module meaning
- allowing modules to execute directly
- allowing adapters to reason, decide policy, or define truth
- changing the boundary rules that keep meaning clean

E) IDENTITY AND SCOPE LAW
- changing canonical identity keys (device_key, entity_key, etc.)
- changing scope semantics or permission models
- changing external-id mapping rules (bridge/vendor/connector ids)

F) SECURITY AND SECRETS LAW
- changing secret handling rules
- changing sanitization rules
- changing logging restrictions related to sensitive data
- changing credential lifecycle expectations

G) OBSERVABILITY LAW
- changing correlation_id requirements
- changing required log fields for reconstructability
- changing traceability guarantees for execution evidence

H) ACCEPTANCE TEST AND HARNESS LAW
- modifying what the harness guarantees
- modifying golden flows in ways that redefine correctness
- removing tests that enforce invariants

I) MIGRATION AND STRUCTURAL REALIGNMENT LAW
- changing schema versioning rules
- changing migration methodology
- changing how identity continuity is preserved across changes

J) MULTI-AGENT COEXISTENCE LAW
- allowing parallel truth/state systems
- changing what “shared truth and state” means
- changing how agents may share artifacts or state

K) PRODUCTIZATION GOVERNANCE LAW
- changing the definition of profiles vs forks
- allowing flags/entitlements to alter truth, safety, or state semantics

These are constitutional surfaces.
They cannot be altered by “implementation iteration.”

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
4) CONTROL SYNC IS REQUIRED WHEN DOCUMENTS DISAGREE
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Control Sync is required when canonical documents conflict.

Conflicts include:
- a preservation pack mandates behavior that the Codex contradicts
- the architecture tree mandates boundaries the repo violates
- the platform principles are no longer reflected in implementation
- acceptance tests encode a different truth posture than the Truth Gate doctrine

When conflicts exist, Ashby becomes ambiguous.
Ambiguity is drift.

Control Sync resolves ambiguity by producing a single aligned constitution.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
5) CONTROL SYNC IS REQUIRED BEFORE “FOUNDATIONAL FIXES”
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Control Sync is required before implementing any “foundational fix”
that would otherwise be justified as “necessary to make it work.”

Examples of foundational fixes (representative):
- “the Truth Gate rules are too strict, relax them”
- “HouseState decay is annoying, remove it”
- “vendor logic belongs in the engine for speed”
- “adapters should decide truth because they know the backend”
- “profiles should change the engine because customers want different behavior”

If “making it work” demands weakening invariants,
Control Sync must occur before the change is allowed.

Implementation pressure is not authority.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
6) THE CONTROL SYNC PROCEDURE (REQUIRED OUTPUTS)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

When Control Sync is required, the following procedure is mandatory.

A) DECLARATION
- declare that a Control Sync is being initiated
- name the reason
- name the impacted constitutional surfaces
- freeze foundational changes outside the sync until completion

B) BASELINE SNAPSHOT
- record the current constitution version:
  - Codex numbering and titles
  - preservation pack versions
  - architecture tree
  - acceptance test baseline
  - migration baseline

C) PROPOSED AMENDMENTS
For each change:
- specify what is changing
- specify the invariant being modified
- specify the new invariant language
- specify consequences and new constraints
- specify what becomes invalidated

D) COMPATIBILITY PLAN
- specify migrations required
- specify acceptance tests required or updated
- specify how identity continuity is preserved
- specify how old artifacts remain readable

E) TRUTH AND SAFETY AUDIT
- verify the amendment does not secretly weaken truth posture
- verify it does not weaken security or secrets handling
- verify it does not reduce reconstructability

F) ALIGNMENT UPDATE
Update the canonical corpus together:
- Table of Contents (if structure changes)
- Continuity Lock (if authorship constraints change)
- Platform Principles (if identity law changes)
- Architecture Tree Vision (if boundaries change)
- Preservation Packs (if mechanical rules change)
- Acceptance Tests (to enforce the new law)

G) SIGN-OFF AND VERSIONING
- produce a Control Sync ID (date + sequence)
- record a diff summary of amendments
- lock the new constitution as canonical for future work

H) POST-SYNC VALIDATION
- run the acceptance harness
- verify boundary enforcement
- verify truth posture under representative flows
- verify migrations on a clean baseline

No implementation may claim to be “Ashby” under the new law
until post-sync validation is complete.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
7) THE CONTROL SYNC DOCTRINE (THE KEY RULE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A foundational change is not real until it is synced.

“Implementation first, documentation later” is forbidden for invariants.
That pattern produces drift and retroactive rationalization.

Control Sync exists to prevent Ashby’s constitution from being rewritten by accident.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Control Sync is required whenever:
- an invariant changes
- canonical documents conflict
- the platform constitution must be revised to remain coherent
- a foundational fix would otherwise weaken or alter doctrine
- the structure authority (TOC) must change
- truth/state/scope/security/observability governance must change

Control Sync is required before the change is implemented,
not after.

Ashby remains Ashby only by governed revision.

END OF 34.1


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 34.2
When It Is Forbidden

Control Sync is constitutional governance.

Because it is powerful, it must be rare.

Control Sync is forbidden whenever it is being used as:
- a shortcut
- a justification
- an emotional release valve
- a substitute for disciplined implementation

This subsection defines when Control Sync must not be invoked.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1) CONTROL SYNC IS FORBIDDEN FOR NORMAL IMPLEMENTATION WORK
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Control Sync is forbidden for ordinary iteration.

Ordinary iteration includes (representative):
- implementing a module using existing interfaces
- adding a new adapter/provider under existing contracts
- fixing a bug in a provider
- improving performance without changing correctness semantics
- extending a connector while preserving evidence shapes
- adding new tests to enforce existing invariants
- refactoring internal code while preserving boundaries and behavior

If the change does not modify an invariant,
Control Sync is forbidden.

The platform constitution is not rewritten to accommodate routine engineering.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2) CONTROL SYNC IS FORBIDDEN AS A RESPONSE TO PANIC
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Control Sync is forbidden during “implementation panic.”

Implementation panic is the moment when a problem feels urgent and the temptation is:
- “just move this into the engine”
- “just skip the Truth Gate for now”
- “just write directly to state”
- “just treat the bridge output as truth”
- “just add a special-case flag”

These are drift impulses.

The correct response to panic is:
- pause execution changes
- use kill switches or rollbacks
- add observability
- fix the implementation within existing law
- escalate only if the law is genuinely insufficient

Panic is not authority.
Panic is the environment where forks are born.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
3) CONTROL SYNC IS FORBIDDEN TO HIDE PRODUCT DIFFERENCES
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Control Sync is forbidden as a mechanism to embed product identity in core.

Forbidden motivations include:
- “Enterprise needs different truth posture”
- “Consumer wants fewer confirmations, remove them everywhere”
- “This tier should behave differently in the engine”
- “This profile should have different state semantics”

Productization is achieved by:
- profiles
- flags
- entitlements
within the existing constitution.

If the proposed “Control Sync” exists to make two products behave as two platforms,
it is forbidden.

Ashby is one platform.
Products are configurations.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
4) CONTROL SYNC IS FORBIDDEN TO PATCH OVER BAD BOUNDARIES
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Control Sync is forbidden when the real problem is architectural hygiene.

Examples (representative):
- a module has vendor logic and it is inconvenient to remove it
- adapters are reasoning and it is hard to disentangle
- engine imports bridge code because it was “faster”

These are implementation violations.

The correct response is:
- restore boundaries
- move code to lawful layers
- add boundary tests to prevent recurrence

Control Sync is not a license to ratify violations.
Ratifying violations destroys the constitution.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
5) CONTROL SYNC IS FORBIDDEN TO RELAX TRUTH OR SAFETY FOR CONVENIENCE
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Control Sync is forbidden when the motivation is convenience.

Convenience motivations include:
- wanting to claim success without verification
- wanting to hide UNKNOWN
- wanting to collapse mixed outcomes into a polished narrative
- wanting to reduce confirmations by weakening policy categories
- wanting to allow direct execution bypasses for speed

Truth posture is the core promise.

If the change weakens truth or safety to be “nicer,” it is forbidden.

A correct system may be inconvenient.
An incorrect system is not Ashby.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
6) CONTROL SYNC IS FORBIDDEN TO FIX A SINGLE BUG
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Control Sync is forbidden for single-bug resolution.

Bug fixes are implemented under existing law.

If a bug reveals an invariant deficiency, that is a different claim:
- the invariant itself is insufficient
- multiple features are blocked by the deficiency
- the deficiency cannot be resolved without redefining the law

Only then can Control Sync be considered.

Most problems are not constitutional.
Treating them as constitutional is governance failure.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
7) CONTROL SYNC IS FORBIDDEN WITHOUT A COMPLETE AMENDMENT PACKAGE
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Control Sync is forbidden if it does not produce a complete constitutional update.

A lawful Control Sync requires:
- explicit amendment language
- alignment across all canonical artifacts impacted
- updated acceptance tests where behavior is affected
- migration implications explicitly handled
- versioned sign-off

If the proposal is:
- vague
- incomplete
- “we’ll update docs later”
- “we’ll fix tests later”
then Control Sync is forbidden.

A half-sync is worse than no sync.
It creates ambiguity.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
8) CONTROL SYNC IS FORBIDDEN WHEN A SAFER TOOL EXISTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Many urgent situations have safer tools than constitutional revision.

Allowed safer tools include:
- kill switches (disable unsafe execution paths)
- feature flags (rollout control)
- profile adjustments (enable/disable modules)
- routing preference changes (prefer a safer provider)
- rollback to known-good behavior
- increased observability to diagnose reality

If a safer tool can resolve the situation without changing invariants,
Control Sync is forbidden.

Control Sync is not an operational lever.
It is a constitutional lever.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
9) THE GOVERNANCE TEST (A SINGLE QUESTION)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Before invoking Control Sync, ask:

“Will the Codex become false if we do not change the constitution?”

If the answer is no:
Control Sync is forbidden.

If the answer is yes:
Control Sync may be required, but only under the full procedure.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Control Sync is forbidden when:
- the change is routine engineering under existing invariants
- the change is a panic response
- the change is a product/tier fork attempt
- the change is a ratification of boundary violations
- the change weakens truth or safety for convenience
- the change is a single-bug patch
- the amendment package is incomplete
- a safer operational tool exists

Control Sync is rare because Ashby’s constitution must be stable.

Stability is not stagnation.
Stability is continuity.

END OF 34.2


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 35.1
Technical Completion

Technical completion is the engineering definition of “done.”

It does not mean:
- feature-complete
- vendor-complete
- market-complete
- perfect

It means:
- the platform body exists
- the invariants are enforced mechanically
- the system can be rebuilt and validated from the Codex
- the system can evolve without collapsing into drift

Technical completion is achieved when Ashby can be operated safely
and modified safely without requiring constitutional rewrites.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1) COMPLETION IS NOT A FEATURE COUNT
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Technical completion is not measured by:
- number of modules
- number of vendors supported
- UI polish

It is measured by:
- whether the platform loop is correct
- whether truth posture is enforced
- whether state is authoritative and decays correctly
- whether execution evidence is auditable
- whether boundaries prevent vendor leakage
- whether tests prevent drift

A small Ashby can be technically complete.
A large Ashby can be technically incomplete.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2) THE MINIMUM PLATFORM BODY (NON-NEGOTIABLE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Ashby is technically complete only when the body exists and is correct:

A) ENGINE + POLICY
- intent normalization exists
- policy produces bounded outcomes:
  ACT / CLARIFY / CONFIRM / REFUSE / CHAT
- planning produces ActionRequests (WHAT) without vendor leakage

B) ROUTER + ADAPTER SYSTEM
- router selects adapters by capability and availability
- adapters execute through providers and return ActionResults
- adapters do not reason, decide policy, or declare truth

C) TRUTH GATE
- evidence is classified into truth posture
- forbidden phrases are blocked
- language constraints are enforced
- mixed outcomes and contradictions are representable and preserved

D) HOUSESTATE
- beliefs exist as a single scoped authority
- beliefs record timestamps, confidence, and staleness
- decay is enforced when evidence streams go silent
- restart persistence behavior is defined and validated

E) SCOPES + IDENTITY
- scope boundaries exist and are enforced
- stable platform identity keys exist
- external id mapping exists for bridges/vendors/connectors
- naming is aliasing, not identity

F) SECURITY + SECRETS
- secret storage exists
- credentials never appear in logs or artifacts
- sanitization rules are implemented and tested
- least-privilege posture is supported where possible

G) LOGGING + OBSERVABILITY
- correlation_id exists and propagates end-to-end
- logs can reconstruct:
  input → policy → plan → provider attempts → evidence → truth classification → state update
- provider attribution exists
- log export exists for support

If any of these are missing or implemented as informal convention,
technical completion is not achieved.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
3) THE CLOSED LOOP MUST RUN END-TO-END (WITH REAL EVIDENCE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Technical completion requires at least one end-to-end working flow that proves the body:

- a normalized intent is created
- policy outcome is chosen correctly
- ActionRequest is planned correctly
- execution is attempted through an adapter/provider
- ActionResult evidence is returned
- Truth Gate classifies truth posture
- HouseState updates appropriately (or refuses to update)
- response language matches the truth posture

The flow must remain correct under:
- success
- failure
- partial success
- staleness
- identity ambiguity
- permission denial

End-to-end behavior under uncertainty is the real test.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
4) BOUNDARY ENFORCEMENT MUST BE MECHANICAL (NOT TRUST-BASED)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Technical completion requires that the architecture tree boundaries are enforced.

Evidence of enforcement:
- boundary tests fail when:
  - engine imports bridge/vendor code
  - modules import bridge/vendor code
  - domain imports execution layers
- provider interfaces remain swappable without module changes
- bridges remain isolated and optional
- native_vendor remains isolated and optional per provider availability

If boundaries are enforced only by “developer discipline,”
the system is not complete.
It will drift.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
5) ACCEPTANCE TESTS AND HARNESS MUST EXIST (DRIFT PREVENTION)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Technical completion requires an operational harness that verifies invariants.

At minimum, the harness must validate:

A) TRUTH POSTURE
- verified vs last-known vs unknown behavior
- forbidden phrase prevention
- mixed outcome preservation

B) STATE SEMANTICS
- staleness/decay behavior
- persistence/restart behavior
- contradiction representation

C) EXECUTION EVIDENCE
- ActionResult shapes are canonical and validated
- provider attribution is present
- failure taxonomy is used

D) BOUNDARIES
- forbidden dependencies are rejected
- modules cannot execute directly

E) MIGRATION SAFETY (BASIC)
- schema versions are tracked
- at least one migration path exists and is testable

Without a harness, technical completion cannot be claimed.
The platform will silently mutate.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
6) MIGRATION DISCIPLINE MUST BE REAL (CONTINUITY THROUGH CHANGE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Technical completion requires that Ashby can change without destroying history.

Minimum migration requirements:
- persisted schemas have versions
- migrations are explicit and versioned
- migrations are tested on a clean baseline
- identity continuity is preserved across migration
- the system can load old artifacts/state or migrate them forward

A platform that cannot migrate cannot survive iteration.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
7) OPERATIONAL SAFETY MUST BE IMPLEMENTED
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Technical completion requires safe operational behavior:

- kill switches or safe-disable mechanisms exist for risky execution paths
- timeouts and bounded retries exist for execution attempts
- partial results are preserved and surfaced honestly
- offline and degraded modes are represented as staleness and unknown, not lies
- error messages are sanitized and traceable (correlation_id)

“Works on a good day” is not completion.

Completion means:
- predictable behavior under real failure modes.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
8) MODULARITY AND REMOVABILITY MUST BE TRUE
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Technical completion requires that modules and agents are truly removable:

- enabling/disabling modules is declarative (profile-based)
- disabling a module does not break the platform body
- artifacts remain accessible only through governed storage paths
- a bridge being absent does not collapse the platform

If removing a module breaks the core loop,
the platform has inverted its priorities and is not complete.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
9) REBUILD VALIDATION: A CLEAN-ROOM TEST
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The strongest technical completion test is a clean-room rebuild:

- start from a fresh repo
- implement only what the Codex and preservation packs require
- run the acceptance harness
- demonstrate the end-to-end loop under uncertainty
- verify no forbidden dependencies exist
- verify secrets do not leak
- verify logs reconstruct actions and evidence

If the clean-room rebuild cannot succeed,
technical completion is not achieved.

A platform that cannot be rebuilt is not a platform.
It is a one-off project.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Ashby is technically complete only when:
- the platform body exists and enforces invariants mechanically
- the closed loop runs end-to-end with evidence-driven truth posture
- boundaries prevent vendor leakage and bridge takeover
- tests and harness prevent drift
- migrations preserve continuity
- operational safety is real
- modularity and removability are true
- a clean-room rebuild can validate correctness

Technical completion is the moment Ashby becomes self-preserving.

END OF 35.1


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 35.2
CONCEPTUAL COMPLETION

Technical completion means the platform works as code.
Conceptual completion means the platform survives as understanding.

A system can be technically complete and still be non-resurrectable:
- if core terms are vague
- if critical assumptions exist only in a founder’s head
- if “how to reason about changes” is tribal knowledge
- if the same word means different things in different places
- if core constraints are felt but not stated

Conceptual completion is achieved when Ashby can be rebuilt and extended correctly
by people who did not invent it, because the *meaning* is fully preserved.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1) DEFINITION: WHAT “CONCEPTUAL COMPLETION” IS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Conceptual completion exists when the following are true:

A) THE PLATFORM HAS A STABLE INTERNAL LANGUAGE
- terms are defined
- terms are used consistently
- terms do not shift meaning across sections or implementations

B) THE PLATFORM HAS A STABLE MENTAL MODEL
A competent reader can answer, without guessing:
- what Ashby is
- what Ashby is not
- where truth comes from
- where state lives
- how action is gated
- how uncertainty is represented
- how removability works
- how growth is permitted without collapse

C) THE PLATFORM’S CRITICAL JUDGMENTS ARE EXTERNALIZED
Meaning:
- the “why” behind laws is explicit
- the failure modes that created each law are explicit
- the decision rules for change are explicit

D) THE PLATFORM DOES NOT REQUIRE TRIBAL KNOWLEDGE
If correct operation depends on:
- “the right kind of engineer”
- “the original author being around”
- “remembering how we did it last time”
then the platform is not conceptually complete.

Conceptual completion is not additional features.
It is *the removal of hidden assumptions*.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2) WHAT MUST BE CONCEPTUALLY UNAMBIGUOUS (NON-NEGOTIABLE TERMS)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The following primitives must be defined so clearly that different builders
would produce the same architecture when implementing them.

A) WHAT vs HOW
- WHAT: meaning, goals, and decisions expressed as ActionRequests
- HOW: execution, translation, and evidence production through adapters/providers
- the separation must be definitional, not stylistic

B) EVIDENCE
- what constitutes evidence
- what does not
- how evidence is represented (ActionResult)
- how evidence is stored and referenced

C) TRUTH CLASSES
- VERIFIED / LAST_KNOWN / UNKNOWN / FAILED
- what each means
- what language each permits
- what language each forbids

D) HOUSESTATE
- what it stores (belief, not raw reality)
- how it decays
- how it handles mixed reality
- what it must never store (large artifacts, transcripts, raw logs)

E) SCOPES
- what a scope is
- containment rules
- scope-bound authority
- why scope is required for meaning and safety

F) MODULES, AGENTS, CONNECTORS
- modules are removable “organs” of capability meaning
- agents are specialized modules with deeper workflows and artifacts
- connectors integrate non-physical domains
- none of these may alter core truth or state law

G) POLICY OUTCOMES
- ACT / CLARIFY / CONFIRM / REFUSE / CHAT
- how each is chosen
- what each implies about execution permission and language posture

If any of these are “hand-wavy,” resurrection becomes interpretation.

Conceptual completion means no interpretation is required.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
3) THE TWO CORE QUESTIONS EVERY BUILDER MUST BE ABLE TO ANSWER
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A) “WHERE DOES TRUTH COME FROM?”
Correct answer shape:
- from ActionResult evidence, evaluated by the Truth Gate,
  optionally cross-checked by readbacks and state observations
- never from conversational fluency
- never from a vendor claim
- never from a bridge’s optimistic acknowledgement

B) “WHERE DOES STATE LIVE?”
Correct answer shape:
- in HouseState, as a scoped belief store
- never in module-internal caches as authority
- never in agent memory as competing truth
- never in bridge registries as canonical identity

If a builder cannot answer these two questions precisely,
the platform is not conceptually complete.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
4) CONCEPTUAL COMPLETION REQUIRES “LAW / MECHANISM / EXAMPLE”
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Every major concept must be represented in three layers:

A) LAW (INVARIANT)
- what must always be true
- what must never be allowed
- what constitutes violation

B) MECHANISM (HOW IT IS ENFORCED)
- architecture boundaries
- schemas
- Truth Gate constraints
- test harness rules
- migration discipline
- logging and observability hooks

C) EXAMPLE (REPRESENTATIVE ONLY)
- a worked example showing correct usage
- clearly framed as illustrative and replaceable
- never treated as authoritative inventory

A platform without law is vague.
A platform without mechanism is aspirational.
A platform without examples is easy to misread.

Conceptual completion requires all three.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
5) “NO SILENT CONVENTIONS” (A REQUIREMENT)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Anything the system depends on must be one of:

- a schema rule
- a boundary rule
- a test
- an explicit written law

If a rule exists only as habit, it will die under pressure.

Examples of silent conventions that must be made explicit:
- “adapters shouldn’t do that”
- “we usually prefer offline”
- “don’t store that in state”
- “don’t mention that as verified”
- “avoid vendor coupling”

Ashby treats silent conventions as incomplete design.
Conceptual completion eliminates them.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
6) MISINTERPRETATION MINIMIZATION (PRINT-EDITION STANDARD)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The Codex must be readable as a printed artifact by someone outside the original team.

That requires:
- explicit definitions
- explicit dependency direction
- explicit lists of forbidden moves
- explicit failure mode descriptions
- explicit boundaries between:
  - invariants vs examples
  - platform shape vs instance inventory
  - permission vs reality
  - evidence vs narrative

This is not stylistic.
It is the difference between “rebuild” and “reinvent.”

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
7) THE “COMPLETION QUESTIONS” CHECKLIST
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Conceptual completion exists only if a reader can answer yes to all of the following.

A) LANGUAGE AND DEFINITIONS
- Do core terms have one meaning everywhere?
- Are synonyms avoided in favor of stable vocabulary?
- Are contradictory phrases eliminated?

B) CONTROL LOOP UNDERSTANDING
- Can a reader describe the full pipeline:
  input → intent → policy → plan → execute → evidence → truth/state → response?
- Can a reader tell where each responsibility lives?

C) FAILURE POSTURE UNDERSTANDING
- Can a reader predict system behavior under:
  - offline conditions
  - stale evidence streams
  - partial execution
  - contradictory readbacks
  - provider outages
- Can a reader predict how the system speaks under each truth class?

D) EXTENSION UNDERSTANDING
- Can a reader add a new module without touching engine law?
- Can a reader add a new provider without touching module meaning?
- Can a reader add a new profile without altering truth/state semantics?

E) CHANGE GOVERNANCE UNDERSTANDING
- Can a reader tell what requires Control Sync?
- Can a reader tell what is forbidden to route through Control Sync?
- Can a reader identify “constitutional vs implementation” changes?

If any answer is no, the platform may still be code-complete,
but it is not conceptually complete.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
8) THE TRUE OUTPUT OF CONCEPTUAL COMPLETION
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The final output is not a document bundle.
It is a *shared invariant mindset* that can be reconstructed from text.

When conceptually complete:
- the system’s meaning survives staff turnover
- the platform can be re-implemented independently and converge
- drift is detectable because the definition is clear
- panic changes are recognized as constitutional violations
- product pressure becomes profiles instead of forks

Conceptual completion is the death of “only the founder knows.”

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Conceptual completion requires:
- explicit definitions of core terms (truth, state, scope, WHAT/HOW)
- consistent language across the Codex
- explicit failure mode descriptions
- explicit mechanisms of enforcement
- representative examples framed as replaceable
- elimination of tribal knowledge dependencies

Conceptual completion forbids:
- relying on “implied meaning”
- permitting multiple definitions of the same primitive
- silent conventions that are not enforceable
- allowing vendor/bridge worldview to become conceptual truth
- allowing “it works” to stand in for “it is understood”

END OF 35.2


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 35.3
FUTURE-PROOF COMPLETION

Future-proof completion is the definition of “done” that survives time.

A system is not complete if it works only at its current size.
Ashby is designed to expand:
- more modules
- more agents
- more vendors and protocols
- more non-physical connectors
- more products and profiles
- more scopes and environments

Future-proof completion is achieved when growth does not require rewriting the soul.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1) DEFINITION: WHAT “FUTURE-PROOF” MEANS IN ASHBY
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Future-proof does not mean:
- predicting every future feature
- pre-building every integration
- writing a perfect architecture that never changes

Future-proof means:
- the platform contains change
- changes happen at permitted seams
- the invariants remain enforceable under pressure
- the platform can be migrated without losing continuity
- the system stays truthful as complexity increases

Future-proof completion is completeness of *constraints and seams*.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2) THE REQUIRED SEAMS (WHERE CHANGE IS ALLOWED TO HAPPEN)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Ashby’s future-proofing depends on stable seams.

A) MODULE SEAM (WHAT EXPANDS)
- adding/removing modules must be routine and non-destructive
- modules remain removable
- modules do not define truth or state

B) ADAPTER / PROVIDER SEAM (HOW EXPANDS)
- new execution paths are introduced as adapters/providers
- providers are replaceable (bridge-backed or native)
- routing policy selects providers; modules do not

C) CONNECTOR SEAM (NON-PHYSICAL DOMAINS)
- business domains expand via connectors, not by contaminating core
- connector credentials remain secrets
- connector outputs remain evidence, not truth

D) PROFILE SEAM (PRODUCTIZATION)
- new products are created by profiles
- not by rewriting engine law
- not by diverging truth posture

E) GOVERNANCE SEAM (CONSTITUTIONAL CHANGE)
- foundational changes route through Control Sync
- non-foundational changes do not pretend to be constitutional
- the Codex remains the authority for what is foundational

A platform without seams becomes a god-system.
A platform with seams remains sane.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
3) THE THREE GROWTH PRESSURES THAT DESTROY SYSTEMS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Future-proof completion requires defenses against the three pressures that
collapse ambitious platforms.

A) VENDOR PRESSURE
- “just let the vendor define state”
- “just treat their API response as truth”
- “just embed their model in our domain”
Defense:
- bridge outputs are evidence
- vendor models are normalized into capabilities
- identity is platform-owned

B) PRODUCT PRESSURE
- “just make this tier behave differently”
- “just add a special case for enterprise”
- “just ship a fork”
Defense:
- profiles over forks
- flags as controlled rollout, not hidden divergence
- entitlements as explicit gates, not behavioral rewrites

C) SPEED PRESSURE
- “just put it here for now”
- “we’ll refactor later”
- “it works”
Defense:
- boundary tests
- acceptance harness
- migration discipline
- Truth Gate law that cannot be bypassed

Future-proof completion exists only when these pressures are structurally resisted,
not merely discouraged.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
4) MIGRATION COMPLETENESS (TIME’S INEVITABLE TAX)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Nothing long-lived avoids migration.

Future-proof completion requires:

A) SCHEMA VERSIONING
- persisted shapes must be versioned
- version changes require explicit migrations

B) IDENTITY PRESERVATION
- device_key continuity must survive:
  - vendor swaps
  - bridge replacement
  - coordinator/fabric changes
  - renames and reorganizations
- identity must not be “recreated” casually

C) ARTIFACT LONGEVITY
- artifacts must remain interpretable
- retention and redaction rules must be explicit
- exports must remain possible without leaking secrets

D) STRUCTURAL REALIGNMENT PLAYBOOKS
- the system must be able to realign state and mappings when architecture evolves
- realignment must be auditable

A future-proof platform assumes migration and is built to survive it.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
5) TEST AND HARNESS COMPLETENESS (DRIFT IS GUARANTEED OTHERWISE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Future-proof completion requires a harness that grows with the platform.

Minimum required harness capabilities:
- boundary enforcement tests (prevent WHAT/HOW collapse)
- Truth Gate posture tests (prevent certainty inflation)
- HouseState tests (decay, mixed reality, restart behavior)
- adapter/provider conformance tests (execution evidence integrity)
- migration tests (version transitions)
- profile/flag/entitlement tests (no hidden forks)

The harness is not “nice.”
It is the mechanical immune system.

A system without an immune system will drift until it is unrecognizable.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
6) OBSERVABILITY COMPLETENESS (FUTURE DEBUGGING)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Future-proof completion requires the platform to explain itself under stress.

Requirements:
- correlation_id everywhere
- provider attribution everywhere
- policy outcomes logged
- evidence logged (sanitized)
- truth class decision logged
- state update decisions logged
- explicit staleness visibility

If the system cannot explain why it acted, future maintainers will guess.
Guessing is how invariants are accidentally rewritten.

Observability prevents rewrite-by-confusion.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
7) OFFLINE COMPLETENESS (DEPENDENCY RESILIENCE)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Future-proof completion includes offline survivability.

Meaning:
- the platform is operable without cloud dependency
- cloud assistance is optional and policy-governed
- offline mode does not permit lying (“unknown” is permitted, fabrication is not)
- entitlement checks and policy must have safe offline posture

Offline capability is not a feature.
It is a resilience contract.

A platform that cannot operate offline is a platform that can be held hostage.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
8) SECURITY COMPLETENESS (THE FUTURE IS ADVERSARIAL)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Future-proof completion requires security posture that survives growth:

- secrets handling is central and strict
- logs and artifacts remain sanitized
- least privilege is pursued where possible
- rotation and revocation are supported
- integrations cannot leak credentials via error surfaces

Security is not separate from resurrection.
A platform that leaks cannot be preserved as text safely.

Future-proof means safe enough to archive.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
9) GOVERNANCE COMPLETENESS (KEEPING THE SOUL LOCKED)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Future-proof completion requires governance that works under panic.

Meaning:
- Control Sync exists, is defined, and is respected
- “panic edits” are recognized as constitutional risk
- the Codex remains the final authority for invariants
- change proposals can be classified:
  - implementation change
  - migration
  - productization (profile/flag/entitlement)
  - constitutional revision (Control Sync)

A future-proof platform is one that knows when it is about to break itself.

Governance is the last defense against slow death.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
10) THE FUTURE-PROOF COMPLETION CHECKLIST
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Future-proof completion exists only if the following are true:

A) ADDING MODULES DOES NOT ALTER CORE LAW
A new module can be added without:
- modifying Truth Gate semantics
- modifying HouseState semantics
- adding vendor dependencies to modules or engine

B) ADDING PROVIDERS DOES NOT ALTER MODULE MEANING
A new provider can be added (native or bridge) without:
- changing the intent language
- changing module planning logic
- changing scope or identity semantics

C) MIGRATION IS EXPECTED AND SAFE
- schema migrations are versioned and rehearsed
- identity mapping survives migrations
- rollback paths exist when possible

D) PRODUCTIZATION DOES NOT FRACTURE THE PLATFORM
- new products can be shipped as profiles
- flags and entitlements are visible and audited
- no hidden tier-specific behavior inside core

E) SYSTEM BEHAVIOR IS AUDITABLE
- support can reconstruct why something happened
- the system can explain failures without inventing reasons

If any of these cannot be proven, the system is not future-proof complete.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ABSOLUTE CONSTRAINTS
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Future-proof completion requires:
- stable seams for extension (modules, adapters/providers, connectors, profiles)
- migration discipline and identity preservation
- a growing test harness that enforces invariants
- observability sufficient for future debugging and audit
- offline survivability and policy-governed cloud optionality
- strict security and secrets handling
- governance capable of resisting panic and product pressure

Future-proof completion forbids:
- expanding by contaminating core meaning with execution details
- allowing bridges/vendors to define identity or truth
- shipping product forks disguised as configuration
- relying on undocumented tribal knowledge
- trading truth posture for perceived smoothness

Ashby is complete only when it can grow without collapse.

END OF 35.3


==============================================================================


ASHBY CODEX — 36.1
THE PROMISE OF ASHBY

Ashby is built on a promise.

It is not a promise of convenience.
It is not a promise of cleverness.
It is not a promise that everything will work.

It is a promise of integrity.

This final statement exists so that a future builder, operator, or reader
can hold the system accountable even when the details of implementation change.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1) THE CENTRAL PROMISE
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Ashby promises:

- to act only within its authority
- to speak only within its evidence
- to remember as a belief system, not as a fantasy
- to preserve uncertainty rather than cover it
- to remain understandable rather than impressive

Ashby’s defining feature is not intelligence.
It is disciplined truth.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2) THE PROMISE OF HONESTY (NO LIES)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Ashby will not claim:
- an action occurred when it did not
- a state is current when it is stale
- a device is reachable when it is unknown
- a decision was made when it is merely inferred

When evidence is weak, Ashby will weaken its language.

When evidence is absent, Ashby will say UNKNOWN.

Honesty is not a tone.
It is an enforced posture.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
3) THE PROMISE OF NON-GUESSING (NO FICTION)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Ashby will not guess targets when ambiguity exists.

When “the kitchen light” could mean multiple things,
Ashby will clarify.

When a request could cause irreversible change,
Ashby will require confirmation.

When a plan depends on missing context,
Ashby will request what is required to proceed.

This is not friction.
It is prevention of silent harm.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
4) THE PROMISE OF OWNERSHIP (TRUTH AND IDENTITY BELONG TO ASHBY)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Ashby will not delegate its definition to vendors.

Bridges may execute.
Vendors may provide data.
Protocol stacks may report states.

But truth classes, identity keys, scope boundaries, and policy outcomes
remain Ashby-owned.

Ashby can use external systems without becoming them.

This is how Ashby survives the decay of any dependency.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
5) THE PROMISE OF MEMORY (HOUSESTATE IS SINGULAR)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Ashby promises a single world memory:
- scoped
- persistent
- decaying honestly
- capable of mixed reality

Ashby will not fragment into competing memories across agents or modules.
If an agent exists, it lives inside the body.

Memory is not a list of facts.
It is a belief model with explicit uncertainty.

This is the only kind of memory that can survive real-world failure.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
6) THE PROMISE OF MODULARITY (REMOVABILITY)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Ashby promises that what grows can also be removed.

Modules and agents are organs, not mutations:
- addable
- upgradable
- removable
without rewriting the body.

This promise prevents Ashby from becoming a god-system.
It is how Ashby expands without collapsing.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
7) THE PROMISE OF EXPLAINABILITY (ASHBY EXPLAINS ITSELF)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Ashby promises that actions will remain reconstructable.

Given an outcome, the platform can show:
- what it believed
- what evidence it received
- what it attempted
- what provider executed
- what succeeded or failed
- why it spoke the way it did

This is not “debug logging.”
It is accountability.

A system that cannot explain itself cannot be trusted.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
8) THE PROMISE OF SAFETY (HUMANS FIRST)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Ashby promises that safety outranks smoothness.

When risk exists:
- Ashby prefers confirmation over irreversible action
- Ashby prefers refusal over unsafe execution
- Ashby prefers clarity over persuasion

This is especially true in domains where:
- money moves
- access is granted
- security changes
- privacy is implicated
- physical harm is possible

A helpful system that harms is not helpful.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
